authors: Linxuan Li, Dechun Yuan, Zhihao Han, Jiali Liu, Chaoyue Zhao, Nian Li
abstract: Magnetically coupled wireless power transfer is highly sensitive to the relative height between the transmitter and receiver coils. Meanwhile, complex electromagnetic interference and measurement noise induce fluctuations in the voltage fingerprints and degrade the fingerprint-library quality, which in turn leads to an unstable closed-loop alignment. To address these issues, this study proposes a position identification and closed-loop alignment method based on decoupled sensing-coil voltage fingerprints and integrated neural network regression. First, a four-quadrant symmetric decoupled sensing-coil array is deployed at the transmitter, and a compensation network is introduced to suppress direct-coupling effects. Thus, the multi-channel sensing voltages are primarily driven by the planar position variation of the receiver coil, thereby forming voltage fingerprints with improved spatial separability. Second, an original evaluation metric for centimeter-level alignment reliability is developed, and a lightweight residual calibration strategy is designed to target hard samples and tail errors to alleviate the error amplification caused by fingerprint noise and distribution imbalance. On this basis, a multi-model fusion integrated neural network is constructed to enhance disturbance rejection and prediction consistency. Finally, closed-loop alignment experiments are conducted over a two-dimensional workspace and compared with representative baseline methods. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves more concentrated terminal alignment positions and more stable closed-loop behavior, even when the fingerprint library is of low quality, thus validating its engineering applicability and robust alignment capability.

title: A UAV Localization Method Based on Multiple Single Chip Radars and Velocity Optimization
authors: Yutao Xiang, Qiang Li, Wei Zhang, Zhikai Zhai, Rui Chen, Huaizong Shao
abstract: With advances in integrated chip technology and decreasing material costs, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly widespread. However, insufficient regulations have led to more unauthorized flights, while the low cost and high power redundancy of UAVs make them attractive for military use. To address these challenges, cost-effective single-chip millimeterwave (mmWave) radars are commonly used for UAV localization. Yet, existing methods mainly focus on single UAVs or radar stations, neglecting mmWave radar’s potential for high-resolution joint tracking. This paper presents a novel multi-radar UAV tracking method that jointly estimates velocity to enhance localization accuracy and target association. The proposed approach extends the detection range of single-chip radars, improves positional precision, and leverages velocity data to avoid false associations. Specifically, radar echoes are processed to extract and correct velocity and position data, followed by refined estimation and prediction for accurate velocity association. Trajectories are then constructed and updated using a Kalman Filter (KF). Comparative experiments verify the proposed method’s accuracy, robustness, and superiority over conventional tracking algorithms across various scenarios.

title: Distributed Online Optimization over Partially Free-In and Free-Out Networks
authors: Yuxuan Liu, Maojiao Ye, Lei Ding, Qing-Long Han
abstract: This paper studies a distributed online optimization problem over partially Free-In and Free-Out (FIFO) networks, in which a set of unfixed agents cooperate to minimize the sum of a group of time-varying functions over a time horizon. To be specific, the agents are divided into static agents and dynamic agents. The static agents are those who remain in the network during the whole time horizon, while the dynamic agents are allowed to join and leave the network freely. Based on the dual averaging technique, two novel distributed algorithms are developed to address the distributed optimization problem in such a dynamic environment. In the case where agents can distinguish whether their out-neighbors are dynamic agents or static agents, a weighting matrix based algorithm is developed. In the case where the identities of out-neighbors are unavailable, a gradient-storage based algorithm is developed, which has higher communication and local storage requirements than the weighting matrix based algorithm. By assuming that each dynamic agent has at least one in-neighbor and one out-neighbor who are static agents, the running average regret is shown to be upper bounded by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathit {T}}})$</tex-math></inline-formula> under suitable step-sizes, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathit {T}$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the time horizon. A simulation study on binary classification is given to verify the effectiveness of the developed algorithms. In addition, a numerical example is used to highlight the advantages of the proposed methods over the distributed sub-gradient decent method.

title: Circuit and Operation of Multi-Pulse Auto-Transformer Rectifier Unit With Reduced Weight and Magnetics Requirement
authors: Himanshu Saini, Sandeep N, Saravana Prakash P
abstract: A symmetric extended zig-zag 48-pulse rectifier unit (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{SEZ}^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>48PRU) is proposed for power quality improvement in aviation applications. The configuration upgrades a conventional 12-pulse zig-zag auto-transformer by integrating extended windings and employing a pulse-doubling circuit (PDC), thereby achieving a 48-pulse supply current. The resulting supply current exhibits a total harmonic distortion of less than 3%, ensuring compliance with DO-160G standards. The PDC diodes conduct a maximum of only 0.71% of the total load current, leading to significantly reduced conduction losses. A comprehensive design methodology for the auto-transformer is developed, including a step-by-step detailed description of its volume and weight computations. Theoretical analysis is supported by both simulation and experimental validation, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed configuration. Comparative evaluations demonstrate its advantages in terms of reduced %kVA rating, enhanced harmonic mitigation, and improved efficiency over existing state-of-the-art rectifier systems.

title: CLIP-ADA: CLIP-guided Artifact-invariant Generalizable Synthetic Image Detection
authors: Jingyi Deng, Chong Zhang, Chenken Xu, Chenhao Lin, Zhengyu Zhao, Shuai Liu, Qian Wang, Chao Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative models necessitates detection methods that generalize to synthetic images containing diverse generator and semantic artifacts. Recent research has leveraged pre-trained vision-language models, such as CLIP, to extract forensic features that distinguish real and fake images, illustrating their promising performance in synthetic image detection. However, a systematic investigation into the embedding space of CLIP to guide its principled utilization for synthetic image detection remains largely unexplored. This paper addresses this gap by first analyzing the multi-stage CLIP image embedding space to uncover its relationship with cross-artifact forensic patterns. Our findings reveal that the mid-level stages primarily encode forensic and generator artifact features, while the high-level stages primarily encode semantic artifact features. Building upon these insights, we propose the CLIP-guided Dual-level Augmentation and Forensic Distribution Adaptation (CLIP-ADA) framework to perform artifact-invariant generalizable detection. Specifically, dual-level augmentation diversifies fake embeddings and suppresses artifact encoding during training to mitigate detectors from excessively relying on artifact features. Moreover, forensic distribution adaptation reformulates synthetic image detection as identifying distributional deviations from the CLIP encoded real embeddings and thereby designing adapters to extract cross-artifact forensic features in a detection scenario-adaptive manner. Extensive evaluations on both the conventional single-generator and continual learning-based multi-generator training settings demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, both suppressing the state-of-the-art methods by over 6% of average accuracy on unseen data from more than 10 generators.

title: RailS: Load Balancing for All-to-All Communication in Distributed Mixture-of-Experts Training
authors: Heng Xu, Zhiwei Yu, Chengze Du, Ying Zhou, Letian Li, Haojie Wang, Weiqiang Cheng, Jialong Li
abstract: Training Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models introduces sparse and highly imbalanced all-to-all communication that dominates iteration time. Conventional load-balancing methods fail to exploit the deterministic topology of Rail architectures, leaving multi-NIC bandwidth underutilized. We present RailS, a distributed load-balancing framework that minimizes all-to-all completion time in MoE training. RailS leverages the Rail topology’s symmetry to prove that uniform sending ensures uniform receiving, transforming global coordination into local scheduling. Each node independently executes a Longest Processing Time First (LPT) spraying scheduler to proactively balance traffic using local information. RailS activates N parallel rails for fine-grained, topology-aware multipath transmission. Across synthetic and real-world MoE workloads, RailS improves bus bandwidth by 20%–78% and reduces completion time by 17%–78%. For Mixtral workloads, it shortens iteration time by 18%–40% and achieves near-optimal load balance, fully exploiting architectural parallelism in distributed training.

title: TID Degradation Mechanisms in Gate-All-Around Silicon Nanowire FETs
authors: Chloe A. Champagne, Dennis R. Ball, James M. Trippe, Romain Ritzenthaler, Jerome Mitard, Dimitri Linten, Lloyd W. Massengill, Steven L. Kosier, Robert A. Reed, En Xia Zhang, Michael L. Alles, Stefano Bonaldo, Daniel M. Fleetwood, Brian D. Sierawski
abstract: The susceptibility of nanowire FETs (NWFETs) to total ionizing dose (TID) is investigated via technology computer-aided design (TCAD) models that are validated via comparison to experimental studies. The simulated contributions of trapped charge in the gate oxide, spacer, and shallow trench isolation (STI) to performance degradation are compared to the measured TID response of NWFETs to identify the dominant source of TID degradation. Results indicate that degradation in both the gate oxide and the spacer are dominant contributors to degradation in p-type NWFETs, while the spacer is the main contributor for n-type NWFETs. An analysis of the impact of spacer length on the TID response of devices indicates that spacers may become less important in gate-all-around (GAA) technology. Results are compared with previous technology nodes, highlighting the reduced role that STI and spacer dielectrics play in NWFET TID degradation.

title: Dynamic Offloading for Deadline-Aware Dependent Tasks in UAV-Empowered Multi-Access Edge Computing
authors: Ji Wang, PeiPei Chen, Miao Zhang, Quanjun Yin, Kaikai Chi, Yong Peng
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-empowered multi-access edge computing (MEC) networks (UEMN) have emerged as a promising solution for delivering flexible, scalable communication and computational services to mobile user equipments (UEs) in dynamic environments. However, existing research has yet to tackle the challenges of managing online-arriving, deadline-sensitive applications with complex task dependencies and unpredictable behavior in UEMN. In this paper, we propose a solution to tackle the problem of dynamic task offloading, aiming to maximize the success rate of applications while considering both deadline constraints and UAV energy consumption. We introduce a two-stage scheduling algorithm, called the Deadline-aware Scheduling scheme with Energy Management (DSEM), to formulate efficient offloading strategies in resource-constrained UAV systems. In the first stage, we prioritize applications and tasks based on the urgency and path-based ranking respectively, decoupling the temporal and data dependencies between tasks and ensuring optimal execution order. In the second stage, we optimize offloading decisions by jointly considering energy consumption and task execution time. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DSEM algorithm significantly improves the success rate of applications compared to existing benchmarks, offering an effective solution for real-time task scheduling in UEMN.

title: Design Optimization of a MG-PMSM based on Torque Balance and Trust Region Boundary Surface
authors: Shiqi Liu, Jiakuan Xia, Yiming Ma, Yaqian Chen, Menglin Song, Lu Sun, Yunqi Zhao, Chunhua Liu
abstract: The magnetic-geared permanent-magnet synchronous machine (MG-PMSM) has attracted substantial attention owing to its contactless speed modulation capability, three-port operation, and potential to replace mechanical gearboxes. Nevertheless, particular issues arise during the optimization of MG-PMSM. The torque balance of the inner rotor between the magnetic gear (MG) and permanent-magnet synchronous machine (PMSM) must be strictly maintained, forming a partially discrete set of combinations that satisfy the torque balance condition. Secondly, the parameter boundaries by torque balance and efficiency constraints invalidate certain sections of the parameter optimization range. The partially discrete parameters, partially invalid parameter ranges, complex structure, and nonlinear parameter coupling of MG-PMSMs pose significant challenges to their optimization. Therefore, this paper proposes an optimized design method for MG-PMSM based on torque balance and trust-region boundary surface. First, an accurate subdomain model (ASM) is established to characterize the magnetic field distribution within the MG-PMSM rigorously. Subsequently, dimensionality reduction conditioned on torque balance is performed. Thereafter, a parametric trust region boundary surface is established with efficiency and torque balance as the constraints. Torque density optimization is performed based on trust-region and combined with the multi-island genetic algorithm (MIGA). Finally, the accuracy of the proposed optimization method is validated through finite element analysis (FEA) and prototype testing.

title: Carbon-Aware Decision-Making for Data Centers in Intraday Electricity Markets Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yuxiong Huang, Difei Sun, Qingqiu Liu, Gengfeng Li, Zhaohong Bie
abstract: With the growing demand for computational services, data centers (DCs) have become major electricity consumers, contributing significantly to carbon emissions and posing new challenges to power system flexibility and sustainability. This study addresses the intraday scheduling of DCs under high-frequency fluctuations caused by renewable integration and load uncertainty. A carbon-aware bi-level optimization framework is proposed: the upper level clears the market based on electricity prices and carbon intensity, while the lower level minimizes the total operating cost, including electricity procurement, carbon emissions, and task migration costs. To overcome the real-time challenges of intraday decision-making, the problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is developed. The agent interacts with the environment to dynamically adjust power procurement and task scheduling. The convergence and training stability of the proposed method are validated through representative learning metrics, including mean reward, explained variance, total loss, and value loss. Simulation results on a modified IEEE 5-bus system show that the approach outperforms traditional day-ahead strategies, reducing costs and emissions while enhancing spatiotemporal coordination. The PPO algorithm also exhibits a better performance than other benchmark DRL methods. Overall, the DRL-based carbon-aware scheduling strategy offers promising potential for advancing low-carbon and resilient power systems.

title: A Physics-Constrained Graph Generative Adversarial Network for Augmenting Scarce Voltage Violation Scenarios in Distribution Networks
authors: Siying Chen, Qi Cao, Wenzheng Li, Haipeng Xie
abstract: The proliferation of distributed energy resources is creating significant voltage stability challenges for power distribution networks. However, the scarcity of real-world data on rare but critical voltage violation events hampers the development of robust AI-based risk assessment models. To address this data scarcity and imbalance, this paper proposes a novel Physics-Constrained Graph Generative Adversarial Network (PCG-GAN). The framework introduces a topology-aware Wasserstein critic that leverages Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) to learn the grid's structural dependencies. This is combined with a physics-informed loss function that enforces compliance with power flow equations. The generator architecture utilizes a conditional, perturbation-based mechanism to ensure stable training and targeted synthesis of specific voltage violation types. Validated on the IEEE 33-bus test system, the proposed PCG-GAN successfully generates high-fidelity, physically valid, and controllable risk scenarios, demonstrating its potential to enhance data-driven grid monitoring and security analysis.

title: A Multi-Time-Scale Regulation Potential Assessment Method for Virtual Power Plants Based on Physical-Information Dual-Driving
authors: Hongjuan Zheng, Weiguo Zhang, Xinyi Wang, Jize Sun
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of evaluating the regulation potential of flexible adjustable load resources within new power systems. A physics-information dual-driven methodology for multi-time-scale assessment is proposed. At the physical level, mechanistic models for typical flexible loads (e.g., air conditioners, electric vehicles) are established to quantify their regulation capacity and response characteristics. At the information level, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network is developed to integrate load clustering features and multi-scale influencing factors. Furthermore, a hybrid TCN-LSTM neural network is employed to extract both local high-frequency patterns and long-term dependencies within load sequences, thereby enhancing prediction accuracy and robustness. Case studies based on operational data from a regional virtual power plant demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance in short-, medium-, and long-term load forecasting. This work provides an effective core assessment tool for the dynamic aggregation and flexible dispatch of virtual power plants.

title: Real-Time Corn Leaf Disease Classification Using MobileNetV2 and Streamlit
authors: Srinivasan A, Jayasri N, Krishnakumari R
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Early and accurate detection of the disease in crops is important to ensure a healthy yield and improve farming efficiency. This paper proposes an end-to-end corn leaf disease classification system, which utilizes an AI-based MobileNetV2 architecture, integrated with a Streamlit web application. The proposed system has achieved 98.24% classification accuracy while remaining computationally efficient at fewer than 3.4M parameters and an average time of 92 ms for inference. These value-added features constitute the major contributions of this work: it realizes confidence-based unknown image detection with 91.2% accuracy, voice feedback with integration of gTTS, and fully optimized mobile-ready deployment in the field. The system has shown very strong practical applicability compared to previous approaches through large-scale evaluation on 1,049 test images and field trials involving 150 farmers. The proposed system fills crucial gaps present in current techniques, such as the absence of mobile deployment, an inability to deal with unknown images, and limited real-world verification.

title: Distributionally Robust Resilient Operation of Power System with Advanced Adiabatic Compressed Air Energy Storage
authors: Tianyu Li, Laijun Chen, Hanchen Liu, Sen Cui
abstract: With the increasing integration of power sources featuring stochastic output characteristics into the power grid, the need for reliable mitigation strategies against the damaging impacts of extreme events on the power system warrants significant attention. As a high-capacity and flexible form of energy storage, Advanced adiabatic compressed air energy storage (AA-CAES) is capable of providing substantial support for the secure and stable operation of the grid. This paper proposes a two-stage distributionally robust resilient operation method of power system with AA-CAES. In the day-ahead stage, the unit commitment schedule for both AA-CAES and thermal power units (TPUs) under the worst-case probability distribution is determined by optimizing the master problem.Firstly, a dispatch-friendly model of AA-CAES is proposed, which reflects the operation charactersitics of the system intuitively.Subsequently, a resilient operation model of power system with AA-CAES is presented. In the intra-day stage, adjustments are made to the power of AA-CAES and TPUs to minimize system load shedding under contingencies involving line failures. Numerical studies are conducted on the IEEE 39-bus system with integrated AA-CAES. The results demonstrate that, under the worst-case scenarios, a given capacity of AA-CAES can reduce the system’s load shedding by up to 2.08% through flexible operations.

title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Automated Domain Model Construction
authors: Tatjana D. Stojanović, Kristina S. Jovanović, Saša D. Lazarević
abstract: Domain models are fundamental artifact in software engineering. Errors made during their construction can be difficult to correct in later development stages. Domain models are derived from software requirements, which are most often expressed as unstructured textual descriptions. Domain model construction is still usually done manually, which makes their quality depend from knowledge and experience of the modeler. While rule-based system for automation of domain model construction have shown their ability to extract concept, their usability is limited due to lack of contextual understanding and semantic ambiguity. Large language models provide advanced contextual understanding and reasoning, but lack consistency, determinism and formal constraints. Hence, here is presented approach for construction of domain model by combining LLMs for their contextual understanding with the stability of heuristics. This paper introduces TEXTER, a framework that enables automated construction of formal, executable domain models from informal textual requirements.

title: Construction and Application of Post evaluation System for Power Grid Projects Assisted by Artificial Intelligence
authors: Yingna Jiang, Xiaowei Bi, Peifen Cheng
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: This article proposes an artificial intelligence-assisted post-evaluation system for distribution network engineering projects. The system is designed to provide full process intelligence for project deviation identification, causal chain inference and post-evaluation report generation, and consists of a model layer, an inference layer and a generation layer. The model layer uses the SHAP method to analyze the key driving factors in an interpretable way. The inference layer is based on an engineering causal knowledge graph and the semantic reasoning ability of large models. It automatically generates an 'event process result' causal chain and ensures the reliability of the logical chain through cross-layer consistency verification. The generation layer uses CoT inference and template generation capabilities of large models to automate the generation of post-evaluation texts and ensures the quality of the results through expert feedback. Verification results demonstrate that the constructed system performs well in terms of prediction accuracy, causal interpretation, logical reasoning and automatic writing, improving evaluation efficiency by 70-75% and significantly enhancing standardization and repeatability of evaluation results.

title: Design and Optimization of Preamplifier based Comparator using 180nm Technology
authors: Rohit Kumar, Shikha Bathla, Pradeep Kumar, Nidhi Gaur
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: This work presents a preamplifier-based high-speed comparator design specifically developed for analog-to-digital conversion systems. The proposed architecture demonstrates significant performance enhancements, particularly in switching speed and offset cancelation, when compared to traditional preamplifier comparator implementations. The design is implemented in a 180nm CMOS technology through Cadence Virtuoso, the circuit operates with a 1.8V power supply. The simulation results show substantial performance gains, with slew rates of 8.902 kV/µs (positive) and 7.509 kV/µs (negative), along with minimized signal transition times. The design achieves a propagation delay of 33.9µs while maintaining an ultra-low power dissipation of 0.525µW.

title: Blockchain and AI-Powered Tourist Safety System for Real-Time Risk Management
authors: N.Mirudhula Devi, Avighnaa Thirumaran, M.Nikil, Architha Rajeswaran, T.Prasanthi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Tourism serves as a cornerstone of cultural exchange and economic growth. However, persistent safety challenges and unreliable information continue to undermine traveler confidence, particularly in developing regions such as India. TrustTrip introduces an integrated AI–Blockchain framework for real-time tourist safety management, combining data transparency, security and intelligent assistance. The blockchain layer, secured with AES encryption and JWS tokens, ensures tamper-proof data integrity and trustworthy communication between users and authorities. Complementing this, an AI-driven chatbot delivers contextual safety insights, personalized recommendations and emergency support. A multimodal alert module dispatches verified notifications to first responders and emergency contacts through SMS and push channels, embedding geolocation and severity metadata for timely intervention. In parallel, a digital twin–based visualization layer generates dynamic safety heatmaps and crowd-density overlays, empowering authorities with proactive situational awareness. By fusing blockchain transparency with AI adaptability, TrustTrip establishes a secure, intelligent and scalable paradigm for enhancing tourist safety, trust, and governance in emerging smart destinations.

title: Adaptive Control of Water Sprinkler Systems for Sustainable Agriculture
authors: Muzaffar Naveed, A. Vijay Kumar, Alivelu M Parimi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Efficient water management is an important factor for sustainable agriculture, especially in the context of climate variability and water scarcity. This paper presents the development of an adaptive control system for the optimal operation of a water sprinkler by dynamically regulating a DC motor voltage. The research combines closed-loop controlling according to real-time environment conditions and predicting weather data to actually control the water sprinklers and a virtual sensing mechanism to measure the performance metrics, incorporating real-time inputs from Kaggle, e.g., rainfall and temperature. The proposed Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System with Rainfall Prediction and Crop-based Adjustment System (ARPCAS) improves the precision of the control system, which is much more superior than the conventional fuzzy logic, where it is constantly learning and adapting to the change in the environment. The simulation studies are validated with the hardware implementation, with the results showing high improvements in irrigation efficiency, reduced electricity consumption, and improved soil moisture maintenance, which are critical to overall higher yield crops and sustainable agricultural practices.

title: Capacity Planning of Constant- and Variable-Speed Pumped Storage Under Renewable Resource Droughts
authors: Puheng Wang, Jinlong Xv, Yuxing Tong, Xiuting Rong, Chao Huo
abstract: Renewable resource droughts (RRDs) , refer to a prolonged period of significantly reduced renewable energy generation, pose challenges to power system reliability and stability. In this study, a capacity planning method for constant-speed and variable-speed pumped storage (PS) units is proposed, considering the effects of RRDs. First, a short-term renewable energy (RE) model is developed based on the theory of absorption region. Second, a long-term RE model is formulated within a multi-scenario framework, in which typical scenarios are generated through clustering of historical data, and RRD scenarios are identified according to predefined criteria. Third, a PS capacity planning model is established by modeling constant- and variable-speed PS units separately, while incorporating thermal unit constraints and the system constraints. Finally, case studies on the modified lEEE RTS-79 system illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed planning method.

title: Leveraging Outcome Metrics to Design Educational Hackathon: Setups for Sustainable Student Projects
authors: Ana B. Miličević, Zorica M. Bogdanović, Tatjana D. Stojanović
abstract: The paper develops a conceptual metric framework for the design of sustainable educational hackathons in ICT higher education, grounded in a case study of a Web3 hackathon at the Faculty of Organizational Sciences(FON). The framework integrates a set of students, faculty, and institution-level indices that quantify knowledge, skills, motivation, professional development, and post-hackathon support. The methodology includes secondary analysis of combined quantitative and qualitative data from three waves of surveys and interviews with participants, mentors and organisers. The results show growth in knowledge and stable motivation, but low project continuity and a limited number of support structures, indicating a gap between the pedagogical potential and the existing ecosystem. The proposed set of metrics provides a basis for institutional self-evaluation and for redesigning hackathons towards greater sustainability of results. Based on this, the paper provides recommendations for integrating “sustainability” into the design and planning of educational hackathons by defining a basic set of metrics for institutional self-evaluation and continuous improvement.

title: SignFlow: Multilingual ISL Paragraph Translator with Virtual Assistant
authors: Madhuri Sahu, Prathamesh Girhale, Ujjwal Kapate, Utkarsh Waghmare, Vikram Gujar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computation-Based Sensor Application (ICAIQSA)
abstract: Effective communication between the hearing-impaired community and the wider population is often hindered by limited understanding of Indian Sign Language (ISL).Current systems are usually only able to recognize single words or sentences; they are unable to process continuous gestures and produce outputs that make sense. This research presents SignFlow, a real-time multilingual ISL paragraph translator with an integrated virtual assistant. The system recognizes sequential ISL gestures from webcam input, creates coherent English paragraphs, and translates them into Hindi and Marathi using computer vision techniques (MediaPipe Hand Landmarks, Dynamic Time Warping, LSTM) and natural language processing. To ensure accessibility across various modalities, outputs are offered in speech, text, and captions. Sign-to-sign Q&A is further made possible by an interactive assistant, which improves usability and inclusivity. testing demonstrates >87% gesture recognition accuracy with low-latency performance, validating the feasibility of deploying such systems in real-world communication, education, and healthcare contexts. Future work aims to expand vocabulary, integrate larger ISL datasets, and support additional regional languages for broader accessibility.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Based Visual System for ABO and Rh Blood Group Prediction
authors: Manjuladevi C, Swathi M, Rohini S, Gobinath A, Padma Priya S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: In medicine, a prompt and precise identification of a patient's blood group is critical in transfusions, surgery, and emergencies but is time-consuming and prone to human error with traditional methods such as slide and tube agglutination. This project solves these shortcomings by proposing an automated blood group detection system using deep learning. A synthetic data of mimicking realistic agglutination card patterns for the main blood groups was generated and augmented with data augmentation measures for improving resistance to change in orientation, brightness, and noise. A transfer learning model with a pre-trained MobileNetV2 architecture was modified to identify ABO and Rh groups, and the model was trained and validated using performance measured with confusion matrices, classification reports, and accuracy–loss curves. It was stored in a compact form for convenient deployment on light diagnostic equipment. This two-pronged strategy is an economical, scalable, and reliable solution to automated blood typing identification, which is best suited to rural health clinics and mobile health units. It was trained on a synthetically generated dataset and the model will later be validated on real clinical images.

title: SDN-Enabled Intelligent Irrigation Framework with IoT Sensor Integration for Smart Agriculture
authors: Dineshkumar B, Surender S, Esther Grace S, Akalya S, Ajith, Sowmiya S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Agriculture accounts for a large percentage of freshwater usage world wide, and inefficient irrigation systems tend to lead to over-irrigation and waste of water. Conventional irrigation techniques are inflexible and rigid, making them less effective in meeting variable soil and crop conditions. These limitations can be addressed through this paper's vision to implement an SDN-based intelligent irrigation system coupled with IoT sensors for smart farming. The framework utilizes temperature, pH, soil moisture, and nutrient sensors to keep track of the health of crops and the environment in real time. A centralized controller of SDN is used to control IoT devices dynamically, optimize routing, and provide effective water distribution. The proposed system is realized by utilizing a Mininet-based software platform with Python-driven modules for sensors in order to simulate actual deployment. Experimental analysis proves that the combination of SDN with IoT greatly improves the system performance, with a 10% improvement increase in irrigation efficiency, and a 15% reduction in sensor node energy expenditure as compared to the traditional IoT-based irrigation systems. In addition, the centralized decision-making ability of the controller reduced network congestion and facilitated adaptive scheduling, resulting in efficient water usage. In general, this study confirms that the blending of SDN and IoT provides a new and scalable solution to smart irrigation, resolving essential challenges in agricultural water management while creating opportunities for efficient farming practices.

title: Design and Kinematics of an Active Suspension Rover for Extreme Terrains
authors: Sarah M. Lam, Heaven De Leon, David Dugas, Daniyal Ansari, Robert O. Ambrose
abstract: Amid renewed efforts to establish a sustained human presence on the lunar surface, the development of advanced mobility systems capable of traversing extreme terrain has become increasingly critical. To address these needs, NASA has developed several mobility platforms, such as the Space Exploration Vehicle (SEV) and the Lunar Terrain Vehicle Ground Test Unit (LTV GTU), which combine swerve-drive kinematics with active suspension. These large-scale vehicles enable NASA to develop concepts of operations for future missions; however, their size and cost make extensive testing and performance evaluations difficult. To overcome these limitations, the Robotics and Automation Design (RAD) Lab has developed the RAD Exploration Vehicle (REV), a small-scale kinematics and controls testbed designed to model the kinematic degrees of freedom of these similar rovers. REV facilitates the investigation of new control modes and locomotion strategies for active suspension platforms. This paper presents the design and implementation of REV, emphasizing key features that enhance mobility and functionality. In addition, an adapted swerve-drive kinematic model for REV is described, followed by the results of ground-based kinematics validation tests.

title: EduHub: An AI-powered community-based system to enhance the quality of rural education in India
authors: Abdul Subhan Omeez, Shobha Rathod, Sri Sai Venkata Gayathri Dhara, Amirtha Preeya V, Chanti Vasavi, Falisha Umaiza
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Education in rural India still suffers from the challenges of lack of infrastructure, lack of trained teachers, digital availability challenges, and social and economic barriers to enhance urban-rural learning divisions. This paper introduces Edu Hub, a community-based, AI-powered system for improving the standard of education in rural India. EduHub has a personalized learning platform, content that can be accessed offline, and community learning spaces to enable equitable access and inclusion. Our framework also focuses on building teacher capacity, localization of the curriculum, and working in partnership with parents, making the system locally relevant and sustainable. Utilizing artificial intelligence to enable adaptive learning, EduHub will narrow the digital divide while advancing educational equity and empowerment for the long term. The system itself has been designed to be portable, enabling it to be easily adapted to multiple rural educational contexts throughout India and ultimately help develop futures and inclusive learning ecosystems across the globe.

title: QoS-Aware Adaptive Flow Rule Placement in Software Defined Internet of Vehicles
authors: Sowmya P, Dinesha P, Anitha Saravana Kumar, Akilandeswari J
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Conventional networks face several challenges with regard to network agility and programmability due the integrated control and data planes. In contrast to this, in Software Defined Networks (SDN), the control plane and the data plane are decoupled, facilitating a control mechanism, which is centralized and programmable. However, by default in SDN, flow rule installation strategy is reactive, which imposes latency inefficiency and resource under utilization. This paper proposes a quality-of-service (QoS) aware adaptive flow rule placement scheme in software-defined Internet of Vehicles (SDIoV) network, integrating proactive and reactive flow rule placements to enhance network performance. In addition to the reactive strategy that account for any unclassified flows dynamically, the proposed work proactively installs the necessary flow rules in the access point (AP) for classified flows to suffice QoS. First, the flows are categorized as delay-sensitive, loss-sensitive, and best-effort flows, using the values of Differentiated Services Code Point (DSCP) of incoming packets. The classified flows are then forwarded to their corresponding queues for QoS handling. This approach enhances efficiency while simultaneously reducing latency for delay-sensitive flows and packet drop rate for loss-sensitive flows. The proposed work is completely self-contained at the network layer. Consequently, we have implemented the SDIoV architecture using the OpenFlow protocol with Ryu controller and Mininet for topology simulation.

title: Color Space Fusion for Semantic Segmentation in Indian Adverse Weather Driving Scenes
authors: Veer Chheda, Vansh Shah, Hezal Lopes, Vishakha Shelke
publish: 2026 6th Biennial International Conference on Nascent Technologies in Engineering (ICNTE)
abstract: Performing semantic segmentation in bad weather conditions is still extremely difficult because RGB sensors often have poor visibility in such conditions. In this study, we have explored various colour spaces to combine RGB and NearInfrared images to improve the perception of environment in bad weather conditions. We assessed the YUV, LAB and HSV colour spaces to combine RGB and NIR images along with an RGB baseline for comparison. The IDD-AW dataset was used for this fusion across the YOLOv8 and YOLOv11 nano and small segmentation models. According to our experimental results, LAB fusion along with YOLOv11 yields the best results which had a SmIoU of 17.96 and a mIoU of 26.46. These results are a 10% improvement over the RGB baseline. Since YUV and LAB color spaces have separate luminance channels, they have higher scores when compared to HSV, which shows moderate improvements. This tells us that choosing the right color for space for the RGB and NIR fusion has a huge positive impact on semantic segmentation in conditions with fog, low light, rain and snow even for smaller models focusing on real-time inference.

title: Impact Analysis of Solar PV Penetration in Distribution Network on Power Loss and Reliability
authors: Seema Jadhav, S Bindu
publish: 2026 6th Biennial International Conference on Nascent Technologies in Engineering (ICNTE)
abstract: Increasing energy demand and recent advancements in distributed generation (DG) technology has encouraged integration of DG in power networks. As the power generation from renewable energy based DG is fluctuating in nature, evaluation of power loss and reliability is crucial for steady and reliable operation of networks. For the increased use of DG, economic impact analysis is important for the utility. This paper aims to analyze the impact of penetration of DG on power network by estimating power loss, voltage profile, reliability and cost. A comparative study of simulation results in MATLAB and ETAP software, with and without DG is presented for different DG penetration level. The optimal DG size and location is obtained for loss minimization in IEEE 5 bus system with DG installation. Reduced power loss, improved reliability indices, and cost savings are the results of DG integration. However, the location and capacity of solar PV integration significantly affects the system power losses and reliability.

title: Study on Safety Boundaries of Sodium-Ion Batteries Under Typical Electrical Abuse Conditions
authors: Xuehai Li, Xiaochao Wu, Ran Yu, Baiquan Yu, Junhua Ma, Bin Wei
abstract: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have emerged as a critical technology for large-scale energy storage, due to their inherent advantages of abundant sodium reserves, cost-effectiveness, and superior thermal stability. However, the current research landscape lacks a systematic understanding of the key safety parameters of diverse SIB systems under complex operating conditions. Furthermore, the intrinsic thermal runaway mechanisms of in these SIB systems remain insufficiently elucidated. These knowledge gaps have restricted the precise development of safety protection technologies. This study investigates 3 Ah SIBs with layered oxide and polyanion-type compound cathodes under overcharge, overdischarge, and adiabatic overcharge conditions. By analyzing the characteristics of the initial stage of thermal runaway, we innovatively employ the peak position of the second derivative of temperature to determine the safety boundary. Compared to the traditional method using T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> temperature as the thermal runaway onset point, this approach not only extends the safety warning time by >900 seconds but also effectively addresses the limitation of traditional methods failing to provide warnings when thermal runaway does not reach T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>. Consequently, it offers a new theoretical basis for the safe operation and management strategies of SIBs.

title: Data-Driven Industrial Load Flexibility Classification and Dynamic Pricing Method Designation
authors: Zijie Ren, Bowen Zhou, Zhaoyuan Wang
abstract: To address the insufficient exploitation of demand response (DR) potential in industrial loads, this paper proposes a data-driven flexibility classification and personalized dynamic pricing method. First, utilizing readily available intraday hourly electricity consumption data from several industrial users, the K-means clustering algorithm extracts industry load patterns. Feature analysis incorporating peak-valley difference, load rate, and a defined flexibility score enables load flexibility classification. Second, based on the classification results, a differentiated dynamic pricing model is designed, superimposing incentive coefficients closely linked to load flexibility characteristics and system requirements onto the base time-of-use (TOU) tariff. Finally, case studies involving two typical industrial enterprises demonstrate that the proposed method effectively identifies industry load characteristics and designs personalized dynamic pricing strategies. It achieves approximately 2% electricity cost savings for users while effectively guiding load peak shaving and valley filling operation. This method enhances the precision of demand response and the participation rate of industrial users.

title: Cuckoo Search–Tuned ML models for Crop Prediction
authors: Amol Bhilare, Om Deshmukh, Yash Ganatra, Debabrata Swain, Manish Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computation-Based Sensor Application (ICAIQSA)
abstract: The agriculture industry is one of the most contributing sectors to a country’s economy. With changing climates and a rapid increase in population, there is a global need for accurate crop prediction to assist farmers and agricultural professionals in crop cultivation. The improper selection of crop is a great problem for a farmer that affects the productivity volume of the crop to larger extent. This study focuses on crop prediction using environmental conditions like rainfall, humidity, and temperature, along with macronutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, which are critical for cultivation. In the presented study three models were used: Random Forest, Support Vector Machine and LightGBM. Cuckoo search is used for hyper-parameter tuning individual models and stacking of these three models is also performed. Random Forest, Stacked model and LightGBM with the Cuckoo Search Algorithm achieved the best accuracy of 99%.

title: A Dissipation Energy Flow Method for Locating Forced Oscillation Sources Using FIF and MST
authors: Tao Jiang, Nan Ye, Xue Li, Bin Wang, Bohan Liu
abstract: Accurate location of forced oscillation (FO) sources is crucial for the safe and stable operation of power systems. Conventional methods struggle with effective oscillation component extraction from multi-channel measurements due to low-frequency trends, noise, and the time-varying characteristics of FO, which reduces location accuracy based on dissipation energy flow (DEF). To address these issues, a forced oscillation source location (FOSL) method based on fast iterative filtering (FIF) and multivariate synchrosqueezing transform (MST) is proposed. First, FIF is used to adaptively extract dynamic components from measurement data. Then, MST captures inter-channel correlations, allowing for parallel extraction of FO components across multiple channels and constructing a time-frequency coefficient matrix representing each bus’s FO components. Subsequently, a time-frequency DEF model is further derived based on the MST, identifying the FO source through energy characteristics. Finally, validation on the WECC 240-bus system shows that the proposed method can accurately and effectively locate forced oscillation sources.

title: Toward a UAF-Based Multi-View Territorial Architecture: An MBSoSE Approach to Model Critical Infrastructures
authors: Jean-Samuel Wienin, Jérôme Tixier, Souad Rabah
publish: 2026 International Workshop on Systems Engineering Research
abstract: This work treats the territory as a socio-technical System-of-Systems and proposes a Model-Based Systel of System Engineering (MBSoSE) modeling approach based on the Unified Architecture Framework (UAF) to prepare for and manage risks affecting critical infrastructures. It supports the definition of a minimal yet extensible UAF-territory profile that makes public missions and values explicit, allocates capabilities to essential services, and renders interdependencies between sectors such as energy, water, communications, and health queryable. An operational approach, structured into strategic, operational, functional, logical, and then physical resources views, ensures end-to-end traceability, anchors resilience in orchestration policies and measurable thresholds, and integrates parameters and measurements for what-if analyses. Current limitations lie in the absence of a complete case study and the variability of practices and datasets; an experiment on a reference textbook case territory with baseline scenarios and shared acceptance criteria is planned. In the medium term, the objective is to provide interoperable, simulable models to compare mitigation options, prioritize investments, and objectify decision support in land-use planning, preparedness, and crisis response.

title: Research on Multi-level Response Optimization and Control Technology of Virtual Power Plants Based on Spot Trading
authors: Yingying Sun, Xiaoliang Wu, Wei Su, Ningji Fang, Wenjing Yang
abstract: With the improvement of domestic electricity market mechanism and the refinement of commercial operation mechanism of virtual power plants, designing a set of refined and multi-scenario resource cluster optimization and control strategies, which cater for the spot trading market environment, has become a key challenge for virtual power plants. By combining CNN algorithm with LightGBM algorithm, this paper proposes an electricity price and load forecasting model and algorithm for virtual power plant systems under the power spot trading mode. While considering the load regulation response of virtual power plants, this paper brings forth the objective function and constraint conditions for the optimal operating cost, and a multi-level response optimization and control algorithm for virtual power plants is established. Taking a certain province’s market transaction disclosure margin as an example for case analysis, this paper verifies the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed electricity price forecasting model and multi-level response optimization and control algorithm.

title: PLANTSHIELD AI: An Integrated Deep Learning And Predictive Analytics Framework For Smart Pest Management And Data-Driven Rice Crop Disease Optimization
authors: P Pavithra, J Nithyasri, E. Srividhya, S Jayanthi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: PlantShield AI represents a novel solution within the farming industry as it proposes an answer to a highly important issue plant pathology outbreaks and kills rice crops with pests and diseases through the combination of deep learning and predictive analytics. Accurately identifying and classifying rice diseases using crop images through Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and predicting outbreaks of pests using time-series forecasting models using the historical and environmental data are the tasks of this system. It also helps to reduce the losses of yield and avoid unnecessary overuse of pesticides through early detection and accurate interventions. PlantShield AI is a developed system that was trained in MATLAB with the Image Processing Toolbox and Deep Learning Toolbox on a huge set of labeled healthy and diseased rice photos, which means high classification rates. The structure facilitates real time tracking and decision making which presents an effective scalable solution using data-driven technology that helps in achieving sustainability and growth of rice farming. This paper has shown that AI-based diagnostics and predictive analytics could be used to defend crops and manage resources better in the field of agriculture today.

title: Mouse Movement Detection and Data Loss Analysis using Machine Learning
authors: A. Tamilarasi, M. Jagadeesan, Nisanth G, Vashanthakumar Ks, Selva J, Sivasubramanian S
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: The analysis of mouse movement patterns has gained importance in HCI research, user behavior modeling, and the design of responsive interactive systems. However, the integrity of the tracked data is frequently compromised by intermittent data losses that are often the result of hardware constraints, environmental interference, or software latency. This study explores the use of machine learning algorithms to identify and recover from mouse movement data loss. Real-time mouse trajectories were collected for the purpose of training machine learning models for example, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Support Vector Machines, to classify between normal and anomalous movement patterns. Inspired by recovery strategies from self-healing reactive systems, we will introduce a recovery analysis that predicts the incomplete mouse path and continues the interaction. From the results of this study we demonstrate that the framework for identifying data anomalies and recovering the lost segment of the interaction is possible. This provides a means of developing a more robust framework for user interaction and engagement with interactive systems. The approach proposed enables the development of intelligent interfaces that support adaptive and intelligent processing to help manage incomplete or noisy mouse movements for improved usability and reliability.

title: Climate Risk Index for Extreme Load Event Prediction in Power Systems
authors: Yuanyuan Fu, Haowen Gao
abstract: Accurate prediction of extreme load events is crucial for power system reliability and steady operation, while extreme weather conditions are deeply related to the severe load occurrence due to its influence to the electricity demand. However, existing forecasting models often fail to adequately capture the complex climatic influences on load consumption, leading to their inability to anticipate the occurrence of extreme load events. This paper proposes a novel Climate Risk Index (CRI) that quantifies the compound impact of multiple meteorological hazards on electricity consumption. The CRI integrates four weather indices: thermal index, rainstorm severity index, wind chill index, and continuous heat index, using a data-driven linear regression approach to determine optimal combination weights. Moreover, comparative analysis using Light Gradient Boosting Machine model shows that incorporating CRI as an additional feature will significantly enhance the prediction performance, improving the accuracy, precision, and F1-score notably, demonstrating the effectiveness of CRI as a meaningful and practical predictor for extreme load event forecasting, providing valuable insights for power system planning and risk management.

title: Designing Cognitive AI-Enhanced Machine Learning Architectures for Adaptive and Intelligent Network Optimization
authors: Guru Kesava Dasu Gopisetty, Balajee. Maram, Manikonda Srinivasa Sesha Sai
publish: 2026 6th Biennial International Conference on Nascent Technologies in Engineering (ICNTE)
abstract: The blistering development of next generation communication systems necessitates network infrastructures which are not only to scale, but are also self-optimizing and adaptive. The proposed framework: Cognitive AI-Enhanced Machine Learning Architectures combines deep reinforcement learning, meta-learning and cognitive AI agents within dynamic network environments. In comparison to standard optimization-based systems, the given architecture can be based on intelligence prediction and constant feedback loop in order to optimize routing, resource distribution, and traffic management in volatile conditions automatically. A detailed literature review identifies the existing gaps over adaptive optimization and caveats of mere-algorithmic or static models. The methodology will include cognitive agent-based model design, real-time system integration and adaptive learning pipelines tested through simulation over $5 G$ / $6 G$ network scenarios. Results show the throughput efficiency increases by 27-34 marketing, the latency variance decreases by 19 marketing, and the energy utilization increases up to an amount of 40 market. The results highlight the future of cognitive AI-driven networks as a foundation of resilient, intelligent and future-ready network optimization approaches.

title: Comparison of Optimizers for LSTM Model to predict State of Charge Estimation of Electric Vehicle Batteries
authors: Sahukari Bhavana, Vellure Rahul, Venkadesan Arunachalam, Sadheesh Kumar S J, Ram Jethmalani Hemaparuva
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: The State of Charge (SoC) is the information about the energy remaining in a battery with respect to its full capacity. The SoC plays a vital role in maintaining safety, enhancing performance, and estimating the driving range of Electric Vehicles (EVs). The precise SoC assessment is therefore crucial for a proper battery management system. Traditional estimation methods, such as Open Circuit Voltage measurement, Coulomb counting, and Kalman filtering, often suffer from reduced reliability when subjected to dynamic operating conditions. To address these challenges, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks are adopted for their ability to capture long-term temporal patterns in battery data. Nevertheless, the performance of deep learningbased SoC estimation models is strongly dependent on the choice of optimization technique used during training. This research examines the influence of four optimization algorithms—Adam, Adagrad, RMSProp, and AdamW—on the performance of an LSTM-based SoC estimation framework. A comparative evaluation is carried out to assess their effectiveness in terms of estimation accuracy, and the corresponding results are presented and discussed.

title: Machine Learning-based Plant Disease Severity and Progression Prediction System
authors: Achal D. Bangade, Chetan Puri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: The Agriculture has remained under the menace of plant diseases, which are a significant pillar of the global food security, causing immense losses in revenues and wastage of produce more so in key crops like potatoes. Manual methods of detection are slow, subjective and laborious, and are linked with delay that which encourages the spread of diseases in resource limited settings. To address these problems in this paper, a Machine Learning-Based Plant Disease Severity and Progression Prediction System has been advanced by automated image processing, severity, and prediction of progression. Using a hybrid structure containing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to detect and localize diseases, quantify their severity with U-Net segmentation, and predict its evolution with a 1.2-day MAE with a pool of 400 potato leaf images of healthy, Such ends can identify and localize 94.5 percent of the diseases, estimate the area of the affected area with 0.86 IoU, and forecast how the disease will evolve with a 1.2-day MAE. It compares this to the current detection-only systems demonstrating 9-11 percent accuracy improvements and new progression models that can be utilized to implement proactive interventions that would significantly reduce by 25-30 percent the quantity of fungicides utilized. The implementation of the system, which relies on the React.js frontend and Flask backend, is an easy-to-use solution to sustainable agricultural practice, and scalable precision farming can be suggested.

title: Emergency Network Reconfiguration and VAR Control for ADNs Based on Topology-Adaptive Dimension-Augmented Power Flow
authors: Guochao Lu, Zhongguan Wang, Li Guo, Xialin Li, Yixin Liu, Chengshan Wang
abstract: High penetration of distributed generation (DG) significantly strengthens the coupling between power supply and weather conditions in active distribution networks (ADNs). Specifically, the power flow (PF) diversion and reconfigured topology under extreme weather can exacerbate the risk to voltage security of ADNs. Therefore, it is necessary to achieve emergency topology-VAR coordinated control for ADNs with high-penetration DG. However, given the inaccurate feeder parameters, the physics-based methods encounter challenges, whereas the data-driven PF model lack adaptability in varying topologies. In response, this paper proposes a topology-adaptive dimension-augmented PF (TA-DAPF) method, which can establish a meta model for PF to achieve integrated optimization of topology configuration and reactive power. The topology and DG are co-optimized in a rolling manner based on predictions of DG and load power. Finally, the proposed method is validated on IEEE 33-bus and IEEE 69-bus systems with high-penetration DG.

title: A Flexible Distribution Network Planning Approach Considering Coupling Static Voltage Stability
authors: Shengyu Huang, Shuang Gao
abstract: This paper proposes a novel flexible interconnection planning approach for distribution networks to address static voltage stability issues in high-penetration renewable energy integration scenarios. Firstly, a planning-operation coordinated optimization model for flexible interconnection devices (FIDs) is proposed, the upper-level model optimizes soft open points (SOP) sitting and sizing, while the lower-level model optimizes flexible distribution network operation. Secondly, based on the single-port coupling equivalence method, a coupling static voltage stability index (CVSI) suitable for topology changes is constructed by considering multi-source and multi-load bus coupling effects, and this index is embedded into the planning model. Finally, the model is solved vis the generalized Benders decomposition algorithm and verified in the IEEE 33 - bus system. Results show CVSI effectively quantify static voltage stability; the CVSI-based planning scheme reduces operating costs, improves global stability margin, and achieves security-economy coordination.

title: Design and Implementation of an Optimal Wave Energy Measurement System
authors: Ranathunga U.G.D.N, Akalanka L.A.D.I, Arachchi A.C.H, Terashmila Lasagani
abstract: This paper presents the development of a real-time, IoT-enabled ocean wave monitoring low cost buoy designed for shallow water applications. Global wave measurement designs were reviewed and adapted to develop a cost-effective system optimized for shallow water applications. The proposed system integrates sensors—including an 3-axis accelerometer with gyroscope, GSM GPRS Module and GPS module—managed by an ESP32 microcontroller and powered by a solar-based energy system with dedicated power electronics. Key wave parameters, such as significant wave height and zero-crossing period, are acquired and transmitted in real time for remote access. A forecasting model using machine learning is under development to predict future wave energy potential based on wave and weather parameters, adapted for local bathymetric conditions. The design aims to support localized wave energy assessment and marine research with an affordable, scalable, and deployable solution.

title: Distributed Power Disaggregation for Heterogeneous Household Microgrids Based on Optimal Sensitivity
authors: Muchun Wan, Guangchao Geng, Yuzhong Gong, Quanyuan Jiang, Wei Du, Jun Wang
abstract: In recent years, the household microgrids with photovoltaic (PV) and energy storage have experienced rapid development. The large-scale integration of household microgrids has provided power grid with vast regulation resources, giving rise to the emergence of household microgrid aggregators (HMAs). For HMAs, the key challenge lies in meeting the grid regulation requirements while ensuring the optimality and real-time performance of power disaggregation. Therefore, this paper proposes a distributed power disaggregation model based on optimal sensitivity. First, the optimal sensitivity is employed to calculate the marginal cost curves for heterogeneous household microgrids. Second, based on the different marginal cost curves, a distributed power disaggregation model is established. Finally, using real-world data from the UK, comparative studies with existing centralized optimization and proportional disaggregation methods demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model in terms of optimality and real-time performance.

title: Rapid Evaluation of Frequency Nadir Considering Low Voltage Ride-Through Process of Renewable Energy Generators
authors: Cong Li, Bowei Dai, Zhenjun Gao, Xiaoqi Zhang, Chao Huo, Xuetao Dong, Ping Wei, Chao Duan
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy generation, it significantly impacts the dynamic response characteristics of power system. For the prediction of frequency nadir, current methods mainly rely on the frequency response model. However, existing studies are all based on the prerequisite that the specific renewable energy generators entering low voltage ride-through (LVRT) mode are known in advance from time-domain simulation. To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes a quasi-steady-state (QSS) modeling method based on time-scale separation which can identify the operating state of renewable energy generators separately. Subsequently, combined with conventional ASF model, a QSS-ASF model based on critical quasi-steady-state points is constructed to evaluate the frequency nadir considering LVRT process. Finally, the proposed method is validated on the modified IEEE 39-bus system, proving the accuracy of the frequency nadir assessment under different power source structure.

title: Field-Scale Weed Classification in Rice Crops using AI-Driven Vision Models
authors: Abhishek Rana, Vikas Attri, Punam Rattan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Weed identification and classification are critical to enhance crop yield and aiding precision farming. The traditional manual methods of identifying weeds are labour-intensive and inefficient and therefore necessitate automated vision-based systems. This paper presents a deep learning-based classification system, which will help distinguish between rice crops and weeds. The model makes use of a field-gathered dataset of custom field dataset using a mobile camera in the natural environment conditions at Lovely Professional University as well some sampling images from Rice Field weed BD Dataset. Three convolutional models, namely CNN, VGG-16 and ResNet-18, were trained on an 80:20 dataset division using Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and the CrossEntropyLoss function. The performance of the models was assessed using precision, recall, F1-score, loss convergence during training, and confusion matrices, which help justify the quality and generalization ability of the deeper models. The researchers assess the efficiency of the developed CNN models in detecting the presence of weeds in a farm's practice environment and emphasize the potential application of these neural networks in future automated precision farming programs.

title: Scalable OFDMA-Based Multi-User Channel Access for FANETs in Disaster Scenarios
authors: Sumaiya Sultana, A. F. M. Shahen Shah, Muhammet Ali Karabulut, Haci Ilhan
abstract: Flying Ad Hoc Networks (FANETs), with its high mobility and dynamic topology, enables wireless communication between unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs); this makes efficient channel access a major challenge. However, traditional singleuser and conventional contention-based medium access control (MAC) protocol suffer from extreme control overhead and unsuccessful backoff adaptation, which leads to high access delay and spoiled network performance under dense UAV deployments. Regarding these issues, this paper proposes an orthogonal frequency division multiple access (OFDMA)-based MAC protocol that jointly integrates multi-user request-to-send/clear-to-send (MU-RTS/CTS) signaling and an backoff mechanism with joint channel sensing. By authorizing aligned transmission on multiple frequency resource units and restricting excess contention and backoff expansion, the proposed model notably reduces control overhead while improving channel utilization and synchronization among UAVs. A transmission Opportunitybased analytical model is evolved to assess system throughput and delay under increasing network density and large-scale simulations are administered for authentication of performance. Simulation results show that the crucial upgrades throughput and reduces access delay compared to traditional FANET channel access, especially in dense UAV scenarios.

title: Physical-Constrained Data-Driven Power Flow for Three-Phase Three-Wire Distribution Feeder
authors: Yuxuan Zhang, Yixin Liu, Li Guo, Xialin Li, Zhognguan Wang, Chengshan Wang
abstract: This paper proposes a physics-constrained, data-driven power flow (PF) model tailored for unbalanced three-phase three-wire (3P3W) distribution feeders. By introducing a phase-coupling matrix, the classical Lin-DistFlow model is extended to capture inter-phase interactions using only scalar variables. A physically guided regression approach is developed to improve the accuracy of the data-driven model, and a dimension-lifting technique is further employed to compensate for modeling errors. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method achieves over 60% improvement in average PF accuracy under significant measurement noise compared to benchmark methods, while maintaining reliable sensitivity characteristics. The model offers both high mapping accuracy and dependable power-voltage sensitivity, making it well-suited for a wide range of practical applications.

title: Scalable Pothole Detection System for Urban Road Networks
authors: E. Priya, Venkat Dronadula, Bharathwaj S, Harrish Raja D
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Degraded road surfaces, particularly potholes, remain a persistent concern in urban transportation systems, negatively impacting commuter safety, vehicle longevity, and infrastructure maintenance planning. Conventional road inspection techniques, which rely on manual surveys or specialized monitoring equipment, are often expensive, labor- intensive [2], [3]. This paper presents a low-cost, embedded pothole detection and mapping framework designed for continuous, real-time operation in smart transportation environments. The proposed system integrates a Raspberry Pi, a vehicle-mounted camera, and a Neo-6M GPS module to capture road surface imagery and associate detected defects with precise geographic coordinates. Pothole identification is carried out using a lightweight YOLOv11 deep learning model trained on a custom dataset of 13,000 annotated images, optimized for deployment on resource-constrained hardware. Detected potholes are spatially aggregated and visualized through a heatmap-based interface, enabling intuitive assessment of road condition distribution and severity. Experimental evaluation shows that the system achieves an accuracy of 80.2% while maintaining real-time inference performance on the Raspberry Pi. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework provides a practical, scalable, and economically viable solution for automated road condition monitoring, supporting safer navigation and data-driven infrastructure maintenance.

title: Real-Time Construction Site Accident Detection System with Siren Alert
authors: Anushka Bhivgade, Chetan Puri, Ketan Wanjari, Riya Hawage
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Construction sites are inherently hazardous, with risks such as falls, fire incidents, and non-compliance with personal protective equipment (PPE) leading to significant injuries and fatalities. Traditional safety monitoring methods, reliant on manual inspections and basic sensor systems, suffer from inefficiencies and human error. This paper proposes a real-time construction site accident detection framework utilizing computer vision and deep learning techniques. The system integrates three key modules: helmet detection using YOLOv8, fall detection based on YOLOv8 with aspect ratio analysis, and fire/smoke detection using OpenCV-based color and motion analysis. Detected violations or hazards trigger on-screen alerts and audible siren alarms, with events logged for record-keeping. The proposed approach eliminates the need for manual supervision, offering a scalable and cost-effective solution suitable for diverse construction environments. Experimental evaluations demonstrate the capability of the framework to operate under varying lighting and environmental conditions with reliable accuracy. The software-based system achieves real-time performance and provides a foundation for future integration with IoT devices, enhancing construction site safety through automated monitoring and intelligent decision-making.

title: Mode Switching-Induced Instability of Multi-source Feed DC Microgrid
authors: Shanshan Jiang, Zelin Sun, Jiankun Zhang, Hua Geng
abstract: In DC microgrids (DCMGs), DC-bus signaling based control strategy is extensively used for power management, where mode switching plays a crucial role in achieving multi-source coordination. However, few studies have noticed the impact of mode switching and switching strategies on system voltage stability. To fill this gap, this paper aims to provide a general analysis framework for mode switching-induced instability in multi-source DCMGs. First, manifold theory is employed to analyze the stability of the DCMG switched system. Subsequently, the instability mechanism and its physical interpretation are explored. The positive feedback activated by the decreasing DC bus voltage during the switching process leads to instability. Switching strategy may inadvertently contribute to this instability. To improve stability, a novel control method based on mode scheduling is proposed, by adjusting switching strategy and thereby correcting the system trajectory. Finally, both real-time simulations and experimental tests on a DCMG system verify the correctness and effectiveness of theoretical analysis results.

title: A Multimodal Approach to Ergonomic Assessment Fusing an IMU-Based RULA and Surface EMG System
authors: Ratikanlaya Tanthuwapathom, Udomporn Manupibul, Wimonrat Jarumethitanont, Weerawat Limroongreungrat, Songpol Ongwattanakul, Warakorn Charoensuk
abstract: Work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WMSDs) often arise from prolonged periods of static sitting and inadequate ergonomic posture while using a computer. This study investigates the correlation between posture-related ergonomic risk and physiological muscle strain by combining an inertial measurement unit (IMU)-based rapid upper limb assessment (RULA) system with surface electromyography (sEMG). Twenty office workers and college students performed two 30-minute typing tasks, during which five IMU sensors recorded the kinematics of their neck, trunk, and shoulder, while EMG measured activity in the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) and upper trapezius (UT) muscles. The results indicated that higher RULA neck and trunk scores were consistently associated with greater EMG amplitude, suggesting that these muscles exerted more effort to maintain poor posture for extended periods. The UT muscles exhibited significantly more activity than the SCM muscles. Muscle fatigue, evaluated via normalized median frequency (NMF) slopes, showed substantial inter-individual variability, indicating possible compensatory movements during low-level static tasks. Overall, the findings demonstrate that poor posture significantly increases muscle workload and highlight the usefulness of sensor-based systems for objective ergonomic assessment.

title: Low-Loss Millimeter-Wave Surface-Mounted Wideband Filtering Power Divider Based on MEFAB Process
authors: Jin Xu, Zi-Hao Zhou, Zi-Fan Jin, Biao Yang, Hui-Kun Yang, Shi-Xin Meng, Jia-Hao Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: This article presents the design of rectangular microcoaxial line (RμCL) wideband filtering Wilkinson power divider (WPD) using Micro-structure Electrochemical Fabrication (MEFAB) process. Firstly, a two-stage RμCL Wilkinson power divider is introduced as a foundation, operating from 11.95 to 43.6 GHz with a fractional bandwidth (FBW) of 113.9%, a minimum insertion loss (IL) of 0.8 dB, isolation better than 15 dB, amplitude and phase imbalances below ±0.38 dB and ±3°, respectively, and a circuit size of 0.69λ₀ × 0.34λ₀, where λ₀ is the free-space wavelength at 29 GHz. On this basis, an 18-40 GHz RμCL wideband filtering WPD is further designed by using stepped-impedance stub loaded coupled-line resonator (SIS-L-CLR) replacing the first stage of the above designed WPD. Design equations for the filtering configuration are systematically derived through even-odd mode analysis, with theoretical predictions verified by electromagnetic simulations. The fabricated RμCL filtering power divider achieves an operating band of 17.95–41.05 GHz, a FBW of 78.3%, a minimum IL of 0.9 dB, isolation better than 15 dB, amplitude imbalance under 0.19 dB, phase imbalance below 3.4°, and a compact circuit size of 1.18λ₀ × 0.4λ₀.
