title: High-Precision 6DOF Pose Estimation via Global Phase Retrieval in Fringe Projection Profilometry for 3D Mapping
authors: Sehoon Tak, Keunhee Cho, Sangpil Kim, Jae-Sang Hyun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Digital fringe projection (DFP) enables micrometer-level 3D reconstruction, yet extending it to large-scale mapping remains challenging because six-degree-of-freedom pose estimation often cannot match the reconstruction’s precision. Conventional iterative closest point (ICP) registration becomes inefficient on multi-million-point clouds and typically relies on downsampling or feature-based selection, which can reduce local detail and degrade pose precision. Drift-correction methods improve long-term consistency but do not resolve sampling sensitivity in dense DFP point clouds. We propose a high-precision pose estimation method that augments a moving DFP system with a fixed, intrinsically calibrated global projector. Using the global projector’s phase-derived pixel constraints and a PnP-style reprojection objective, the method estimates the DFP system pose in a fixed reference frame without relying on deterministic feature extraction, and we experimentally demonstrate sampling invariance under coordinate-preserving subsampling. Experiments demonstrate sub-millimeter pose accuracy against a reference with quantified uncertainty bounds, high repeatability under aggressive subsampling, robust operation on homogeneous surfaces and low-overlap views, and reduced error accumulation when used to correct ICP-based trajectories. The method extends DFP toward accurate 3D mapping in quasi-static scenarios such as inspection and metrology, with the trade-off of time-multiplexed acquisition for the additional projector measurements.

title: Polar Finite-Difference-based Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Eddy Current Problems
authors: Zhitao Zhao, Yujiao Zhang, Hongda Sun, Qiang Zhang, Xiongfeng Huang
abstract: For electromagnetic problems, Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) based on automatic differentiation (AD) often suffer from high computational cost and difficulty in accurately capturing interface behavior. The integration of the Finite Difference Method (FDM) into PINNs offers a promising way to alleviate the high computational burden. However, existing attempts are typically limited to rectangular geometries and constant-coefficient settings. This paper proposes a finite-difference-based PINNs framework formulated in polar coordinates. The discrete residuals and boundary conditions are derived in polar form, with finite-difference quotients embedded directly into the loss function to replace AD. A vectorization strategy suitable for spatially varying coefficients is proposed, enabling efficient computation when the convolution stencil changes dynamically with material properties. Furthermore, the Fourier feature mapping is employed to enhance the network’s ability to learn high-frequency components, while harmonic averaging is applied to achieve second-order accuracy in the vicinity of material discontinuities. A two-dimensional eddy-current field problem in a single-phase induction motor is used for validation. The relative L2 errors of the real and imaginary parts of the solution are 2.86% and 1.5%, respectively, showing a close match with finite element results. In addition, on a baseline network, increasing the width of each hidden layer by 100 neurons leads to only about a 2% increase in training time, demonstrating favorable scalability and practical applicability of the proposed framework. In summary, the proposed methods provide a generalizable solution for learning electromagnetic PDEs in circular, annular, and sector-shaped regions.

title: Enhancing Torque Capability of Flux-Switching Electrically-Excited Machines by Dual-Armature Winding Configuration
authors: Hui Wen, Bingtuo Chen, Wenting Wang, Chuyan Wu, Jiongjiong Cai, Lijian Wu
abstract: Flux-switching electrically excited (FSEE) machines offer attractive advantages such as low cost, no risk of demagnetization, and flexible flux regulation. However, their torque capability is generally inferior to their permanent magnet (PM) counterparts, primarily due to the inherently weaker DC field excitation. This study reveals that the torque growth is limited even under strong DC excitation, as the distinctive over saturation effect restricts further increase in magnetic flux across the stator armature winding. To address this limitation, a dual armature (DA) winding configuration is proposed, featuring armature windings wound on both the stator and rotor. The proposed rotor armature winding remains unaffected by the over saturation effect and effectively utilizes rotor space. In addition, geometric optimization featuring wide field slots and narrow stator armature slots provides another means to mitigate the over saturation effect. As a result, under Id = 0 control, the optimized DA-FSEE machine achieves a 0.99Nm torque output, significantly outperforming the optimized FSEE machine with a 0.68Nm torque output. Furthermore, by coordinating stator and rotor current phase angles at ϕs = 10° and ϕr = -30°, the torque of the DA-FSEE machine can be elevated to 1.20Nm, marking a 75% improvement over the optimal FSEE design. This additional enhancement mainly stems from the torque contributed by the dual-armature interaction, a mechanism that is neither functional under Id = 0 control nor utilized in prior DA research. Finally, a DA-FSEE prototype is fabricated and tested to validate the finite element simulation under reduced ampere-turn levels.

title: How the Properties of Inter-Area Modes of Oscillation Vary with Operating Conditions: A Study in the US Eastern Interconnection
authors: Shuchismita Biswas, Jim Follum
abstract: Safe and reliable power grid operations require maintaining small-signal stability, which can be compromised by poorly damped oscillation modes. Undamped inter-area oscillations have historically contributed to several major blackouts. Traditional planning models often fail to capture the wide range of system conditions encountered in real-time operations, leaving potential stability risks unrecognized. This motivates the development of measurement-based tools that continuously monitor dominant oscillatory modes and provide early warning of issues such as low damping. This paper reports on a yearlong study in the U.S. Eastern Interconnection (EI) that used synchrophasor measurements to track two dominant modes and investigated how they varied with operating conditions. Quantile regression was applied to construct a correlation model relating mode properties to operating conditions, and a bootstrap method was used to quantify uncertainty in the correlation estimates. The results show that the frequency and damping ratio of both modes decrease with increasing system loading. In addition, the study finds evidence that increased penetration of inverter-based generation leads to higher frequency and lower damping in one mode, while no significant impact is observed for the other. The methodology developed in this study provides a systematic framework for periodically assessing the evolving characteristics of inter-area modes and for flagging emerging stability risks before they pose serious reliability threats.

title: A Novel Control Strategy for Hydrogen Electrolyzer to Ride-Through Coupled Frequency and Voltage Disturbance Events
authors: Muhammad M. Kabsha, Muhammad Noman Ashraf, Mohamed Shawky El Moursi, Tarek H. M. El-Fouly, Ahmed Al-Durra
abstract: Large-scale hydrogen electrolyzers (HE) are currently gaining more interest in integration into power systems to enhance the energy transition towards renewable energy sources. However, to ensure power systems' stability and security, the large-scale HE plants should comply with the grid code regulations (GCRs). This paper presents a novel coordinated control strategy for HE plants to ensure efficient ancillary services while respecting the technical constraints of HE technology. The proposed approach enables the HE plants to simultaneously support the grid during coupled frequency and voltage disturbance events induced by consecutive and simultaneous disturbance events while considering the active power ramp rate (APRR) constraints of the HE. Coordinated control schemes are incorporated into the power-electronic interface (PEI) of the HE plant to achieve fast frequency and voltage support. Additionally, to comply with low-voltage ride-through (LVRT) without triggering the HE plant, the proposed strategy is compared with conventional techniques to simultaneously provide the necessary power to the HE plant and provide fast frequency support to the power grid. The proposed control strategy shows superiority in enhancing the transient frequency and voltage responses of the HE plants during coupled frequency and voltage disturbance events. A Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) test system is used to validate the proposed strategy.

title: Enabling Computation and Communication Overlap in PIMs for on-device LLM Inference
authors: Siu Jeong, Suhwan Kim, Changyong Eom, Jaehyuk Huh
abstract: DRAM-based processing-in-memory (PIM) exploits all-bank execution to accelerate memory-bound workloads, but limited PIM capacity makes runtime data movement unavoid able and often performance-critical. Existing PIM architectures tightly couple computation and memory access across all banks, serializing computation and data transfer and limiting opportunities to hide communication latency. This paper proposes a bank-level computation–communication overlap mechanism that selectively assigns a subset of bank resources for communication, enabling concurrent data transfer without disrupting ongoing computation while preserving the unified control model of all bank PIM. Evaluations on mixture-of-experts (MoE) and multi small language model (multi-SLM) inference workloads using a simulated LPDDR5-based PIM system show execution time reductions of up to 19% and 16%, respectively, along with consistently improved channel bandwidth utilization compared to all-bank and channel-level overlap baselines.

title: Lab characterization of the soft X-ray transition edge spectrometer sensor array for the Advanced Photon Source
authors: Tejas Guruswamy, Orlando Quaranta, Danielle Riekse, Jad Panjaitan, Lisa Gades, Kelsey Morgan, Joel Weber, Daniel Swetz, Antonino Miceli
abstract: Soft X-ray resonant scattering is a sensitive probe of electronic, magnetic, and orbital order, but its weak signals are often embedded in substantial elastic, inelastic, and fluorescence backgrounds. In order to enable new science at the Advanced Photon Source (APS) X-ray synchrotron, we have developed a transition edge sensor array-based spectrometer instrument optimized for energy-resolved detection in the 0.2 to 2keV band, sustained operation at elevated photon count rates, and a mechanical design that allows for a small sensor–sample distance without interfering with existing hardware. The newly fabricated 256-pixel sensor array consists of sidecar Mo/Au transition edge sensors with Bi/Au absorbers, packaged onto a microsnout platform with frequency multiplexed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>Mux readout. We report on laboratory characterization of this array prior to final instrument integration and beamline deployment. DC current–voltage measurements establish acceptable uniformity and yield but identify a higher than intended bilayer critical temperature. Readout characterization shows the majority of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>Mux channels operating in a regime compatible with TESlimited energy resolution. Soft Xray spectral measurements using a lab X-ray source demonstrate a fullwidthathalfmaximum energy resolution median across the array of 2.3eV at Al K<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> (1.5keV), with almost 80% of all pixels producing spectra with resolution below 3eV. The larger than targeted energy resolution is primarily attributed to a reduced resistance sensitivity to temperature (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>) relative to the prototype designs. Overall the results indicate the spectrometer meets the minimum requirements for resonant soft X-ray scattering and related measurements at the APS. Immediate next steps focus on commissioning of the instrument at the beamline, validation of high countrate operation, and establishment of energy calibration and data analysis workflows tailored to soft X-ray science.

title: Gaussian Mixture Model Based Bayesian Learning for Sparse Channel Estimation in Orthogonal Time Frequency Space Modulated Systems
authors: Surbhi Gehlot, Suraj Srivastava, Sandeep Kumar Yadav, Lajos Hanzo
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: A novel Gaussian mixture model (GMM)–aided sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) framework is proposed for channel state information (CSI) estimation in orthogonal time-frequency space (OTFS) modulated systems. The key attribute of the proposed algorithm lies in casting CSI recovery as an SBL inference problem, where posterior distributions are iteratively refined under a hierarchical GMM prior. Using this approach, the sparsity-inducing variances beneficially promote sparsity in the delay–Doppler (DD) domain, while additionally augmenting the capability of SBL to exploit channel statistics more effectively. Moreover, to fully exploit the GMM's ability to approximate arbitrary probability density functions and model complex multipath fading scenarios, the channel statistics are represented using a complex Gaussian mixture. Simultaneously, the method leverages time-domain (TD) pilots without requiring wasteful DD domain guard intervals, thereby ensuring low pilot overhead and high spectral efficiency. The CSI recovered is subsequently applied in a linear minimum mean square error (MMSE) detector for reliable data detection. To benchmark performance, the Oracle-MMSE and the Bayesian Cramér-Rao lower bound (BCRLB) are also derived. Our simulation results demonstrate significant performance improvement over the state-of-the-art sparse estimation methods.

title: The Price of Optimal Fleet Selection
authors: Wentao Zhang, Site Qu, Guoqiang Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Balancing the size of vehicle fleet and the quality of transportation service is important yet challenging for urban transportation systems. This paper studies the optimal fleet selection problem in dial-a-ride regime. To achieve this, a two-stage mixed-integer programming formulation is presented. It is demonstrated that there exists an optimal vehicle set provided that the considered dial-a-ride problem is weakly solvable. The strong solvability problem is also discussed by introducing a discount factor to strike a trade-off between the fleet size and routing cost associated. When the probability measure of the vehicle set is unavailable, the Wasserstein measure, anchored by an empirical distribution derived from training data, is used for approximation. It is revealed that a nontrivial duality gap exists, leading to an infeasibility of the Lagrangian argument employed in the distributionally robust framework that transforms an infinite stochastic program into a finite alternative. By employing a robust primal decomposition method, it is shown that the relaxed version of the considered program is equivalent to a finite-dimensional quadratic conic program. Subsequently, a set of disjunctions is constructed to extract the optimizer with a finite number of iterations. Furthermore, the “distance” between the empirical and true vehicle sets is discussed as well. Finally, the benchmark data is carried out to illustrate the theoretical justification and significance of the proposed method.

title: Idealization-Oriented Planning for Mixed Traffic at Highway Interchanges: Mitigating HDV-Induced Inefficiencies Under High CAV Penetration
authors: Pengfei Li, Yihe Chen, Yunhao Hu, Keqiang Li, Yugong Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Highway interchanges are critical nodes in transportation networks but frequently experience congestion due to complex interactions between ramp diverging and merging flows. While Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) are expected to improve traffic efficiency, the coexistence of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) introduces disturbances that undermine performance. Prior studies have addressed certain cooperative strategies of CAVs in mixed traffic, but they often overlook the interactions between adjacent bottlenecks in interchange scenarios and the heterogeneity of HDV driving styles. To address this gap, this study proposes an Idealization-Oriented Planning (IOP) scheme that enhances interchange traffic efficiency in mixed traffic with high CAV penetration. Enabled by Cloud Control Systems (CCS), an idealized optimum for the overall travel efficiency under the assumption of full CAV penetration is firstly derived as a reference, based on which short-horizon strategies are computed to mitigate disturbances caused by HDV behaviors. Specifically, a DeePC-based Lane-Change Hesitation Guidance mechanism identifies conservative HDVs in advance and adjusts lane-change gaps to prevent excessive deceleration. In parallel, an Aggressive Merging Avoidance mechanism formulates a potential game in which adjacent CAVs cooperate to constrain inefficient HDV cut-ins, yielding safe and system-efficient strategies. A Python-based simulation platform validates the proposed approach, showing that IOP outperforms benchmark methods across various traffic conditions and HDV penetration rates (5%–30%); as an illustrative case, under high-flow conditions with 15% HDV penetration, IOP achieves a delay reduction of up to 53%.

title: A Video-Based Autism Detection In Children Using MLP Classifier and Computer Vision Technique
authors: Renuka N, Kannan N, Karthikeyan T A, Madhiuksha S, Akshara R K, Madhumitha S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: This article examines ways machine learning and computer vision can be used to detect the symptoms of autism in children at an early stage.The moment of diagnosis is important, both to the healthcare and to the development of the child, hence automating it makes a lot of sense.In this case, MediaPipe is used to extract information concerning the facial expression and the body movements.OpenCV takes care of face capture of uploaded videos as well as live video streams.After acquiring these features, we save them in the form of CSV files and then subject them to an MLP classifier, so as to determine whether the videos depict autistic or non autistic behavior. The results of the MLP classifier are high accuracy after number crunching. It can be used with both pre recorded videos and live footage and the system even records the live results automatically that can be reviewed later.Experiments indicate that MediaPipes feature extraction with deep learning with MLP would actually be effective.This would make the process a viable tool of early screening and behavioral evaluation. To clinicians, this would save time on manual observation and a higher chance of detecting autism in its early stages, thus children would obtain help earlier. On balance, this study advances the automated autism detection, which will combine computer vision and machine learning into the tangible healthcare solutions.

title: A novel modified circular shaped CPW antenna for 5g applications
authors: C. Anandhan, R. Thangam
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: This paper presents a low-profile single element port wideband and multiband printed conformal antenna that is suitable for use in 5G applications. The planned antenna is printed on a FR4 substrate that is 0.8 mm thick and has dimensions of 26 mm x 32 mm. The CPW antenna structure could operate in the following spectrums across a 10 dB impedance bandwidth: 3.4 – 3.8 GHz. The radiation efficiency is up to 93 % and the overall gain is around 4.8 dBi across the operating band. Additional research has shown that the proposed antenna is capable of functioning well for multi-band as well as wide-band applications.

title: Design and Implementation of an Automated PII Detection System using DeBERTaV2 and NLP
authors: Nandhini Priya P, Treasa Philip, Yazhini K, R. Roopa Chandrika
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: This project focuses on the development and implementation of an intelligent system that automatically detects and removes Personally Identifiable Information (PII) from textual data using advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) and deep learning techniques. The proposed model employs the DeBERTaV2 transformer, an enhanced version of BERT, fine-tuned for token classification and Named Entity Recognition (NER) tasks. The system identifies sensitive information such as names, emails, addresses, and phone numbers while ensuring data privacy and maintaining semantic consistency. Python and Streamlit are used for system development, providing an interactive interface for text analysis and redaction. To validate performance, multiple deep learning models—BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT, and DeBERTaV2—were compared using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy metrics. The results demonstrate that DeBERTaV2 outperforms other transformer-based models, achieving an overall accuracy of 96context-aware PII detection significantly improves data protection and reduces the need for manual intervention, supporting secure data handling in educational and research domains.

title: Incentive-Based Demand Response Strategy Optimization Considering External and Behavioral Uncertainties
authors: Zihan Cai, Hao Wu, Jiaxu Du, Kai Hou, Ruifeng Zhao, Danyang Xu
abstract: As a flexible demand-side management approach, demand response (DR) can effectively alleviate power supply– demand imbalances and has gradually become an essential mechanism for routine power system regulation. However, practical applications of demand response are often challenged by uncertainties arising from external environments and user behaviors, which affect optimization outcomes and implementation effectiveness. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes an improved incentive-based DR model that simultaneously incorporates both external and behavioral uncertainties. Specifically, external uncertainty is characterized through a response uncertainty factor, while behavioral uncertainty is captured by introducing the degree of user rationality. The proposed model is initially formulated as a bi-level optimization problem involving interactions between the load aggregator (LA) and users, and subsequently transformed into an equivalent single-level optimization problem to facilitate efficient computation. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed model by demonstrating significant reductions in the total cost for LA and notable improvements in the efficiency and accuracy of demand response implementation.

title: Sustainable Energy Generation using Piezoelectric Footstep Mechanism
authors: U. Prabhu Manikandan, P. Samuel Pakianathan, S. Harish Natarajan, P.I. Jay Akash
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: This study provides a new approach that seeks to deal with energy issues through the deployment of the pressure by human footsteps as a renewable energy source to produce electricity. The solution involves integrating piezoelectric sensors that are installed in floor panels, these sensors take advantage of the piezoelectric effect to produce electrical charges whenever they are subjected to mechanical force by footsteps of human beings. Since the resultant output is usually of low magnitude and is not smooth, it is then fed through a signal conditioning unit to rectify and control the voltage. The system is monitored by an Arduino microcontroller that control the storage of the collected energy and provides a real-time display of the real performance of the system. The main aim of this study is to come up with an efficient power generating system that is friendly to the environment and can be implemented in areas with high population like parks, railway stations and shopping centers. This study shows charging capability of small electronic devices, and how the daily human activity can be translated into sustainable energy. Finally, it goes in line with the idea of smart cities by transforming the traditional infrastructure into an active, and zero-emission source of energy that facilitates environmental sustainability.

title: An Integrated Analysis of Traditional, Hybrid and AI-Driven Localization Strategies in Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Sucheta Panda, Sushree Bibhurada B. Priyadarshini
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Research in Intelligent Computing in Engineering (RICE)
abstract: Localization is a key part of the Internet of Things(IoT). It helps in tracking objects, monitoring the environment and managing smart cities. This paper reviews different localization methods and explains its working, advantages and limitations. It discusses main techniques like Time of Arrival(ToA), Angle of Arrival(AoA) and Received Signal Strength(RSS).Range-based methods such as triangulation and trilateration are used to find the exact position of nodes. Systems like GPS, Wi-Fi and cellular networks are also studied for their coverage and accuracy. Inertial-based systems are useful but can become less accurate over time due to drift. The paper also explains hybrid methods that combine multiple techniques and use machine learning to improve accuracy. New technologies like Bluetooth Low Energy(BLE) and UltraWideband(UWB) are effective for indoor localization. The study also looks at important factors such as energy use, privacy, cost and scalability. Unlike traditional reviews, this paper uses both qualitative and quantitative methods to compare techniques. By evaluating traditional, hybrid and AI-based techniques, it highlights trends, performance and future research directions in Wireless Sensor Networks(WSNs).

title: An Efficient Signal Processing using EfficientNetB4-Lite Supported Deepfake Detection in Spectral Sensing TinyML Environment
authors: V. Saranya Devi, Mo'Ath Alluwaici, P. Adi Lakshmi, S.A. Shifani, K Gagan Kumar, V. Manasa
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Efficient Signal Processing (SP) enables quick pattern recognition from raw sensor data, reducing computational load by eliminating noise and emphasizing critical areas for accurate detection, enhancing system performance. The research introduces the Efficient Signal Extraction and Deep Fake-Focused Surveillance (ESEDFS) model for structural health monitoring and video authentication in limited-resource settings. It reduces system noise with a Butterworth filter (BWF) and utilizes data from 12 transducers to create 66 master baselines for analysis. Structural changes are quantified through feature metrics. For deep fake detection, CCTV frames are resized, normalized, and converted into tensors before input to an optimized EfficientNetB4-Lite model for real-time authentication. The ESEDFS framework is tailored for real-time on-device anomaly detection in edge environments. It focuses on nondestructive structural damage detection through ultrasonic- guided-waves and light deep fake detection for CCTV surveillance, operating without cloud computing or large-scale video forensics. Efficient microcontrollers incorporate features like dynamic range and quantization for effective model performance. The performance analysis is loss of (0.9, 0.6, 0.3), accuracy of 87% to 97%, ESEDFS overall of 92.1%, 91.2% accuracy and a 90.8% F1-score, ROC-AUC of 0.97. Calculation.

title: Rice Leaf Disease Classification using EfficientNet and Transfer Learning
authors: Sukanya N. S., V. Asha, Vasumathi M. T., Sumit Singh, Suyash Trivedi, Sujay M. Mundaragi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: To ensure food security and improve crop productivity, early identification and rapid analysis of rice leaf diseases are crucial. Conventional disease identification methods rely on manual visual inspection, which is labor intensive, subjective, and often unreliable at early stages. This study proposes an automated rice leaf disease classification system based on transfer learning using the EfficientNet architecture. The proposed approach focuses on identifying disease-affected rice leaves, including Leaf Smut disease, Brown Spot and Bacterial Blight. A curated dataset was constructed and enhanced through data preprocessing and augmentation techniques to improve robustness under realistic agricultural conditions. The EfficientNet backbone was fine-tuned by modifying its classification layer to enable effective feature extraction and accurate disease classification. Experimental evaluation on an independent test dataset demonstrates that the proposed model achieves an overall accuracy of 99.7%, together with high precision, recall, and F1-scores across all disease categories. These results indicate that the EfficientNet-based methodology provides a reliable and computationally effective solution for detection of rice leaf diseases.

title: MD-LiViT:Multi-Domain Lightweight Vision Transformer for Assistive Lung Cancer Detection from CT Scans and Histopathological Images
authors: Rahul Mishra, Satya Prakash Sahu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Lung malignancy ranks among the world’s most aggressive and life-threatening cancer types. Still its detection remains a challenging issue. Early identification and diagnosis can significantly improve the rate of survival for patients. This study introduces MD-LiViT, a multi-domain lightweight vision transformer developed to assist lung cancer diagnosis by utilizing chest CT scans and high-resolution histopathological microscopic images. The proposed model is designed to address modality heterogeneity and provides a solution for deployment on resource-constrained devices. MD-LiViT is evaluated through a 5-fold cross-validation method, achieving an overall test accuracy of 99.87% for the histopathological image dataset along with 98.79% from chest CT scan image dataset. For enhanced interpretability, Grad-CAM is employed to uncover the visual patterns that affect prediction outcomes. Heatmaps illustrate radiological and pathological regions of interest to assist clinicians in decision-making.MD-LiViT is a lightweight, efficient, and understandable system for both radiographic and pathological analysis, offering a promising resource-efficient clinician decision-support system in lung cancer screening and treatment.

title: Harnessing Quantum Computing for Precision Medicine: Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) for Drug Discovery, Genomic Analysis, and Healthcare Optimization
authors: Karthikeyan M P, S.J. Sadheesh Kumar, Manimuthu.P, S.Sridevi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Quantum computing has emerged as a promising paradigm for solving computationally intractable problems in healthcare, particularly within the scope of precision medicine. Traditional approaches to drug discovery, genomic analysis, and healthcare optimisation face significant limitations due to the exponential complexity of biological and clinical datasets. This article discusses the use of the Quantum Approximate Optimisation Algorithm (QAOA) as a quantum-classical hybrid framework to solve these issues. In drug discovery, QAOA provided better binding affinity prediction and lower root mean square deviation (RMSD) than traditional docking techniques. In genomic analysis, QAOA-boosted feature selection enhanced classification accuracy at the cost of reduced convergence time. Likewise, QAOA-based scheduling yielded better performance than traditional heuristics in healthcare optimisation by providing greater resource usage with fewer iterations. Experimental results show that warm-starting QAOA with classical initialisation reduces convergence further by up to 30%. These results highlight the promise of QAOA in speeding up biomedical computations and pave the way for scalable, quantum-enabled solutions in precision medicine.

title: Multi Perspective Effect Analysis of Quantum Computing in the Rise of Artificial Intelligence
authors: Seda Nur GÜNGÖR, Mehmet KARAKÖSE
abstract: The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence (AI) and quantum computing (QC) is fostering a research landscape in which these two technologies increasingly shape each other across multiple technical layers. This study provides an overview of ten representative categories, including quantumenhanced machine learning, quantum optimization for AI models, quantum encoding, hybrid quantum-classical architectures, quantum-inspired models for AI, AI-assisted quantum circuit optimization, AI-driven variational parameter tuning, AI-assisted quantum hardware calibration, and AIdriven quantum architecture search. It approaches QC-AI interactions within a conceptual model framework that organizes these relationships across dimensions of directionality, layer, and modality. This framework aims to provide an integrative analytical basis that supports future methodological and system-level research.

title: Data-Driven Identification of Electricity Market Supply Curves
authors: Hongyang Li, Yunpeng Xiao, Zicheng Wang, Zhantao Fan, Shiqi Liu, Dawei Liao, Yiping Chen
abstract: Power sector reform and electricity market development are pivotal globally to enable the energy transition and enhance power system efficiency. Intensifying electricity market competition heightens demand for decision-making tools. Accurate identification of the electricity market supply curve is critical for market operators to enhance market surveillance and efficiency, while enabling participants to maximize profits. Electricity markets generate large volumes of high-resolution time-series data, enabling robust data-driven approaches. This paper proposes a data-driven method to identify electricity market supply curves. By employing the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) to characterize the supply curve and integrating Bayesian inference with Particle Markov Chain Monte Carlo (PMCMC), the method achieves posterior estimation of the day-ahead market supply curve. Crucially, this approach operates without reliance on unit-specific physical parameters or bidding information. The framework delivers actionable insights, supporting market participants in bidding strategy formulation and operators in market risk management.

title: A Curriculum-Grounded AI Tutoring System
authors: Sara Đurović, Sara Kovačević, Marko Lasica, Boban Uskoković, Adna Softić, Amra Džuho, Almir Badnjević
abstract: This paper presents TutorMe, an AI-assisted elearning chatbot platform designed to support students with lesson explanations, question answering, quiz generation, and learning-material navigation using curriculum-aligned, digitized resources. TutorMe is implemented as a modular web application integrated with a domain-restricted knowledge base built from structured PDF learning materials. The contribution is engineering-focused: we describe a reproducible design for a knowledge-base-grounded tutoring assistant, document key configuration choices (prompting strategy, retrieval behavior, and platform settings), and report a pilot offline evaluation using a rubric for manual assessment of correctness and groundedness. In an internal test set of 120 questions spanning biology, chemistry, physics, and mathematics at three difficulty levels, manual review showed that 80.0 % of answers were fully correct. Biology exhibited the lowest accuracy (60%) due to terminology imprecisions, while mathematics achieved 93.3%. We discuss limitations including hallucination risk, curriculum drift, privacy, and the need for teacher oversight, and outline steps toward deployment-grade validation.

title: Cluster-Based Hybrid MAC Protocol for FANET with Massive MIMO in 5G and Beyond
authors: Ece Tan, Sumaiya Sultana, A. F. M. Shahen Shah, Muhammet Ali Karabulut, Haci Ilhan
abstract: Flying ad hoc networks (FANETs) composed of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) provide flexible and infrastructure-independent connectivity in 5 G and beyond. Recently, cluster-based FANET architectures have been proposed in disaster and emergency scenarios to support search and rescue operations. However, their performance is severely limited by high node density and contention-based medium access. In this paper, a cluster-based hybrid medium access control (MAC) protocol is proposed in which the cluster head is equipped with massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) capability. An algorithm is presented to explain the mMIMO-enabled hybrid MAC protocol for cluster-based FANET. The proposed MAC divides channel access into a contention-based control phase and a scheduled data phase, where the cluster head exploits mMIMO spatial multiplexing to serve multiple UAVs simultaneously. Analytical study is carried out. Simulation results are provided, which show that the proposed protocol performs better than the traditional singleantenna cluster-based system.

title: Investment Value Analysis and Strategy Research of New Energy Storage and Virtual Power Plant
authors: Yaping Yang, Qian Cao, Peng Chang, Changling Li, Qingyang Meng, Nan Liang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: Under the "dual carbon" goals, the development of a new-type power system has become a core task in energy transition. As critical infrastructure supporting high-proportion renewable energy integration, new energy storage technologies and virtual power plants are demonstrating increasingly prominent investment value. This paper systematically evaluates their investment potential by analyzing technical-economic characteristics, market mechanisms, and policy environments, while proposing differentiated investment strategies. The study reveals that electrochemical energy storage has achieved commercial readiness in frequency regulation and peak-shaving markets, whereas virtual power plants show significant potential in load aggregation and demand response applications. Investors should focus on three dimensions—technological maturity, policy support, and business model innovation—adopting phased and diversified portfolio strategies.

title: Design and Development of Modular Limbs for Reconfigurable Robots on the Moon
authors: Gustavo H. Diaz, A. Sejal Jain, Matteo Brugnera, Elian Neppel, Shreya Santra, Kentaro Uno, Kazuya Yoshida
abstract: In this paper, we present the development of 4-DOF robot limbs, which we call Moonbots, designed to connect in various configurations with each other and wheel modules, enabling adaptation to different environments and tasks. These modular components are intended primarily for robotic systems in space exploration and construction on the Moon in our Moonshot project. Such modular robots add flexibility and versatility for space missions where resources are constrained. Each module is driven by a common actuator characterized by a high torque-to-speed ratio, supporting both precise control and dynamic motion when required. This unified actuator design simplifies development and maintenance across the different module types. The paper describes the hardware implementation, the mechanical design of the modules, and the overall software architecture used to control and coordinate them. Additionally, we evaluate the control performance of the actuator under various load conditions to characterize its suitability for modular robot applications. To demonstrate the adaptability of the system, we introduce nine functional configurations assembled from the same set of modules: 4DOF-limb, 8DOF-limb, vehicle, dragon, minimal, quadruped, cargo, cargo-minimal, and bike. These configurations reflect different locomotion strategies and task-specific behaviors, offering a practical foundation for further research in reconfigurable robotic systems.

title: NeuroVision : EEG based image reconstruction using Deep-Learning
authors: Ajinkya Tamhankar, Aditya Rahulkar, Mohammed Vora, Milind Shende, Ashish Talekar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computation-Based Sensor Application (ICAIQSA)
abstract: Decoding human neural signals into a high-level, semantic format remains a grand challenge in neuroscience and artificial intelligence. While prior work has explored direct EEG-to- image reconstruction, progress has been hampered by training instabilities and a critical bottleneck in achieving semantic accuracy. This paper addresses this "semantic gap" by pivoting from direct image synthesis to EEG-to-text translation. We propose a novel, direct-pathway architecture that connects a domain-specific, hybrid CNN-Transformer EEG encoder directly to the multi-modal bridge (Q-Former) of a large, pre- trained Vision-Language Model (BLIP-2), bypassing its vision encoder. Our core contribution is a robust, decoupled two-stage training curriculum that ensures stability. Stage 1 trains the from-scratch EEG encoder components against a frozen BLIP model, driven by a powerful combined loss including a token- level Cross-Entropy objective. Stage 2 freezes the EEG encoder and fine-tunes the pre-trained Q-Former and language projection layers. We demonstrate that this strategy successfully overcomes common pitfalls like mode collapse and catastrophic forgetting. Our results show a consistent and significant decrease in validation loss, from an initial 29.37 down to a final best of 16.42. Qualitatively, the model evolves from generating random phrases to producing coherent sentences with emergent semantic relevance to the ground truth. This work provides a validated methodology and a powerful baseline for future research in high-fidelity neural decoding

title: Enhancing IoT Security Through Deep Learning Based Botnet Detection
authors: Russia S, Sumathi G, Prakash Kumar P S, Jabeen Begum S, Kavitha V, Murugan K
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices made automation and connectivity to take place across industries. But at the same time, it exposed major security vulnerabilities. Specifically, botnet attacks are particularly dangerous because they use IoT devices of vulnerable nature to establish Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks, data exfiltration, and malicious intent of a very high scale. Conventional methods in the shape of signature-based and statistical-based methods tend not to keep up with the changing characteristics in tactics in assaults and heterogeneity of the device behaviors. To address these obstacles, the current study proposes an improved IoT botnet detection model with deep learning and evaluated with the N-BaIoT dataset containing actual traffic of hacked computers with the Mirai malware installed as well as BASH Lite malware. The framework uses an attribute selection mechanism based on paying attention to the attributes to identify the most salient attributes and a CNN-DNN hybrid architecture to be used to make a high-classification effectiveness than recursion based architecture like LSTM. Experimental results depict high accuracy and high F1-score along with low false positive rates in different device categories. In terms of facilitating field implementation, the trained system integrates a Stream lit-based interface enabling real-time identification and IoT botnet traffic visualization. The lightweight deployment system includes support for the upload of CSV files, interaction-based analysis, and result visualization and is suitable for use in edge gateway and even in cloud-supported architecture. In conclusion, the system presented provides a scalable, dynamic, and user-focused solution geared towards enhancing IoT security in the context of the ongoing attacks by botnets.

title: Automating P-Card Reconciliation with UiPath and Microsoft Power Automate: A Transformational Approach to Streamlining Financial Operations
authors: Pullaiah Babu Alla
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computation-Based Sensor Application (ICAIQSA)
abstract: Modern business operations handle more financial transactions while facing stricter regulatory standards and higher performance demands. The process of P-card reconciliation stands as a vital organizational function that validates all company expenses for accuracy and compliance. The process of manual reconciliation requires excessive time and produces multiple errors while needing substantial resources. The paper introduces a combined intelligent automation system that uses UiPath Robotic Process Automation (RPA) together with Microsoft Power Automate and AI-based intelligent document processing for P-card reconciliation. The proposed system performs complete P-card reconciliation through automated steps, which start with transaction data extraction, followed by receipt acquisition and classification and GL code assignment and exception management, and audit record maintenance. The system architecture unites rule-based bots with machine learning (ML) for adaptive decision-making to achieve both operational efficiency and system resilience. The experimental testing shows that the system achieves better results in terms of processing speed and accuracy, and compliance preparedness. The paper demonstrates results while discussing practical applications and assessment methods, and potential development paths for automated financial operations.

title: Reuse of Retired Wind Generators for Mechanical Inertia Enhancement in Inverter-Based Grids
authors: Yukai Huang, Yihua Hu, Chong Qiu, Hao Wang, Songtao Huang, Jing Duan
abstract: The growing integration of inverter-based renewable energy sources has substantially reduced both system inertia and short-circuit capacity, intensifying stability challenges in modern power networks. Simultaneously, the global retirement of wind turbine generators raises sustainability concerns related to equipment disposal. This study introduces a dual-purpose approach that reuses retired wind turbine generators (RWTGs) as auxiliary grid support units. The assessment of the dynamic support potential of RWTGs is conducted, and a hysteresis-based frequency regulation scheme is developed to counteract secondary frequency dips. In addition, a coordinated voltage and reactive power control mechanism is proposed to enhance system resilience under fault conditions. The proposed strategies are evaluated on a modified section of the IEEE 39-bus system under high renewable penetration scenarios. Simulation results demonstrate that the integration of RWTGs contributes to frequency and voltage stability, offering a sustainable and effective path forward for grid support in low-inertia systems.

title: Analytical analysis of the transient behavior of PLL-VSC converter
authors: Jing Li, Huailiang Li, Lingzhi Zhu, Yibin Tao
abstract: The paper conducted a transient stability analysis of the Voltage Source Converter (VSC) converter. Firstly, a mathematical model for a single VSC converter connected to the grid was established based on the typical grid-connected structure of the VSC converter. Secondly, the regular perturbation model of the converter was derived based on this mathematical model, and the multi-scale solution of the converter was given using the perturbation method. Then, the analytical analysis of this model indicated that the converter has different forms of motion solutions, corresponding to the typical motion forms of the VSC after experiencing large disturbances. Finally, the simulation examples further verified the effectiveness of the proposed method and theoretical analysis.

title: Predictive Energy Management of Large Public Buildings Based on Double DQN—Under the Background of Intelligent Health Monitoring
authors: Junzhu Wu, Xinhang Lv, Lieke Lv
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: With the rapid development of the Internet of Things and big data technology, the energy management of large public buildings is facing the dual challenges of energy conservation and health and environmental protection. The traditional control strategy is difficult to adapt to the dynamic and changeable environment demand, while the predictive energy management provides a new idea for the realization of fine control. In this paper, Double DQN (Deep Q-Network) algorithm is applied to building energy management system, which can effectively alleviate the Q overestimation problem of traditional DQN algorithm and improve the stability of the strategy by decoupling the process of action selection and value evaluation. By constructing a state space containing indoor and outdoor environmental parameters, equipment status and health indicators, and designing a multi-objective reward function with energy consumption minimization and health constraint satisfaction as the core, the autonomous learning of energy dispatching strategy in complex dynamic environment is realized. The empirical results show that the proposed method is superior to the comparison algorithm in key indicators such as cumulative reward and comprehensive energy health index, and shows good robustness under extreme weather and load fluctuations, which provides a feasible path for intelligent energy management of large public buildings.

title: Construction and Analysis of System Operational Flexibility Demand Curves Based on Shadow Pricing Theory with the Participation of Source-Load Entities
authors: Boqiang Li, Yuwei Wang, Dongchen Li, Yunfei Chu, Shuguang Liu, Yong Wang, Zihao Zhang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: With the increasing proportion of renewable energy such as wind and solar energy, and the reduction of traditional units such as thermal power units, the power system is facing dual pressures of power supply guarantee and energy integration, and the problem of insufficient system flexibility regulation capacity has become increasingly prominent. To address this issue, this study constructs the benefit function of operational flexibility, uses the Shadow Pricing Theory to derive the relationship between the demand price and demand quantity of operational flexibility, establishes a supply-demand equilibrium analysis model that reflects the scarcity of flexibility through price signals, and ultimately constructs the operational flexibility demand curve. This provides a basis for Independent System Operator (ISO) to guide all source-load entities to participate in system flexibility regulation through price signals.

title: A Synergistic Machine Learning Framework for Type 2 Diabetes Prediction using Ancestry-Specific Genomic and Lifestyle Data
authors: Prajvinsharan. H, Karthikeyan. V, Nithish Gowtham. R, G. Ram Sankar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive framework for Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) prediction by integrating genomic and lifestyle data through machine learning. A Polygenic Risk Score (PRS) was developed using ancestry-specific DIAMANTE GWAS summary statistics for South Asian populations with Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) clumping. A Lifestyle Risk Score (LRS) was implemented based on FINDRISC. Both scores trained a Logistic Regression classifier on a simulated dataset, achieving AUC-ROC of 0.88. The pipeline demonstrates synergistic value of combining genetic predisposition with modifiable lifestyle factors for preventive medicine.

title: Research on Voltage Support Methods for AC-DC Hybrid Distribution Networks Under Voltage Sag Conditions
authors: Gaoming Li, Weinian Ouyang, Dong Liu, Jiaming Weng, Xiaoyong Cao, Fei Chen
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: With the rapid development of energy internet and the increasing penetration of renewable energy, AC-DC hybrid distribution networks have emerged as a critical infrastructure in modern power systems. However, voltage sags—a prevalent power quality issue—can induce abnormal shutdowns of sensitive equipment, leading to production interruptions and equipment damage, thereby severely threatening power supply reliability and system stability. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a collaborative control method integrating rapid fault identification and dynamic voltage support functions. First, an intelligent analysis model is constructed through multi-source feature extraction to achieve millisecond-level precise identification of voltage sag event types and fault sources. This model dynamically optimizes thresholds to balance sensitivity and anti-interference capabilities. Second, Combined Active and Reactive Power (PQ) Control with Voltage and Frequency (VF) Control, a storage-based dynamic voltage restorer (DVR) compensation system is designed, incorporating a PQ-VF flexible switching control strategy. The effectiveness of this approach is validated through MATLAB/Simulink simulations under multiple fault scenarios, demonstrating its capability to mitigate voltage deviations and enhance system resilience. This work addresses the critical need for adaptive voltage regulation in hybrid AC-DC grids, offering a novel solution to mitigate the impact of voltage sags on critical loads and ensure sustainable energy integration.

title: NODA-MMH: Certified Learning-Aided Nonlinear Control for Magnetically-Actuated Swarm Experiment Toward On-Orbit Proof
authors: Yuta Takahashi, Atsuki Ochi, Yoichi Tomioka, Shin-Ichiro Sakai
abstract: This study experimentally validates the principle of large-scale satellite swarm control through learning-aided magnetic field interactions generated by satellite-mounted magnetorquers. This actuation presents a promising solution for the long-term formation maintenance of multiple satellites and has primarily been demonstrated in ground-based testbeds for twosatellite position control. However, as the number of satellites increases beyond three, fundamental challenges coupled with the high nonlinearity arise: 1) nonholonomic constraints, 2) underactuation, 3) scalability, and 4) computational cost. Previous studies have shown that time-integrated current control theoretically solves these problems, where the average actuator outputs align with the desired command, and a learning-based technique further enhances their performance. Through multiple experiments, we validate critical aspects of learning-aided time-integrated current control: (1) enhanced controllability of the averaged system dynamics, with a theoretically guaranteed error bound, and (2) decentralized current management. We design two-axis coils and a ground-based experimental setup utilizing an air-bearing platform, enabling a mathematical replication of orbital dynamics. Based on the effectiveness of the learned interaction model, we introduce NODA-MMH (Neural power-Optimal Dipole Allocation for certified learned Model-based Magnetically swarm control Harness) for modelbased power-optimal swarm control. This study complements our tutorial paper on magnetically actuated swarms for the long-term formation maintenance problem.

title: Lightweight ECG Diagnosis on IoT Edge with FFT-Driven CNN Acceleration and Sparse Projection Learning
authors: Mahimai Don Bosco F P, Chitra E
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: The real time biomedical signal processing in Internet of Things (IOT) healthcare systems is limited by low computational power and energy of edge devices. This paper introduces a novel hardware-software co design for effective electrocardiogram (ECG) diagnosistics. This system use Compressive Sensing (CS) to reduce sampling overhead while retaining significant features required for diagnostics. A lightweight reconstruction scheme, Staged Projection Refining Multiple Orthogonal Matching Pursuit with Arithmetic-Efficient QRD (SPR-ROMP-AEQRD), is developed with the QR decomposition (QRD) operations, where the QRD sequences have been optimized through Unsigned Divide-and-Conquer Multiplier (UDCM). For disease classification, the architecture integrates a fast 1-D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized with Hermitian symmetry, which minimizes computational complexity and reduces the number of required multiplications. The combination of these two systems offers a highly effective method for specific signal diagnostic requirements, and the low power designs would be highly suitable for embedded and wearable medical systems. The proposed method achieves 200MHz frequency, 80.25 ps of reconstruction time, 1994mW of power, 44.27dB of RSNR<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, 65.95 dB of PSNR<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, 99.65% of accuracy, 98.85 % of precision, 99.94% of sensitivity, 97.21 % of specificity, 79 LUT, 82 FF, 8 IO, and 1.04 ns of delay.

title: Akshara: Contextual Transliteration of Brahmi Script Using Deep Learning
authors: Utsav Khairnar, Vivek Suvarna, Niranjan Pillai, Harshit Bhat, Sangeetha Selvan
publish: 2026 6th Biennial International Conference on Nascent Technologies in Engineering (ICNTE)
abstract: Recognizing Brahmi script is a big challenge in Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and a key part of understanding historical documents. Working with old scripts unfolds all kinds of problems: faded manuscripts, messy handwriting, and characters that don’t look anything like each other. The usual OCR tricks, like template matching or rule-based systems, just don’t cut it for Brahmi. But deep learning has changed the game. Now we have tools like Transformers, CNNs, and RNNs that push recognition much further than before. Still, there’s a catch: most modern models need a ton of labeled data, and for something as old as Brahmi, that’s hard to get. So, in this study, we built a hybrid deep learning model that combines CNNs and RNNs, and we leaned on data augmentation and transfer learning to boost accuracy without needing huge datasets. Along with explaining how we put together our dataset, trained the model, and measured its performance, this paper compares the already existing methods to solve the problem and our approach. Our goal is pretty straightforward: make Brahmi recognition easier and more accurate, and in the process, help preserve historical texts. That way, researchers in linguistics and archaeology can get to these materials without jumping through so many hoops

title: NeRF-based Visualization of 3D Cues Supporting Data-Driven Spacecraft Pose Estimation
authors: Antoine Legrand, Renaud Detry, Christophe De Vleeschouwer
abstract: On-orbit operations require the estimation of the relative 6 D pose, i.e., position and orientation, between a chaser spacecraft and its target. While data-driven spacecraft pose estimation methods have been developed, their adoption in real missions is hampered by the lack of understanding of their decision process. This paper presents a method to visualize the 3D visual cues on which a given pose estimator relies. For this purpose, we train a NeRF-based image generator using the gradients back-propagated through the pose estimation network. This enforces the generator to render the main 3D features exploited by the spacecraft pose estimation network. Experiments demonstrate that our method recovers the relevant 3D cues. Furthermore, they offer additional insights on the relationship between the pose estimation network supervision and its implicit representation of the target spacecraft.

title: Designing for Distributed Heterogeneous Modularity: On Software Architecture and Deployment of the MoonBots
authors: Elian Neppel, Shamistan Karimov, Ashutosh Mishra, Gustavo H. Diaz, Hazal Gozbasi, Shreya Santra, Kentaro Uno, Kazuya Yoshida
abstract: This paper presents the software architecture and deployment strategy behind the MoonBot platform: a modular space robotic system composed of heterogeneous components distributed across multiple computers, networks and ultimately celestial bodies. We introduce a principled approach to distributed, heterogeneous modularity, extending modular robotics beyond physical reconfiguration to software, communication and orchestration. We detail the architecture of our system that integrates component-based design, a data-oriented communication model using ROS2 and Zenoh, and a deployment orchestrator capable of managing complex multi-module assemblies. These abstractions enable dynamic reconfiguration, decentralized control, and seamless collaboration between numerous operators and modules. At the heart of this system lies our open-source Motion Stack software, validated by months of field deployment with self-assembling robots, inter-robot cooperation, and remote operation. Our architecture tackles the significant hurdles of modular robotics by significantly reducing integration and maintenance overhead, while remaining scalable and robust. Although developed with space in mind, we propose generalizable patterns for designing robotic systems that must scale across time, hardware, teams and operational environments.

title: Active power fluctuation mechanism and support strategy of sending-end system under commutation failure
authors: Xiang Hu, Fan Tang, Zhenlin Ni, Jiayu Liu, Yang Cao, Liangfei Tao
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: Aiming at the problem of active power transmission drop of DC system after commutation failure in sending-end system, a support strategy based on multi-type rectifier cooperative control is proposed. Firstly, by analyzing the power balance characteristics of the sending end system after commutation failure, the main influencing factors are revealed. The dynamic model of DC active power is established and the change trend of DC active power after commutation failure is analyzed. Then, the coordinated control strategy of line commutated converter HVDC (LCC-HVDC) and modular multilevel converter (MMC) is designed. Finally, the proposed active power support strategy is verified based on PSCAD / EMTDC.

title: Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm for Reliable Diagnosis of Benign vs. Malignant Breast Tumors
authors: G S Pradeep Ghantasala
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) represents a potential hybrid quantum-classical approach capable of addressing challenging optimization problems that arise in medicine from detailed patient data analysis. The present investigation is extending the scope of QAOA application to predicting and classifying breast tumors as either benign or malignant by efficiently locating decision boundaries in a high-dimensional feature space. Features most indicative of diagnosis will be encoded in quantum states, taken from reliable breast cancer datasets, so that the resulting QAOA-based classifier can be the one which will optimize the parameterized quantum circuits to result in the minimum classification error. Besides, these machine learning techniques will be benchmarked with QAOA to show where performance metrics have been improved - not only in accuracy but also in overall robustness and computational efficiency. QAOA can unveil the non-linearities intrinsic in tumor characteristics, thus, it can also achieve detection accuracies comparable to those of classical methods, while at the same time, it remains scalable with future developments in quantum hardware, as the results indicate. Thus it is the landmark case study on quantum optimization algorithms that are operationally validated for clinical decision support systems for early detection and improved prognostic outcomes for breast cancer patients.

title: A Fault Diagnosis Method With Temporal Constraints For Power System Based On Intuitionistic Fuzzy Colored Petri nets
authors: Zhenhang Yuan, Mi Zhao, Min Lu
abstract: Power system faults generate extensive alarm data with important temporal characteristics. Traditional diagnostic methods, however, often struggle to accurately identify faulty components under conditions of information distortion and time constraints. To address the limitations of existing Petri net based approaches, particularly their insufficient utilization of temporal constraints and limited adaptability to changes in system topology, this paper proposes a diagnosis method based on Intuitionistic Fuzzy Colored Petri Nets (IFCPN). The method incorporates a bidirectional temporal reasoning mechanism that dynamically selects an optimal time reference to validate alarm timestamps and initialize confidence levels within a hierarchical probabilistic framework. In addition, an automatic modeling approach using CPN Tools is developed to construct diagnostic models according to grid topology. Case studies on the IEEE 14-bus system demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in accurately identifying faults under complex conditions such as breaker refusal, protection misoperation, and missing alarms.

title: Reliability Evaluation of Power Systems Considering State of Safety of Battery Energy Storage
authors: Ke Wang, Bo Hu, Changzheng Shao, Kaigui Xie, Chengrong Lin, Zhihong Liu
abstract: Battery energy storage systems (BESS) could enhance the reliability of renewable energy-dominated power systems. However, they also expose power systems to the risks of catastrophic safety incidents like thermal runaway, fires, and explosions. To address this issue, this paper proposes a reliability evaluation model for power systems considering the state of safety (SoS) of BESS. First, a generalized Pareto distribution-based scenario generation method is presented to capture the variability of renewable energies. Then, the SoS constraint is derived based on the scenario and system operational parameters. Finally, the SoS constraint is included into the reliability evaluation model for power systems. Case studies based on a modified IEEE 30-bus system demonstrate that the proposed model can efficiently reflect the risks related to BESS and provide more accurate reliability evaluation results.

title: Traffic Sign Detection and Classification using Deep Learning Algorithms with Advanced Preprocessing Techniques
authors: A. Mary Jenifer, R. Balamanigandan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Autonomous vehicles and intelligent transportation systems must rapidly and accurately identify traffic signs. This work outlines a thorough pipeline for traffic sign recognition and classification. The model employs a pre-trained ResNet50 as its foundational architecture for feature extraction. The proposed approach utilises a multi-tiered preprocessing framework that integrates Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation (CLAHE), Fast Non-Local Means (FNLM) denoising, and normalisation to improve image quality and reduce the effects of noise and variable lighting conditions. Utilising CSV-based annotations, regions of interest (ROIs) corresponding to traffic signs are automatically extracted and incorporated into datasets for each category. This results in enhanced accuracy in testing and training. The dataset employed for training and evaluating the model consists of nine unique kinds of traffic indicators. They analyse its performance by analysing the F1-score, accuracy, precision, and recall. Multiple visualisations illustrate the effectiveness of the preprocessing pipeline and the model's capacity to detect signals in difficult conditions. The proposed technique effectively identifies traffic signs in real-world scenarios due to its high classification accuracy and practical efficacy.

title: Construction and Practice of a Payment Risk Early Warning System for Power Grid Engineering Based on Process Optimization
authors: Ke Xiong, You Wen, Liang Chen, Danni Chen, Zhiqing Song
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: Payment risk in power grid infrastructure projects directly affects project progress and financial stability. Traditional manual and rule-based methods suffer from inefficiency and delayed detection. This paper proposes a process-optimization-based early warning system for payment risk. A multi-level risk indicator system is developed, integrating stream processing, machine learning, and knowledge graph reasoning to enable real-time monitoring and intelligent inference across multi-source data. A case study conducted in a power grid company shows that the proposed system significantly improves accuracy, recall, and F1-score compared with baseline methods. Moreover, the average lead time of risk detection is reduced to less than two days, while maintaining second-level response time. These results demonstrate the practical value of the system in enhancing financial risk management and ensuring reliable execution of large-scale power grid projects.

title: Non Invasive Blood Glucose Level Monitoring
authors: Leelavathi R, Sharavana K L, Lavanya S, Pavithra R, Pooja S U, Parimala Devi M
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Research in Intelligent Computing in Engineering (RICE)
abstract: Diabetes is one of the most common non-communicable conditions globally, and it has a significant impact on quality of life and a heavy burden on healthcare systems. The necessity of regular blood glucose (BG) monitoring and the discomfort involved with invasive self-monitoring practices like finger pricking have driven the search for non-invasive approaches. Existing invasive practice is painful, requires expertise, and underscores the urgency for effective, easy-to-use alternatives. Here, we examine the design and assessment of a near-infrared-based non-invasive glucose trend estimation system. A MAX30102 photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor coupled with an Arduino UNO is employed to record PPG signals. The signals are then analyzed with computational algorithms to examine the potential of glucose trend estimation. Machine learning models are employed to correlate derived PPG features with glucose level estimations. The anticipated glucose patterns are shown in real-time on an LCD interface, offering a proof-of-concept for a non-invasive, consumer-friendly monitoring method.

title: Sequence Immittance Modeling and Stability Analysis for Hybrid Renewable Energy Station Consisting of GFL and GFM IBRs
authors: Haiyu Zhao, Xuerui He, Wei Yao, Hongyu Zhou, Shanyang Wei, Jinyu Wen
abstract: Large-scale new energy grid-connected systems, comprising heterogeneous generation units, stations, and other Inverter-based Resources (IBRs), introduce wide-frequency oscillation characteristics to the grid. The sequence immittance method is crucial for analyzing these oscillations but has mainly focused on unit-level or small systems. This paper proposes a sequence immittance modeling and stability analysis for hybrid renewable energy station consisting of grid-following (GFL) and grid-forming (GFM) IBRs to fill this gap. First, it reveals the multi-level coupling mechanism between units and stations and confirms the feasibility of sequential decoupling modeling. Second, based on the coupling characteristics and sequential decoupling relationships, it constructs a sequence immittance model for hybrid stations, accurately defining port division and proposing multi-level sequential analysis criteria. Finally, a topology integrating two GFM units and two GFL units into an external grid is built in MATLAB/Simulink, verifying the accuracy of the model and the efficiency of the criteria.

title: Adaptive Self-Healing Security Framework for DevSecOps using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Avdhesh Kumar Bhardwaj
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Integrating security into devOps (i.e. devSecOps) is an industry requirement under the current demand for rapid and secure delivery. However, static security checks or immovable rulebased systems are still too slow to catch the threats and misconfigurations which are usually present while building, integrating, or deploying. And advocate the development of self-healing, proactive security with the goal of assisting in devSecOps pipeline to stop using reinforcement learning (RL) in order to identify, mitigate and remediate security issues in real time. The RL is designed to monitor the pipeline state and to be able to learn the policies involving code, dependence changes, build logs, and underlying metadata in order to make autonomous decisions in order to remediate (i.e emergency policy in setting, isolating the environment, changing configuration, or patching the dependency). Also describe a design for an RLbased security layer compute a proxy system, design a training system, and design system evaluation metrics together with describing the obstacles which may be present. This work illustrate to the user, which is the goal of the study the manner in which the goal of the study devSecOps tools with.

title: Voiceprint Fault Identification Method of Transformer in New Energy Station Based on Feature Fusion and Improved Wave Search Algorithm
authors: Zhuohang Zhang, Bing Kang, Guili Ding, Xiaocui Yuan, Yiming Wan, Zongyao Wang
abstract: As the core equipment of electric energy collection, boost and grid connection, the transformer of new energy station may cause serious consequences such as station shutdown. Aiming at the time domain and spectrum feature significance of the fault voiceprint signal, this paper proposes a diagnosis method based on feature fusion and improved wave search algorithm. The MFCC and GFCC features are extracted by the Mel filter and the gamma-pass filter respectively. After linear combination, the improved Fisher ratio method is used to eliminate the low contribution components, and the regularization is introduced to prevent over-fitting, forming an optimized fusion feature MGCC. The MGCC features are input into the IWSA optimized support vector machine (SVM) model to achieve accurate classification of transformer fault voiceprints. Experiments show that the MGCC feature fusion extraction method and the IWSA-SVM model proposed in this paper have good recognition effects. The accuracy of fault recognition of new energy stations under small samples can reach 99.52 %, which is superior to other methods. It is an effective new energy station transformer voiceprint fault diagnosis method.

title: Comparative Performance Analysis of PI and PID Controllers for Linear and Nonlinear Conical Tank Systems with Enhanced Level Control Using I-PD Strategy
authors: Sindhu N P, R. Kiruba, E. Govinda Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: This work presents a comparative study of PI and PID controllers applied to both linear and non-linear tank level processes. A single tank level control process is considered to represent a linear system, while a conical tank illustrates nonlinear behavior arising from variations in cross sectional area with height. The process dynamics are determined through a step response system identification method and approximated by an order of one included with dead time (FOPDT) model. Controller tuning is performed using the Ziegler-Nichols (ZN) method. Simulation results are obtained for different set-point changes at various operating conditions. For the linear case, both PI and PID controllers provide satisfactory tracking performance. However, in the nonlinear conical tank, the PID controller tends to destabilize the system under negative step changes, highlighting the challenges of nonlinear dynamics and the importance of appropriate controller selection. This study suggested an I-PD controller for controlling a nonlinear conical tank, which is tuned using Ziegler-Nichols method, the controller tracked -5% to -20% set-point changes accurately. Compared to a conventional PI/PID controller, the I-PD provided smoother and more stable control. The simulated results show that proposed I-PD controller is better for nonlinear processes.

title: An Optimized Framework for Accurate Skin Cancer Classification
authors: Parthasarathy M, Manojkumar B, Pavithra M, Ajitha C
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Malignant dermatological conditions, including Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma, pose significant global health threats and require immediate and accurate diagnosis. Conventional convolutional neural networks lack the capability to extract high-level semantic information and complex local textures simultaneously, thus limiting their diagnostic performance. In this study, a hybrid deep learning model is proposed that combines a DenseNet201 backbone for global semantic feature extraction with a customized CNN module for local lesion texture analysis. To enhance robustness and generalization capabilities, the model integrates comprehensive data augmentation and class balancing techniques. The proposed model achieved an overall accuracy of 92% on a selected ISIC dataset subset focused on the three malignant skin conditions, while maintaining high values for precision, recall, F1-score, and sensitivity. Comparison studies have shown that the proposed hybrid model performs significantly better than the individual DenseNet and basic CNN models that do not combine the dual capabilities of spatial and semantic feature extraction. In conclusion, the proposed hybrid model is a promising automated diagnostic tool for the detection of malignant skin cancers.

title: Economic Analysis of Compressed Air Energy Storage Power Plants Based on Electricity Market Mechanisms
authors: Kun Wu, Yuqiang Tang, Qiang Liu, Da Xu, Chunyi Tang, Jinjun Cao, Na Zhong
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
