abstract: With the rapid development of China’s new-type power system and the increasing penetration of renewable energy, the economic performance of compressed air energy storage (CAES) has attracted growing attention, particularly under electricity market mechanisms. Based on the operating framework of China’s electricity spot market, this study constructs an operational constraint model for CAES power stations and systematically analyzes their revenue structure in spot market environments, including energy arbitrage revenue, ancillary service revenue, and capacity leasing income. Using the W CAES power station as a case study and incorporating 2023 spot market price data from Shandong, Shanxi, and Western Inner Mongolia, the economic performance of CAES is evaluated. Results indicate that the economic viability of CAES is strongly dependent on regional electricity price spreads. Western Inner Mongolia demonstrates the most favorable conditions, achieving a capital financial internal rate of return of 22.19%, significantly outperforming pumped-storage hydropower. Shandong ranks second, while Shanxi shows weaker performance. Sensitivity analysis reveals that project investment and generation output are the key factors influencing economic outcomes. This study provides theoretical references and decision-making guidance for the commercial operation of CAES in electricity markets.

title: Flexible Modeling and Acceleration for Power System Electromechanical Transient Simulation with Renewable Energy Sources and Hybrid HVDC Systems in STEPS
authors: Changgang Li, Dongbao Lv, Peng Wang, Yun Hang, Yujun Zhou, Jiawen Cao, Yutian Liu, Jiongcheng Yan
abstract: Numerical simulation is the key technique for power system analysis. Modern power systems exhibit high-dimensional and nonlinear characteristics, and the modeling and simulation technologies for complex power systems are facing new challenges. This paper introduces three core upgrades to the Simulation Toolkit for Electrical Power Systems (STEPS): 1) a unified hybrid high voltage direct current (HVDC) model supporting multi-terminal HVDC power flow; 2) modular renewable energy source (RES) models enabling flexible modeling; 3) an Automatic Time-Step Adjustment (ATSA) method for accelerated dynamic simulation. Case studies validate the accuracy of the models and the effect of the proposed simulation method.

title: A model for reducing octane loss during gasoline refining process
authors: Yannan Xie, Cheng Huang, Kang Ji
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: Octane number is an important indicator reflecting the combustion performance of gasoline. In order to meet the requirements for gasoline quality during the refining process of catalytic cracking gasoline, the dataset is preprocessed using methods such as the Laida criterion and the maximum and minimum limiting method; Reduce the number of candidate variables through recursive elimination and regression analysis, and then perform correlation analysis on the variables to determine the operating variables; Using parameter grid search as the training strategy, the single regression model method and Boosting method were used to train the main variable operation variables. The learning ability and overall performance of the octane number (RON) loss prediction model were evaluated based on three indicators: interpretability variance, root mean square error (RMSE), and Bad Case number. The Boosting learner was selected as the final model, and genetic algorithm was used to solve the optimal operation values or optimal operation interval ranges of the main variables in the model. This can verify the rationality of the optimal operation conditions of the main variables obtained by our model. The results show that this model can effectively predict and optimize the problem of octane number loss.

title: Coordinated Soft Open Points and Switching Strategy for Enhancing the Dispatchable Region in Resilient Distribution Networks
authors: Dechen Jiang, Chixin Xiao, Maoxin He, Yutong Zhang, Danny Sutanto, Z.Y. Dong
abstract: This paper proposed a coordinated control strategy that integrates soft open point (SOP) and tie-switch reconfiguration to enhance the dispatchable region under uncertain distributed renewable energy (DRE). The dispatchable area represents the system’s capability to accommodate disturbances from distributed renewable energy without violating operational constraints. A Sobol-sequence-based evolutionary algorithm framework is proposed for the co-optimization of DRE disturbance adjustment, SOP active/reactive power scheduling, and network switch configuration. The proposed model simultaneously maximizes the dispatchable area, minimizes SOP energy consumption, and reduces overall voltage deviation, thereby improving the resilience of the power grid. The optimization framework is validated through simulations conducted on a modified IEEE 33-node distribution system, which shows significant potential in enhancing the resilience of distribution grids with high DRE penetration.

title: On the Robust Unit Commitment under Renewable Uncertainty with Pairwise Convex Hull
authors: Qian Ma, Xu Du, Liang Xiao, Qiang Zhang, Tengmu Li, Yicheng Ju
abstract: The increasing penetration of intermittent renewable energy sources (RES) presents significant challenges to the economic and reliable operation of power systems. To address this, this paper proposes a two-stage robust unit commitment model to co-optimize generation schedules and real-time corrective actions. The core contributions are twofold. First, to realistically capture the complex spatial correlations between RES outputs without the prohibitive computational burden of the full convex hull, this paper applies the recently developed Pairwise Convex Hull (PW-CH) method to construct a high-fidelity uncertainty set. Second, to overcome the well-documented conservatism of traditional affine policies, we introduce a novel non-affine re-dispatch scheme. This policy more realistically allows generator outputs to saturate at their physical limits, providing a more flexible and cost-effective system response to uncertainty. To solve the proposed model, a nested column-and-constraint generation algorithm is suggested. Numerical studies on the 24-bus and 118-bus systems validate that the proposed approach, by synergistically combining the PW-CH set and the non-affine policy, significantly reducing total operational costs by 0.8578% and 1.3816% on the 118-bus test systems compared to traditional robust UC models.

title: Detection of Drug Resistant Tuberculosis in Chest Radiographs via Mediastinum Analysis
authors: Sukanta Kumar Tulo, Pramod Martha
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Diagnosis of Tuberculosis (TB) using Chest Radiographs (CXRs) is non-trivial, and identifying drug-sensitive and resistant TB is even more challenging. Analysis of mediastinum in CXRs could provide significant information for detecting TB conditions. The aim of this study is to develop a computerized framework to detect Extensively Drug Resistant (XDR), Multi Drug Resistant (MDR), and Drug Sensitive (DS) TB in CXRs using analysis of mediastinum. The images are acquired from a publicly available database and are subjected to edge-aware contrast enhancement to strengthen local edge contrast. The image projection profiling technique is employed to identify the mediastinal and thoracic borders. Measures that quantify the structural variations are extracted from the profile. Further, ratiometric indices are explored to assess the anatomical differences. Machine learning techniques, including support vector machine, linear discriminant analysis, and multilayer perceptron, are employed for binary classification of XDR, MDR and DS TB. The results indicate that the projection profiling technique can detect mediastinum and lung borders. Features extracted from the profile are found to characterize alterations in TB conditions. The mediastinal width ratio provided a mean difference of 43.9%, 31.6% and 15.4% between XDR vs DS, XDR vs MDR, and MDR vs DS, respectively. In addition, the mediastinal thoracic ratio achieved a mean difference of 35.3%, 0.1% and 43.8%, respectively. Moreover, the features between XDR and DS are statistically significant. Furthermore, accuracy of 79.5%, 77.5%, and 72.0%, and area under the curve of 84.6%, 81.0%, and 78.6% are obtained to differentiate XDR vs DS, MDR vs DS, and XDR vs MDR, respectively. This shows better differentiation between XDR and DS, with the least performance for XDR vs MDR, which aligns with the literature. Thus, it is evident that the proposed methodology could be utilized to identify drug-resistant TB conditions.

title: Research on Three-Phase Power Mutual Assistance Control Strategy for Grid-Connected Converter
authors: Jiazheng Zhu, Yilin Pan, Weidong Wang, Jiawei Ma, Yi Zhang, Sheng Xu
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: Grid-connected converters are suitable for applications involving the integration of large-scale distributed power sources. Currently, with the development of single-phase distributed power sources, distribution networks are prone to localized three-phase power imbalance. Three-phase imbalance may lead to more severe hazards, making it crucial for three-phase grid-connected converters to achieve mutual compensation and rational distribution of three-phase power. This paper proposes a three-phase power mutual compensation control strategy for grid-connected converters based on positive and negative zero-sequence component superposition control. Without requiring additional equipment or sensors, this approach achieves decoupled control of three-phase grid-connected currents by incorporating negative-sequence and zero-sequence current control loops at the algorithmic level into existing grid-connected converters, thereby enabling three-phase power mutual compensation. The feasibility and effectiveness of this control strategy were ultimately validated through MATLAB/Simulink simulation software and real-machine testing.

title: Day-ahead Coordinated Interaction Method for Steel Clusters and Renewable Energy
authors: Yi Zhang, Shuai Fan, Yan Meng, Moyan Zhu, Yong Wang, Guangyu He
abstract: The steel industry has emerged as a crucial solution for promoting renewable energy accommodation and alleviating power grid regulation challenges with its massive load base and high regulation potential. However, the uncertain nature of both RE generation and steel loads poses significant obstacles to their effective coordination. This paper proposes a collaborative interaction framework between steel cluster loads and renewable energy systems under uncertainty. First, the concept of day-ahead customer directrix load is introduced as a bridge to quantify RE regulation demands and provide guidance targets for flexible steel load resources. Subsequently, a day-ahead robust optimization scheduling model for steel parks is developed to address the uncertainties of both RE and loads by constructing worst-case scenarios. The model is efficiently solved using a column-and-constraint generation algorithm. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method enables steel parks to effectively coordinate various internal resources, significantly enhancing renewable energy accommodation while maintaining operational reliability under uncertainty. The results validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in facilitating steel-renewable energy coordination and provide practical insights for industrial demand response in renewable energy integration.

title: State-space Variation Induced Instability During Mode Switching in Multi-source DC Microgrid
authors: Shanshan Jiang, Zelin Sun, Jiankun Zhang, Hua Geng
abstract: DC bus signaling strategy is a common solution for achieving multi-mode autonomous operation in multi-source DC microgrids. This strategy often results in two different types of mode switching, which are classified based on whether state-space variation occurs before and after the switch. This paper investigates the voltage instability caused by state-space variation during mode switching of multi-source DC microgrid. Firstly, this paper analyzes how the dynamic behavior of the mode switching influences system stability and provides a state-space switching stability analysis method. The switching process may cause the system state to deviate from the region of attraction (ROA), thereby triggering system instability. Additionally, the mechanism of state-space variation induced instability during mode switching is revealed: mode switching involves mutual coupling among the adjustment processes of different source control strategies. This coupling exacerbates power imbalance, ultimately leading to system instability. To improve stability, an extended mode scheduling control method has been proposed by introducing transition modes to address the power imbalances during the mode switching. Finally, the experimental results verify the analytical mechanism and control method.

title: IConSCEPT 2025 TOC
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)

title: Design of Conformal Reconfigurable Reflectarray Antenna Based on Flexible Material
authors: Dongyang Lu, Chi Zhang, Zehua Wang, Ruozhou Li, Jing Yan, Ying Yu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this paper, a 10×10 conformal reflectarray antenna (RA) operating at 9 GHz is designed and fabricated based on flexible materials. By controlling the operating state of the PIN diode in each element to change the phase distribution, the designed RA can realize beam-scanning within the range of ±45°. Furthermore, the elements can provide more accurate conformal compensation phase for RA at different bending degrees to minimize the deterioration of the radiation characteristics due to the array bending. The RA prototype made of flexible polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and printed liquid metal was mounted on acrylic supports with different bending degrees during the measurements to investigate the conformal radiation characteristics. The peak gain of the conformal RA is up to 16.13 dBi.

title: Robust Seismic Denoising Framework: A GAN-Based Approach with Multi-Scale Feature Fusion and Signal Preservation
authors: Kun Li, Jin Yuan, Wei Ge, Xingyao Yin, Zhaoyun Zong
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Seismic data acquisition is often affected by complex field conditions and equipment precision limitations, leading to the generation of multiple types of noise that reduce the accuracy of reservoir inversion and geological interpretation. Deep learning techniques have begun to be applied in seismic data denoising due to their efficient feature learning capabilities. However, the complex characteristics of seismic noise pose challenges for conventional convolutional neural networks in learning diverse noise features and achieving effective feature fusion. We propose a novel seismic data denoising methodology based on Generative Adversarial Networks (SD-GAN). The established denoising framework achieves noise distribution prediction through adversarial learning, while integrating Deep Residual Convolutional Networks (DnCNN) with attention mechanisms to precisely learn diverse noise distributions and enable feature fusion. The DnCNN incorporates deformable convolutions that dynamically adjust convolution kernel shapes to adapt to different seismic signal characteristics, enhancing the network's modeling capability for complex geological structures. To ensure effective frequency-domain denoising performance, an adaptive frequency-domain loss term is introduced in the loss function to maximize the preservation of seismic signal characteristics. Validation through synthetic seismic data experiments and real seismic data applications demonstrates that this method exhibits excellent robustness and feasibility across various denoising scenarios.

title: Investigating the Dual-Look Capability of WindSat for Passive Microwave Snow Mapping
authors: Lina Zschenderlein, Richard Kelly, Kari Luojus
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Spaceborne passive microwave data are known to underestimate both snow cover extent (SCE) and snow water equivalent (SWE). Sensor viewing geometry and scan azimuth angle, in particular, have not been investigated thus far regarding their impact on mapping snow on the ground. In this study, we apply a dry snow detection algorithm to WindSat Level 1C brightness temperatures of 18.7 and 37.0 GHz for the Northern Hemisphere winter season of 2016–2017. WindSat provides near-instantaneous dual-look observations for part of its conical scan, and comparing fore- against aft-looking data facilitates the analysis of azimuth angle effects. More snow is consistently detected—especially during the shoulder seasons—by south-looking observations which view predominantly northfacing slopes with snow that is typically drier and deeper than south-facing snow. When using early-morning descending passes to map weekly SCE over Eurasia, fore and aft (south- and northlooking) estimates differ on average by 5.9%, 2.1%, and 4.9% from October to December, January to March, and April to June, respectively. Detected snow agrees with snow maps from the Interactive Multisensor Snow and Ice Mapping System by at least 97%. Combining detected snow from both looks through superimposition further increases the mapped SCE. We note that relief and spillover effects are apparent for mountain regions and mixed land-water pixels, and will likely affect SWE retrievals. Therefore, further research is required to fully quantify these observational differences. Nevertheless, dual-look brightness temperatures are a promising means to complement, if not improve, spaceborne estimates of snow parameters. Such dual-look effects are particularly relevant for high-spatial-resolution observations and global coverage, as anticipated for the Copernicus Imaging Microwave Radiometer.

title: Design and Optimization of a Compact Metacarpophalangeal Joint Angle Sensor For Robotic Finger
authors: Yuanfei Zhang, Tianqi Jiang, Jiahao Zhang, Wangyang Li, Fenglei Ni, Hong Liu
abstract: The metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint of the dexterous robotic finger exhibits two-degree-of-freedom(2-DOF) rotation, thus conventional designs require two discrete joint angle sensors for joint position control. This multi-sensor configuration restricts compactness and integration in dexterous robotic finger design. By embedding a miniature permanent magnet in the joint mechanism, compact measurement of 2-DOF rotation angles is achievable. Using a single three-axis Hall-Effect sensor, a mapping between 3D magnetic flux distribution and the joint's 2-DOF angles is established, thereby enabling determination of the joint angle without external references.To realize high-sensitivity response, sensor's geometry parameters were optimized. Subsequently, an artificial neural network (ANN) was trained to perform inverse mapping from the measured 3D magnetic flux intensity back to the joint angles. Experimental validation confirms that this integrated sensor achieves a maximum absolute joint angle sensing error of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.63^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>. This method offers a promising, compact solution for high-precision MCP joint angle sensing.

title: Possible Self-Arresting Screening Current Stress Damage in a REBCO Test Coil at 42.6 T in 31 T Background Field
authors: Jonathan Lee, Jeseok Bang, Garfield Murphy, Griffin Bradford, Cade Watson, Kwangmin Kim, Jan Jaroszynski, Rastislav Ries, Aixia Xu, Anatolii Polyanskii, Najib Cheggour, David Larbalestier
abstract: REBCO coated conductors (CCs) have now been optimized for current-carrying capacity in high magnetic fields. Production-length CCs may carry more than 1500 A at 4.2 K/30 T in the B//ab configuration. Such high Ic is essential for the construction of new generations of ultra-high field (B > 40 T) magnets. However, high Ic also increases screening current stresses (SCS) and makes these contemporary tapes vulnerable to mechanical damage during magnet operation. To study this vulnerability, we constructed and tested Little Big Coil #6 (LBC6), one of a series of standardized ultra-high field insert coils operated in a 31 T resistive background field at the National High Magnetic Field Laboratory (NHMFL). LBC6 used contemporary conductor with triple the Ic of the earlier LBCs, so we hypothe sized that the coil would be destroyed by SCS during operation. Instead, LBC6 survived multiple quenches from more than 42 T and repeated cycling from 31 T to 41 T. Postmortem analysis revealed plastic deformation throughout the winding pack, but the damage to the REBCO layer was confined to one conductor edge. We propose that this coil exhibited a self-arresting damage mechanism: during the initial energization and quench, SCS damage sufficiently narrowed the superconducting filament width (thus reducing Ic) such that no further SCS damage occurred during subsequent operation. Possible implications for ultra-high field magnet design are discussed.

title: Coupling Structural Descriptors with a Novel Semantic Graph Matching Approach for LiDAR Loop Detection
authors: Yujie Tang, Meiling Wang, Sibo Zuo, Chengxi Yang, Jinhao Jiang, Xieyuanli Chen, Yufeng Yue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Outdoor loop closure detection is essential for correcting odometry drift and constructing a globally consistent map. Semantic-graph-based approaches effectively model object-level topology and achieve strong loop closure performance; however, their effectiveness degrades in background-dominated scenes with few distinctive objects, and establishing accurate injective node correspondences remains challenging. In contrast, structural descriptor methods, though offering stronger environmental generality through spatial-distribution modeling, remain susceptible to LiDAR noise and the discriminative power of point-level features. These limitations motivate the need for a more robust method that combines adaptability with enhanced descriptive power. We propose a novel loop-closure detection framework, SAGE, that integrates highly adaptable point-cloud shape-distribution features and generally reliable semantic graph topology, adaptively combining their similarity measures to improve detection performance. Specifically, we design a semantic graph matching module with dual constraints, local graph feature consistency and global spatial consistency, to achieve more accurate injective node correspondences. In addition, we extract point-cloud shape-distribution features and introduce a fusion mechanism that integrates them with the semantic graph module, assessing reliability and adaptively weighting their contributions. Extensive loop closure detection and pose estimation experiments on various datasets demonstrate that SAGE achieves superior performance over strong baselines. We provide the code at https://github.com/SAGE-11/SAGE.

title: Avalanche Failure Mechanisms of 1200-V/40-A 4H-SiC Junction Barrier Schottky Diodes with Various Epilayer Defects
authors: Jinlan Li, Yan Xu, Huaren Sheng, Quanzhi Chen, Ling Dong, Yiming Shi, Yulin Wang, Liming Zhou, Yuan Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: This study systematically investigates the degradation mechanisms of 1200-V/40-A 4H-SiC junction barrier schottky (JBS) diodes under repetitive avalanche stress. Experimental results reveal a universal rise-then-fall evolution in breakdown voltage, governed by competing effects between interface charge accumulation and material degradation. It is found that failure mode strongly depends on avalanche energy: high-energy pulses induce catastrophic package failure through pin detachment, while low-energy stress causes gradual degradation primarily via transformation of basal plane dislocations, identified as the most critical defects. The study establishes that avalanche robustness constitutes a system-level property, necessitating co-optimization of epitaxial quality, interface stability, and package resilience.

title: DFS-TSPML: Distribution Feature Screening and Three-Stage Physical Information Meta-Learning for Multi-Condition Tool Wear Monitoring
authors: Yuncong Lei, Changgen Li, Zhichao You, Ao Cao, Liang Guo, Hongli Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Under varying conditions, the stage evolution of tool wear exhibits disparate characteristics, with pronounced differences in wear rate and transition points across stages. This variability renders real-time, high-precision measurement and unified monitoring of tool wear exceptionally challenging. To address this issue, a data-distribution-based feature screening and three-stage physics-informed meta-learning are proposed in this paper for multi-condition tool wear unified monitoring. First, high-dimensional data acquired during the cutting process are exploited to construct a time–frequency feature matrix. Information criteria are employed to identify the distribution of tool wear increments. And features whose exhibit maximal multidimensional similarity to both the wear state and its distribution are selected via FSI. Then, a novel three-stage wear physical model is embedded into the monitoring model. These physics-based constraints, together with the measured data, jointly restrict the solution space. An improved meta-optimizer is subsequently adopted to distill the domain-invariant representations shared across multi-conditions. Finally, the monitoring model is rapidly adapted to a new condition with only a handful of samples, enabling real-time and accurate tool wear monitoring. A multi-condition wear experiment conducted on indexable CNC milling inserts demonstrated that, compared with state-of-the-art methods, the proposed method exhibits markedly higher precision, stability, and adaptability in new conditions.

title: On the Service Provisioning and Reconfiguration for Asymmetric Traffic in Drop-and-Continue Optical Networks based on P2MP-TRXs
authors: Ruoxing Li, Xiaoliang Chen, Fei Yang, Meihan Wu, Nelson L. S. Da Fonseca, Zuqing Zhu
abstract: Driven by emerging distributed computing and cloud-edge collaborative applications, metro and regional networks have experienced continuous surges in hub-and-spoke (H&S) traffic, posing great challenges to existing point-to-point network infrastructures. While coherent point-to-multipoint optical transceivers (P2MP-TRXs) present a more cost-effective solution for accommodating H&S traffic, existing P2MP networking paradigms fail to address the dynamic and asymmetric nature of traffic prevalent in such networks, and consequently, could lead to subpar resource utilization. In this paper, we fill this gap by investigating dynamic asymmetric subcarrier allocation and reconfiguration in drop-and-continue (D&C) optical networks. In particular, our approach aims at minimizing the operational cost of service provisioning by invoking reactive and coordinated connection consolidation as a remedy for the inability to accommodate traffic demands with in-service or newly activated P2MP-TRXs. We first devise an integer linear programming (ILP) model to solve the multi-objective optimization problem exactly. As the problem is proved to be NP-hard, we further develop a column generation (CG)-based approximation algorithm that can offer guaranteed optimality bounds within reasonable time, as well as a polynomial-time heuristic framework employing priority-queue-based progressive search. Extensive simulations verify the effectiveness of our proposal, demonstrating up to 43.3% reduction in bandwidth blocking ratio and 2.9% improvement in spectrum utilization compared with the state of the art.

title: ETEP 2025 Author Information Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)

title: Flexible and Privacy-Preserving Access Control Framework for Decentralized Identity Systems
authors: Bin Xie, Rui Song, Zecheng Li, Xiaotie Deng, Bin Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Decentralized identity systems have emerged as a transformative paradigm, granting users unprecedented data sovereignty and privacy-preserving capabilities, fueling critical innovations in Web3 ecosystems. However, these systems primarily serve as identity-layer solutions, forcing verifiers to design special cryptographic protocols for access control deployment, which is an error-prone and expert-dependent process. Moreover, existing approaches fail to effectively combat credential fraud (e.g., credential theft and revoked credential reuse) without compromising privacy guarantees. This paper presents FRAC (Flexible Fraud-Resistant Access Control), an efficient decentralized access control framework that achieves two paradigm shifts: 1) Streamlined access control deployment: a logic-centric paradigm encodes access criteria through declarative verification rules, eliminating manual cryptographic protocol design while enabling instant verifier onboarding and efficient presentation generation; 2) Provable fraud resistance: a format-agnostic defensive mechanism based on Merkle trees prevents malicious credential use, requiring only lightweight hash operations and signature verification instead of computation-intensive operations. We conduct rigorous security analysis based on universally composable security and evaluate the performance, demonstrating FRAC’s security and efficiency.

title: ETEP 2025 Breaker Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)

title: Effect of Incident Angle on Heavy-Ion Irradiation Damage in p-GaN Gate HEMTs
authors: Yuxiong Xue, Weixiang Zhou, Weitao Ding, Bai Ding, Dongping Yang, Chengan Wan, Rui Chen, Zicai Shen, Rongxing Cao
abstract: In this study, we investigated the effect of ion incident angle on the leakage degradation and the electrical performance of p-GaN gate high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) under Taion irradiation. The results showed that the leakage degradation was substantially mitigated as the incident angle increased. Technology Computer Aided Design (TCAD) simulations revealed that under deflected incidence, the electron concentration induced by ionization reduced, resulting in a decrease in the total charge collected at the drain. Furthermore, when the ions were incident towards the drain at a deflected angle, the drain current (ID) duration was shortened due to the channel between the drain and source remaining off. TCAD results also showed that the thermal effect induced by heavy ions weakened under deflected incidence, which suppressed the increase of the drain-source leakage current (IDSS). Moreover, GEometry ANd Tracking 4 (Geant4) simulations demonstrated that with increasing incident angle, the non-ionizing energy loss (NIEL) deposited in the device rose, inducing more acceptor-type traps and consequently causing a progressive increase in dynamic on-resistance (RON,DYN). This study systematically reveals the correlation between heavy-ion damage and incident angle in p-GaN gate HEMTs for the first time, and provides a theoretical basis for the application of GaN devices in aerospace.

title: RIS-Assisted Extremely-High-Frequency Wireless Communication System: Joint Resource Optimization to Enhance Spectral Efficiency
authors: Xu Ma, Haijun Zhang, Wenxuan Xu, Zijun Wu, Yuzheng Ren, Victor C.M. Leung
abstract: Extremely-high-frequency (EHF) wireless communication is recognized as a key technology for future mobile networks, offering low-latency, high-reliability services for real time control applications, with considerable potential for a wide range of uses. EHF links, which utilize the Terahertz (THz) band, are highly susceptible to obstructions, leading to interruptions that undermine system reliability. Using reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) to assist EHF communication is an effective solution to mitigate these challenges. The RIS-assisted EHF downlink communication network model is established in this paper. Based on an analysis of the EHF band, specifically the THz regime, energy loss characteristics, a channel model is developed. Additionally, we construct an optimization model aimed at enhancing the system's weighted spectral efficiency. This model serves as the foundation for a newly designed joint optimization algorithm. The algorithm's purpose is to find the optimal values for the decoding order, beamforming, power allocation factors, RIS element coefficients, and deployment positions. Validation through simulation demonstrates that the proposed approach significantly boosts the system's spectral efficiency, outperforming other baseline strategies within EHF wireless networks.

title: ACSAM: Accuracy-configurable Segmentation-based Approximate Multiplier for Error-resilient Edge-AI Applications
authors: Vasundhara Trivedi, Santosh Kumar Vishvakarma
abstract: Approximate computing has introduced a paradigm shift in hardware-optimized implementation of Edge-AI applications by balancing accuracy and area constraints simultaneously. In this work, a hardware-efficient, segmentation-based 16-bit approximate multiplier, ACSAM is presented for resource-constrained applications with error tolerance capabilities. The proposed 16-bit multiplier integrates various combinations of conventional and proposed 8-bit approximate multipliers with unique shifting and rounding strategies. The proposed multiplier ACSAM achieved upto 18.9% improvement in LUT utilisation on FPGA and upto 2.85× reduction in power and upto 12.27% reduction in area for ASIC implementation on 65nm technology node compared to the state-of-the-art works. ACSAM is validated using an image-blurring application to demonstrate its suitability for DSP and image-processing tasks. Additionally, FPGA and ASIC evaluations confirm its adaptability across diverse implementation requirements.

title: Fractional Range Migration Compensation and Doppler Ambiguity Resolution for Pulse Doppler Radars Using Variable Chirp Rate Signals and Pulse Compression with Partial Reference Signals
authors: Takafumi Nagano, Tomonori Fukuta, Koji Nishimura, Hiroyuki Hashiguchi
abstract: We present a method that simultaneously addresses range migration compensation and Doppler ambiguity resolution for fast-moving targets in pulse Doppler radars. Although pulse Doppler radars are used for high-sensitivity and high-resolution target detection, problems arise when target velocities exceed the Nyquist limit. These problems include a decrease in detection probability due to range migration loss and Doppler ambiguity caused by the folding of the Doppler velocity. Several methods have been proposed to tackle these problems. However, achieving simultaneous resolution remains challenging due to high computational demands and interference between methods in some cases. Our approach integrates a range migration compensation method that uses variable chirp rate signals with a velocity estimation method that employs pulse compression using partial reference signals (i.e., portions of the reference signal) to detect the intra-pulse Doppler-shift frequency difference. However, these methods do not yield satisfactory results when simply integrated; therefore, modifications are applied to each method prior to their combined use. Numerical simulations demonstrate that our method effectively mitigates the decrease in detection probability while estimating target velocities beyond the Nyquist limit.

title: ATC-FL: Adaptive Thompson Sampling for Efficient Clustering in Federated Learning
authors: Shivani Sanjay Kolekar, Jisoo Shin, David J. Richter, Kyungbaek Kim
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across decentralized clients without sharing raw data. To improve scalability and address client heterogeneity, clustering-based FL methods group similar clients to train specialized models. However, under realistic network constraints, existing approaches often incur high communication overhead or fail to adapt to dynamic conditions. We propose ATC-FL (Adaptive Thompson-sampling based Clustering for Federated Learning), a framework that adaptively selects and maintains client clusters to balance communication cost and model performance. ATC-FL formulates cluster count selection as a multi-armed bandit problem, using fixed-budget Thompson sampling to explore candidate configurations. At each round, client updates are embedded and clustered via K-means, with the Davies-Bouldin Index (DBI) serving as a low-overhead reward signal. A re-clustering scheduler adapts to client participation dynamics while bounding server computation. We evaluate ATC-FL on Human Activity Recognition and CIFAR-10 tasks over two emulated backbone-style network topologies (NSFNET and fat-tree) under non-IID data distributions, and additionally on a MIMIC-III medical benchmark to assess cross-domain generalization. We further analyze how ATC-FL’s convergence behavior and communication cost to reach a target accuracy vary with the severity of data heterogeneity. Compared to resource-aware federated clustering methods, ATC-FL achieves faster convergence, lower bandwidth usage, and reduced delay. These results suggest that adaptive clustering and network-aware control can improve efficiency while maintaining stable performance in realistic federated deployments.

title: Dynamics and High-Dimensional Chaotic Signal Generation in a Dual-Loop Optoelectronic Oscillator
authors: Jian Wang, Hao Su, Yufeng Zhang, Jingbo Ma, Hongqian Mu, Jianyong Zhang, Muguang Wang
abstract: With strong unpredictability, high-dimensional microwave chaotic signals are significant for secure communication. However, conventional electronic techniques face challenges in generating high-dimensional chaotic signal with large bandwidth. In this paper, a dual-loop optoelectronic oscillator with cascaded phase modulators is proposed for high-dimensional microwave chaotic signal generation. In the system, phase modulation is converted into nonlinear intensity modulation by using a Mach-Zehnder interferometer to achieve chaotic oscillation. The dual-loop structure causes numerous characteristic frequencies, enhancing high-dimensional dynamics. Its mathematical model based on the Ikeda equation is established and used for dynamics analysis. An experimental comparison demonstrates that the proposed system generates high-dimensional chaotic signal with a broadband spectrum and low time-delay signature. The nonlinear dynamics are further investigated through numerical simulations, including bifurcation route and dynamics performances, which demonstrate the high-dimensional dynamical characteristics and enhanced unpredictability of the generated chaotic signal.

title: An Improved Shunt Capacitor-Commutated Converter and Control Methodology for HVDC Transmission System
authors: Shengwei Wang, Xiaoling Xiong, Dingteng Feng, Zixun Ding, Hao Li, Chengyong Zhao
abstract: Although the Shunt Capacitor-Commutated Converter (SCCC) can reduce reactive compensation equipment costs and mitigate the risk of commutation failure of the line-commutated converter (LCC), its effectiveness remains limited. Meanwhile, the lack of independent active/reactive power regulation constrains system flexibility. To address these issues, this paper proposes an Improved Shunt Capacitor-Commutated Converter (ISCCC) topology and the corresponding control methods, which introduce an Integrated Gate-Commutated Thyristor (IGCT) into the SCCC structure. First, a novel modulation strategy for ISCCC is proposed, which enables dual control freedoms via firing angle and auxiliary angle manipulation. Then, a mathematical model of the ISCCC is formulated, and the relationships between the active/reactive power and the two control angles are derived. To achieve independent power control, a novel control strategy is proposed based on the mathematical model. In addition, to mitigate commutation failure, the mathematical relationship between the extinction angle and the auxiliary angle is derived, and an improved extinction angle control strategy is further presented. Subsequently, hardware-in-the-loop results confirm that the new controls enable independent power regulation and enhance commutation failure resilience. Finally, a failure model of the auxiliary arm is established; meanwhile, an economic comparison of the topologies, along with loss and thermal analyses, validates the feasibility of the proposed design.

title: Detecting Stable Cross-Impact Patterns in Bivariate Time Series
authors: Gennady Andrienko, Natalia Andrienko, Maram Akila, Bahavathy Kathirgamanathan, Miguel Ponce-de-Leon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: This paper presents a visual analytics workflow for detecting stable cross-impact patterns in time series pairs. A sliding window technique computes multiple impact measures, including a novel Kendall's tau variant that tolerates minor fluctuations. Evaluating these measures across various time lags reveals dynamic relationships between time series. An interactive Ikat plot facilitates the exploration of impact distributions, helping identify intervals where specific cross-impacts remain stable (e.g., trends in one series followed by similar or opposite trends in another after a lag). These intervals are extracted as events, whose temporal (and, when applicable, spatial) distributions can be analyzed to uncover broader patterns across multiple time series pairs and over extended time spans. This includes identifying co-occurring cross-impacts and variations in cross-impact presence or type across different periods and data subsets. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the framework's ability to isolate robust patterns, providing a scalable and interpretable approach to analyzing complex temporal dynamics.

title: Magnetic Target Positioning Based on Differential Errors Compensation
authors: Xuning Chen, Jianying Zheng, Yong Cui, Qinglei Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: This paper investigates the accurate positioning problem of a magnetic target in full space. The commonly used positioning approaches rely on the classical scalar triangulation and ranging (STAR) method based on the magnetic gradient tensor (MGT). In these STAR-based approaches, the MGT is approximated by the sensor differentials, which introduces ineliminable errors into the positioning results. To improve the localization accuracy, an iterative differential errors compensation positioning (DECP) method is proposed in this paper. The DECP method establishes the error compensation models for the MGT and the gradient of the tensor invariant, and iteratively updates the target’s position and magnetic moment to approach the true values. The proposed method is evaluated in both simulations and real-world experiments. The simulation results show that the positioning errors of the DECP method is much smaller than those of four STAR-based benchmark methods, and it is able to improve the positioning accuracy by 42.4% − 86.5% in different field experiments, which demonstrates the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method.

title: Countermeasures for Ecological Environment Restoration of Pumped Storage Power Stations in Northern Stone Mountain Areas
authors: Xiaofeng Chen, Qian Hong, Xuan Zhao, Yixuan Zong, Jia Hu, Chongqing Wang, Xiaoxiao Ma
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: To achieve regional adaptability and operational synergy in the ecological restoration of pumped-storage power stations in the Beishishan area, a comprehensive ecological restoration model was constructed, comprising "1 core objective + 5 sub-modules + 3 support systems," covering five core modules: vegetation ecological restoration, soil and water conservation, and hydrological and ecological regulation. A combination of literature review, field monitoring, and numerical simulation was employed, and the model was validated through a three-dimensional system of "numerical simulation + pilot verification + expert review." Parameter calibration and optimization improved the model's regional adaptability. Pilot data show that the prediction accuracy of six core ecological indicators exceeds 88%, and the vegetation restoration efficiency and ecosystem service value are significantly better than traditional schemes, with controllable economic costs and outstanding long-term benefits. This model provides a regional restoration paradigm for similar mountain pumped-storage power stations and has significant engineering promotion value.

title: A Joint Model-driven and Data-driven Distribution Network Line Fault Prediction Method under Typhoon
authors: Yu Li, Yiheng Bian, Gengfeng Li
abstract: In recent years, extreme weather events such as typhoons have severely affected the safety and stability of the grid. In order to accurately assess line fault risks under typhoon, this paper proposes a joint model-driven and data-driven method integrating weather forecasts, geographic data and grid parameters. First, fault mechanisms are analyzed to identify key factors associated with faults. Then, plenty of pseudo-labeled samples are generated using a logistic regression to solve the lack of line fault data. Finally, a one-dimensional convolutional neural network and graph sampling aggregation algorithm (1D CNN-GraphSAGE) is trained to predict weather lines are faulty or not and some labeled samples are used to evaluate the performance of the method. It has been proven that the method can effectively capture spatial and topological features and accurately predict line faults through case studies, it can also adapt to topology changes with transferability and offer better interpretability than traditional data-driven models.

title: Efficient Quantisation with Improved Lightweight YOLO (v3 and v4) - MobileNetv3 Model Channel Pruning for IoT-based Edge Devices
authors: G. Ramkumar, K Jaya Krishna, Yousuf Hazaimeh, M Haribabu, Krishna Bhargavi Y, K Rajasri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Utilizing depth wise separable convolutions, MobileNetV3 facilitates independent feature extraction by utilising diverse channels. This method yields unique feature maps for each channel, capturing varied spatial and semantic patterns. The research introduces the EQILMI model, an optimisation framework optimised for lightweight object detection on embedded systems. The key to this is the use of three methods: 8-bit network quantisation, channel pruning, and a C++ Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)-optimised implementation, which are all directed at making the calculations less complex and the memory space requirements smaller. First, the model performs quantisation to change YOLOv4-MobileNetv3 floating-point data into low-bit integer matrices. After that, it looks at Batch Normalisation (BN) scaling factors to prune those channels that are redundant, thus achieving compressed parameters and faster inference without actually changing the architecture. The performance analysis is time vs data size calculation, time vs threads calculation, number of cycles calculation, loss calculation, and MIoU curve calculation.

title: Transient Instability Detection and Control of VSG Integrated Systems Considering Current Limits
authors: Pu Cheng, Songhao Yang, Zhiguo Hao, Bingfang Li, Chengxiao Wei, Guosong Wang, Dongxu Chang
abstract: The virtual synchronous generator (VSG) has become one of the most popular grid-forming control strategies due to its virtual inertia and grid-support characteristics. However, operational mode transitions during transients, such as current limiting, complicate stability assessment and controller design. To address this issue, this paper first proposes a transient instability detection criterion for VSG integrated systems with current limiting control (CLC) based on the phase trajectory’s concave-convexity. This approach relies solely on VSG’s phase trajectory and can be proven effective under various CLC modes without dealing with the complex switching process, making it suitable for real-time stability assessment. One possible transient stability control for VSG integrated systems is proposed based on the criterion. Electromagnetic transient simulations verify the validity and accuracy of the criterion and control method.

title: Real-Time Security Assessment in Electric Vehicle-Integrated Distribution Networks via Multidimensional Holomorphic Embedding Method
authors: Fan Peixiao, Xie Jiarui, Bu Siqi
abstract: The stochastic and dynamic nature of electric vehicles (EVs) charging demand and distributed energy resources (DERs) uncertainties poses significant risks to voltage stability in distribution networks (DNs), yet most existing methods either oversimplify these spatiotemporal variations or depend on time-consuming iterative computations, limiting their effectiveness in practical, rapidly changing scenarios. Therefore, this paper develops a real-time security assessment method for EV-integrated DNs. First, a dynamic model of DNs is established to incorporate both the spatial and temporal characteristics of EV charging loads and DERs outputs. The voltage stability boundary (VSB) is defined to quantify regional load and generation limits under the coupling of EV load variations and DER uncertainties. Second, a multi-dimensional holomorphic embedding method (MDMEM) is employed to derive the expression of the static voltage stability boundary in DNs. This method not only enables assessment of the VSB, determining EV load constraints for specific regions of the DN, but also facilitates the assessment of real-time voltage stability based on current load and generation states. Finally, the simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in accurately evaluating VSB and identifying real-time voltage stability states in DNs.

title: HyFURE - Hybrid Fuzzy Reinforcement for Adaptive Class-based Traffic Control
authors: Sairam B Subramaniam, M Vijey Arvind, HarshivPranav N, Sushmitha K, Vignesh M, Geetha A V
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: The rapid surge in the number of vehicles per house-hold places immense pressure on urban transportation networks, leading to severe congestion, time loss, and adverse environmental impact. The basic drawback in most of the traffic control systems is the lack of adaptiveness in the signal control, as the signal timings are mostly fixed and operate in a repeating loop. This is followed by the issue of "vehicle blindness", where all vehicles are treated the same, giving no priority to vehicles like an ambulance carrying patients amid regular traffic. This limitation poses a direct risk to public safety, where seconds saved in commute can be crucial in determining the outcome of an emergency that requires an intelligent transportation system. To bridge the current gap, HyFURE, a two-layer Artificial Intelligence (AI) framework, adaptively prioritizes vehicles acting as an Adaptive Traffic Management System (ATMS). The first layer acts as a strategic controller using fuzzy logic to determine the priority depending on the vehicle type (ambulance, trucks, buses, etc.) present at the intersection. This strategy is then communicated to the second layer, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent, which effectively learns the optimal real-time sequence of signal changes that best satisfies the priority set by the strategic controller to reduce the waiting time of ambulances and the overall congestion by approximately 88% and 70% respectively compared to the traditional system. The implementation and validation of the proposed model were done in SUMO, a simulated real-life environment, as it eliminates the risk or the cost element of a live implementation. SUMO is a microscopic traffic simulator used to model and analyze large-scale urban transportation systems.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Expansion Planning for AC/DC Hybrid Power Grids under Extreme Natural Disasters
authors: Hanwen Wang, Xu Zhou, Yang Wang, Xianyong Xiao, Zhihao Liang, Xiang Zhou
abstract: Faced with the dual pressures of increasingly frequent extreme disasters and a high penetration of renewable energy, power systems are encountering significant challenges in maintaining secure and stable operation. Conventional transmission network expansion planning (TNEP) methods, which rely on simulations of typical steady-state scenarios, fall short in ensuring system reliability under severe disaster conditions. To address this issue, this paper introduces an extreme disaster simulation method tailored for the planning stage of hybrid AC/DC power grids, along with an intelligent TNEP strategy based on reinforcement learning. The research follows three main steps: (1) constructing a representative set of extreme scenarios and quantitatively evaluating grid damage in each case; (2) proposing a stepwise Markov decision process (MDP) model suited to the characteristics of TNEP tasks; and (3) developing a reinforcement learning-based intelligent agent to derive optimal wide-area grid expansion schemes. Simulation results on representative systems confirm that the proposed approach significantly improves the reliability of hybrid AC/DC grids in the face of extreme disasters.

title: Transient Performance Enhancement for the ITER Coil Power Supply System
authors: Dengge Jia, Jun Tao, Yiyun Huang
abstract: International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER) coil power supplies energize the superconducting magnet system, demanding robust transient response to meet strict plasma control requirements for sustained nuclear fusion. However, inherent coupled inductor loops in the coil architecture induce significant converter cross-coupling, severely degrading transient performance during current command regulation in high-power operation. Effective decoupling control is therefore essential to enhance converter transient response. This paper proposes a current(I)-based Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Control (I-LADRC) strategy, integrating an Extended State Observer (ESO) with simplified Proportional-Derivative (PD) feedback for real-time disturbance estimation and compensation. This enables active suppression of circuit interactions and parameter perturbations, significantly improving transient response robustness. MATLAB/Simulink Electromagnetic Transient (EMT) simulations validate the approach. Results demonstrate that I-LADRC achieves lower residual coupling current and, when augmented with known coupling model information feedback, exhibits superior transient decoupling performance versus industrial-paradigm control and the model-based paradigm algorithm.

title: A Hybrid LSTM-GRU-PSO Model for Inertial Energy Prediction in Renewable-Rich Power Systems
authors: Fei Kong, Min Lu, Xu Yao, Aopeng Li
abstract: The rapid integration of renewable energy sources has significantly reduced power system inertia, posing critical challenges to frequency stability. Accurate inertia prediction is essential for effective inertia compensation to maintain system stability. This paper proposes a novel hybrid deep learning model for short-term inertia prediction, integrating long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU) networks with hyperparameters optimized via particle swarm optimization (PSO). LSTM captures long-term dependencies and low-frequency inertia trends, while GRU efficiently models short-term dynamics and high-frequency fluctuations. PSO optimizes key hyperparameters to maximize the synergistic effect of LSTM and GRU. Evaluation results show that the proposed LSTM-GRUPSO model outperforms baseline models in prediction accuracy, as quantified by mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE). This improvement enables more rapid and precise inertia compensation, critical for frequency stability in high-renewable-penetration power systems.

title: Research on Cloud-Edge Collaborative Management Technology for Core DC Setting Parameters of Converter Stations
authors: Zhanguo Zhang, Siyuan Liu, Haoran Huang, Xiaojun Shen
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: This paper proposes a cloud-edge collaborative architecture for the retrieval and management of DC protection setting parameters in converter stations. By introducing a dedicated setting management CPU within the control and protection system, the unified reception, parsing, and distribution of setting parameters is achieved. Through the use of a PCIE backplane bus, synchronization of settings among multiple CPUs is efficiently implemented. The system utilizes the TCP/IP Ethernet protocol to communicate with the EWS engineer station, enabling real-time transmission and response handling of setting messages. Combined with a redundant file mechanism and multi-level verification strategy, the system effectively ensures consistency and security of setting parameters during transmission and storage. The proposed solution supports online updates of settings and historical version comparison, enhancing the responsiveness and accuracy of the operation and maintenance system to protection strategy changes. This provides a crucial foundation for the stable operation of DC transmission systems and facilitates the intelligent upgrading of maintenance tasks.

title: A Game-Theoretic and Learning-Based Framework for Flexible Virtual Power Plant Operation under Dynamic Electricity Markets
authors: Daojun Dong, Jianghua Wu, Yuqian Liu
abstract: The increasing penetration of distributed energy resources has led to the emergence of Virtual Power Plants (VPPs) as a key mechanism for enhancing grid flexibility and market participation. However, the concurrent operation of multiple VPPs introduces a dynamic and strategic decision-making environment due to their interdependence through shared market prices and system constraints. Current models often rely on exogenous price assumptions or centralized coordination, which limits their ability to capture endogenous price formation and strategic coupling among agents. To this end, this paper proposes a multi-agent reinforcement learning method for the joint scheduling of multiple VPPs in dynamic markets. The scheduling problem is set up as a Markov game, where each VPP acts as an independent agent that learns the best bidding and dispatch strategies under partial observability and evolving market conditions. By continuously adapting to market feedback and peer behaviors, agents learn to coordinate effectively without requiring centralized control or complete system information. Simulation results demonstrate improvements in economic efficiency and system-wide coordination. This work offers a scalable and adaptive solution for multi-VPP operation.

title: Design of Spiral Slots Loaded Substrate Integrated Waveguide Antenna with Improved Circular Polarization
authors: Gurrala Krishna Reddy, Goutam Rana, Jyotibhusan Padhi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: The circularly polarized antenna based on substrate integrated cavity-backed technology described in this paper. The discussed antenna is developed for Wi-Fi 6E applications. The proposed design is loaded with spiral and cross slots on the radiating patch to accomplish circular polarization with right side orientation of field. The Impedance bandwidth of antenna 100MHz centered at 6.55 GHz. Due to the adjustment of the slot dimensions, the antenna achieved significant 7.00 dB gain with 93% total efficiency. Additionally, modify the parameters of the spiral resonators to obtain an enhanced axial ratio bandwidth (ARB) of 1.5%. These performance enhancements affirm the antenna's strong potential for Wi-Fi 6E applications.

title: An Efficient Fog-Based System for Healthcare Data Analytics
authors: Prashanth Ragam, Sankati Jyothi, Guntha Karthik, Bonagiri Divya
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Research in Intelligent Computing in Engineering (RICE)
abstract: The rapidly growing amount of healthcare data, propelled by wearable sensors, personal health records (EHRs), medical monitoring systems, and Internet of Things (IoT) devices, poses serious problems for conventional cloud-based solutions. High latency, higher network bandwidth use, and concerns about data security and privacy are some of these difficulties. This research suggests a fog computing-based approach to managing, processing, and assessing health data in real-time in hospital settings in order to address these issues. To process, filter, and do preliminary analysis near the data sources, the recommended framework makes the most of fog nodes that are set up strategically. This decentralized system efficiently lowers latency and improves the responsiveness of healthcare applications via the combination of edge devices, fog nodes, and cloud services. By enhancing patient monitoring and emergency response capabilities, the fog computing model lessens reliance on cloudbased services for real-time healthcare decision-making. When compared to conventional cloud-only systems, models of hospitals revealed significant gains, such as fewer delays in data transfer, lower network bandwidth requirements, and improved real-time processing. These results demonstrate how well the concept works for important medical applications. The fog system offers scalable data management, improves data privacy, and accelerates the care of patients. Use cases like as realtime emergency warnings, predictive analytics-based early disease identification, and remote patient monitoring are ideal for it. Strategies for tackling issues with data security, resource allocation, and fog node optimization have been addressed. The fog level machine learning integration of the system extensively optimizes its abilities to support proactive healthcare, which increases patient outcomes by equipping healthcare providers to respond with greater speed and effectiveness.

title: Transfer Learning-Based Computer Vision Modeling Framework for Lumbar Vertebrae Localization in Sagittal Radiographs
authors: Roselle P. Cimagala
abstract: Accurate localization of lumbar vertebrae in sagittal radiographs is a fundamental prerequisite for quantitative spine analysis, yet manual annotation is laborintensive and prone to inter-observer variability. This study presents a transfer learning-based computer vision framework for automated lumbar vertebrae localization in sagittal lumbar spine X-ray images. The dataset comprises radiographs from a proprietary cohort of Honduran patients with spondylolisthesis and the publicly available BUU-LSPINE dataset, restricted to sagittal views. Vertebrae from L3 to S1 were annotated using bounding boxes to enable supervised vertebral localization. The proposed model leverages pretrained feature representations and multi-scale detection to robustly capture vertebral structures under varying anatomical presentations and imaging conditions. Experimental results demonstrate stable convergence and strong localization performance across the evaluated datasets, with qualitative analyses confirming anatomically consistent vertebral detection. As validation was limited to the available data sources and acquisition settings, further evaluation on larger and more diverse cohorts is necessary to establish broader robustness and generalizability. The findings support the feasibility of automated lumbar vertebral localization and provide a foundation for subsequent quantitative spine analysis.

title: PLUME: Procedural Layer Underground Modeling Engine
authors: Gabriel Manuel Garcia, Antoine Richard, Miguel Olivares-Mendez
abstract: As space exploration advances, underground environments are becoming increasingly attractive due to their potential to provide shelter, easier access to resources, and enhanced scientific opportunities. Although such environments exist on Earth, they are often not easily accessible and do not accurately represent the diversity of underground environments found throughout the solar system. This paper presents PLUME, a procedural generation framework aimed at easily creating 3D underground environments. Its flexible structure allows for the continuous enhancement of various underground features, aligning with our expanding understanding of the solar system. The environments generated using PLUME can be used for AI training, evaluating robotics algorithms, 3D rendering, and facilitating rapid iteration on developed exploration algorithms. In this paper, it is demonstrated that PLUME has been used along with a robotic simulator. PLUME is open source and has been released on Github. https://github.com/Gabryss/P.L.U.M.E

title: PQD Classification Method based on Iterative Filtering Kaiser Window S-Transform and NRBO-XGBOOST
authors: Ruochen Zhang, Lin Zhu, Minghui Wu, Xialin Li, Li Guo, Chengshan Wang
abstract: To address the inadequate time-frequency analysis of the S-transform and the low accuracy of traditional Power Quality Disturbance (PQD) classification methods, this paper presents a classification method based on Iterative Filtering Kaiser Window S-Transform (IFKST) and Newton-Raphson-Based Optimizer to optimize Extreme Gradient Boosting (NRBO-XGBoost). Firstly, the iterative loop filtering interval positioning algorithm extracts frequency feature points of disturbance signal. Then the K-S transform is applied to each feature point. Finally, 17 time-frequency features are extracted from the IFKST resulting matrix, and the hyperparameters of Extreme Gradient Boosting are optimized using the Newton-Raphson-based optimizer. The 17 disturbance features are then input into the model to achieve PQDs classification. Experimental results show that this method can classify 21 types of power quality disturbances with high accuracy in noisy environments.

title: Real-Time Traffic Violation Detection using YOLOv8 for Helmetless Riding and Overload Riding
authors: Trupti Lotlikar, Anushka Pawar, Melissa Sequeira, Abhinn Amrit, Siddharth Rane
publish: 2026 6th Biennial International Conference on Nascent Technologies in Engineering (ICNTE)
abstract: Traffic violations such as helmetless riding, overloading, and triple riding are major causes of road accidents and fatalities, particularly in urban areas with high two-wheeler density. Traditional manual monitoring methods are error-prone, labor-intensive, and lack scalability. In this paper, we propose a real-time traffic violation detection system using YOLOv8, capable of directly detecting five key classes: bike, person, helmet, no-helmet, and overload. A custom dataset of over 5,000 annotated images was used for training and evaluation. The model achieved an overall precision of 74.2%, recall of 77.3%, and mAP@0.5 of 75.7%, while running at approximately 175 FPS. These results demonstrate that the system is suitable for real-time deployment in intelligent transportation systems, providing a robust foundation for automated traffic law enforcement.

title: Tanimoto Similarity-Based Pilot Protection for Offshore Wind Power AC Collection Lines Integrated with Negative-Sequence Current Injection
authors: Tianyu Liang, Feng Wang, Jia Zhu, Jiangang Lu, Wei Liu, Chunzhe Li
abstract: Offshore wind power AC collection lines transmitted via MMC-HVDC systems are affected by fault ride-through control of both-terminal converters, leading to weak fault signatures and a high risk of maloperation in conventional power-frequency protection schemes. To address this, a Tanimoto similarity-based pilot protection method integrated with negative-sequence current injection is proposed. The method analyzes fault characteristics under weak feed-in conditions, and by enabling offshore MMC-HVDC converters to inject negative-sequence current, the waveform differences between both ends of the line are amplified. An improved Tanimoto similarity index is introduced to quantify fault severity and reliably identify internal and external faults. A simulation model developed on the MATLAB/Simulink platform shows that the scheme is unaffected by submarine cable capacitance or wind turbine output characteristics, enabling quick and accurate fault identification with improved reliability and sensitivity compared to conventional methods.

title: Hausdorff-Optimal Zonotope Construction for Characterizing Water-Energy Flexibility of Pelagic Island Micro-Energy Grid
authors: Jie Wu, Hong Liu, Xingtang He, Zihan Qi
abstract: Marine clean energy constitutes the foundational energy backbone of zero-carbon pelagic island micro-energy grid (PIMEG). Meanwhile, water and electricity subsystems exhibit tight coupling, high uncertainty, and asynchronous regulation characteristics. Constructing a unified and computationally tractable high-dimensional representation of the flexible operating region (FOR) is essential for enhancing the system’s multi-energy coordination capability under complex disturbances. To this end, we propose a Hausdorff-optimal zonotope construction method for characterizing the regulation boundary of multi-energy coupling systems. The method formulates an iteratively refinable zonotope in the target regulation space to approximate the FOR projected from the original high-dimensional system. A feasibility cone condition and generator direction refinement strategy are introduced to ensure the resulting zonotope is symmetric, convex, and interpretable. Case studies demonstrate the method’s ability to accurately characterize water-energy flexibility in the PIMEG, with favorable performance in boundary accuracy, structural controllability, and visual interpretability.

title: Enhancing Fuel Station Operations: A User-Friendly Mobile-Integrated System for Accurate Dispensing and Payment
authors: Anitha G, Yaswanth Sai Vari Gonda, Sai Tendulkar Teruru, Veera Hemanth Kumar Chitteti, Sadha B, Annamalai K
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Current gasoline dispensing systems often have measurement accuracy issues because of their manual processes, which make users unhappy about inaccurate measurement. The goal of this research is to create a mobile, automated, and integrated fuel bunk system that will increase user satisfaction and precision. The system allows users to start by registering their mobile phone for authentication through One-Time Password followed by selecting their petrol amount before generating a dispensing QR code. Using their Android app, users can conveniently pay at the gas station while getting payment records and automated alerts. Transactions take 5 to 10 seconds for completion, and hence there is an increase in operational speed of 40-50%. This instant system operation enhances business performance through automatic error reduction and delivers convenient service and trust-building benefits to users. The proposed system integrates mobile technology with petrol dispensing to raise operational standards in petrol bunks thus delivering transparent service reliably to current user demands.

title: A Deep Learning-Based Detection and Severity Grading of Grape Downy Mildew: Architectural Evaluation and Deployment on Mobile Application
authors: Parthasarathy Seethapathy, Kannan M, E Madesh, Arjun S, K S Harisankar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Grapevine is among the most valuable fruit crops that are grown in different parts of the world. Nevertheless, the grape production is highly impacted by downy mildew disease that causes serious losses in production. The conventional ways of identifying this disease are tedious, and in most cases, they find it hard to quantify the level of severity of the disease. On the contrary, the advent of advanced technologies has given rise to the creation of other mechanisms of precise disease detection. Artificial intelligence models of deep learning are giving an accurate diagnosis of grape downy mildew disease. The findings indicate that the deep learning model with pretrained ImageNet weights, EfficientNetV2B2, demonstrates notable values, attaining an accuracy of 99.31% with training dataset images and an accuracy of 99.96% with validation dataset images. The pretrained with ImageNet weights CNN model EfficientNetV2B2 classifier is a highly practical and efficient in identifying grape diseases which makes it effective in implementing the system in mobile applications as an effective decision support system to farmers. This research work presents a novel technique for classifying grape leaf disease through the use of pre-trained with ImageNet weight neural network (CNN) models.

title: Advanced Adaptive Resource Management and Optimization in Smart Grids Through the Integration of 5G Network Slicing and Federated Learning Techniques
authors: Lu Jiangang, Shi Zhan
abstract: An adaptive resource management framework for smart grids is presented, integrating 5G network slicing and federated learning to optimize communication and computational resource allocation. 5G slicing allows customization of network resources to meet the specific needs of services like EV fast charging and residential consumption, ensuring optimal bandwidth, latency, and computational power. Federated learning enables decentralized training across regions while maintaining data privacy. The system adjusts resource distribution in real-time to meet fluctuating grid demands, improving efficiency, stability, and scalability, and promoting a more adaptive resource management approach for future energy systems.

title: Short-Circuit Calculation for Renewable Energy-Integrated Power Systems Employing Wind Farm Equivalence Modeling and Equivalent Aggregation
authors: Shengyao Zhang, Ke Jia, Tianshu Bi, Yang Dai
abstract: With the continuous improvement of new energy permeability, the short-circuit current characteristics of new energy stations such as wind farms show significant time-varying characteristics, which poses new challenges to traditional calculation methods. In this paper, a short-circuit current calculation method based on wind field equivalence and equivalent aggregation is proposed, which focuses on solving the problem of accurate calculation of short-circuit parameters in power systems with high proportion of new energy. By establishing a single-machine voltage-current mapping relationship considering the low penetration characteristics of the doubly-fed wind turbine, and further extending to the multi-unit model, the voltage-current mapping relationship of the doubly-fed wind farm is established. At the same time, for the collector network topology, an equivalent aggregation algorithm based on node voltage sensitivity is proposed. By retaining the admittance matrix characteristics of key nodes, the equivalent impedance model of wind farm station is constructed, and the short-circuit current correction calculation formula considering the influence of control strategy is derived. The accuracy of the equivalent aggregation model is verified by simulation examples, and it is proved that the calculation accuracy of the proposed short-circuit current calculation method in each position of the power system is kept within 10%.

title: Fedcure Approach for Secure Access of IoMT Data from Different IoMT Devices
authors: Payel Thakur, Dipak Raskar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things is a subset of the Internet of Things that combines various IoT-enabled devices to gather, process, and send healthcare-related data in order to monitor patient health remotely and in real time. IoMT mostly transmits data via wireless networks, such as 5G and wifi, which are extremely vulnerable to various cyberattacks. Furthermore, IoMT devices lack a standard protocol. Conventional encryption schemes require difficult-to-maintain decryption keys. Conventional AI algorithms require a lot of processing power and are unable to manage the massive amounts of data generated by IoMT. Therefore in this paper, we propose a Fedcure federated learning methodology that will help to secure access the data instead of transmitting the raw data and also train the local models and updates will be shared with the global model. On the basis of these local model updates, the global model is updated. Once global model is updated, update is sent to all the local models connected to it so that they can also be updated further

title: Day-Ahead Multi-Layer Game and Capacity Allocation Optimization for Wind–PV–Storage Coalitions under Electricity Price Uncertainty
authors: Jingyu Zhou, Yanzuo Chen, Shuhong Wu
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Energy Technology and Electrical Power (ETEP)
abstract: With growing renewable penetration and increasingly volatile electricity prices, coordinating profit and risk in day-ahead markets has become a central issue for the refined operation of virtual power plants (VPPs). This paper considers a VPP aggregating wind, photovoltaic (PV) and energy storage units, and proposes a day-ahead optimization framework that jointly addresses bidding, dispatch and profit allocation under price uncertainty. First, a quadratic response function is used to link historical prices to net load deficits, while scenario generation–reduction techniques characterize the joint uncertainty of prices and renewable outputs, and Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) is adopted to measure tail risk. Second, coalition bidding and capacity decisions in the day-ahead market are formulated as an upper-level problem, and the coordinated dispatch of wind–PV–storage under efficiency, degradation and charging/discharging constraints is modeled as a lower-level problem, leading to a Stackelberg game that balances expected profit and delivery risk. Third, at the allocation layer, a risk-adjusted Shapley value with core-projection constraints is introduced to design a mechanism that reflects both marginal contribution and risk bearing, ensuring fairness and coalition stability. Case studies based on real wind/PV and price data from a regional market show that the proposed method markedly reduces extreme-scenario losses and imbalance penalties with only a slight reduction in expected profit, improves renewable utilization, and, through risk-sensitive allocation, provides stronger incentives for storage to participate deeply in the VPP.

title: Development and Application of Digital Risk Database in Higher Education Institutions
authors: Vera Kapetanović, Igor Petrović, Ana Babović Mujović, Maja Krčum, Jelena Nikčević, Tatijana Dlabač
