abstract: The traditional job application process is inefficient, fragmented, and frustrating for both job seekers and recruiters. Applicants must navigate multiple platforms with inconsistent interfaces, inadequate search filters, and redundant data entry, leading to disengagement. Many systems also lack user-friendly designs, making job applications difficult for non-tech-savvy individuals. Recruiters face challenges in managing job listings, tracking applications, and filtering candidates due to inefficient applicant tracking systems. Security concerns also arise as platforms often fail to protect sensitive user data. Additionally, the lack of real-time communication hinders seamless interaction between recruiters and applicants, slowing the hiring process. "Career Bridge" addresses these issues by leveraging the MERN stack to create a secure, intuitive, and role-specific job platform. It offers secure authentication, advanced job searches filters, and comprehensive profile management, optimizing both job-seeking and hiring experiences. With AI-driven recommendations, realtime updates, and an intuitive interface, "Career Bridge" enhances job accessibility and streamlines recruitment, making the process more efficient and user-friendly for all stakeholders.

title: Spatio-Temporal Coupled Discrete-Continuous Flexibility Region for Distribution Systems Considering Multiple Grid-Side Resources
authors: Shida Zhang, Hong Liu, Shaohan Lu, Guocheng Hou
abstract: The flexibility region (FR) of a distribution system is defined as the set of feasible operating points over the target-variable space (e.g., uncertain renewable power) that satisfy all operational and security constraints of the distribution network. To evaluate the capability of accommodating renewable power injections across multiple nodes and time intervals, this paper develops a discrete-continuous, spatio-temporal FR model that incorporates multiple grid side flexibility resources, including battery energy storage systems (BESSs), soft open points (SOPs), and network reconfiguration via tie switches. Owing to the high dimensionality and intricate boundaries of the FR, we introduce a two-step approach that combines analytical expression derivation and boundary optimization to obtain an exact analytical characterization. A 16-bus flexible distribution network is employed for validation.

title: AI-Powered Allergen Prediction Using Protein Language Model
authors: Uday Kiran G, Chaitanya K, Sreenidhi G, Prabhakar Nuthan Raju K, Prashanth B
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Research in Intelligent Computing in Engineering (RICE)
abstract: Accurate identification of allergenic proteins is essential for ensuring safety in food production, pharmaceutical development, and environmental monitoring. Although conventional laboratory techniques such as ELISA and mass spectrometry provide reliable results, they are time-consuming, costly, and unsuitable for large-scale screening. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an AI-driven allergen prediction framework that integrates Protein Language Models (PLMs) with deep learning techniques. A balanced dataset comprising <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6, 0 5 8}$</tex> protein sequences, equally divided into allergenic and non-allergenic classes, was compiled from authoritative databases including AllergenOnline, UniProt, IUIS, and published literature. Pretrained PLMs such as ESM and ProtBERT were employed to generate rich sequence embeddings, which were further processed using a hybrid CNN-RNN architecture. Convolutional Neural Networks were used to capture local sequence motifs, while Long Short-Term Memory networks modeled long-range dependencies within protein sequences. The model was trained using binary crossentropy loss and optimized with Adam and RMSProp optimizers, with dropout and L2 regularization applied to improve generalization. Performance evaluation using Accuracy, Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) demonstrates that the proposed approach outperforms traditional machine learning methods such as Support Vector Machines and Random Forest classifiers. The proposed framework provides a scalable and reliable computational solution for allergen prediction, with potential applications in food safety assessment, drug development, and allergen surveillance.

title: A Blockchain-Based Interoperable Framework for Secure, Seamless, and Optimized Healthcare Data Management in 6G-Ready Edge Healthcare Networks
authors: Jing Yang, Abdullah Ayub Khan, Diva Kurnianingtyas, Muh. Arif Rahman, Sunil Prajapat, Por Lip Yee
abstract: Recent developments in blockchain technology (BT) have greatly increased its potential to guarantee provenance, transparency, data quality, and trustworthiness, especially in the healthcare industry. However, enduring issues such as scalability, privacy protection, resilience, elasticity, and security concerns continue to impede its adoption in healthcare systems. The evolution toward 6G-ready edge healthcare networks further amplifies these challenges, demanding low-latency, high-reliability, and secure data interoperability across distributed medical institutions. This paper explores the critical role of Hyperledger technology in enabling efficient and interoperable health data management systems within this emerging context. Specifically, we focus on Hyperledger Sawtooth for its ability to support compliant protocol development and regulatory framework alignment with government standards. We propose a consortium blockchain network equipped with two dedicated intercommunication channels to facilitate secure and seamless health data exchange, organization, and preservation at the edge. All transactions employ SHA-256 hash encryption and shared public keys, ensuring enhanced provenance, security, and privacy during data transmission. To achieve cost efficiency and frictionless data sharing among healthcare stakeholders, a customizable Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET) consensus mechanism is introduced. The proposed framework, termed “BHI-5”, is evaluated through simulation using key metrics: (i) consortium network usage costs, (ii) computational fluctuations during hash processing and block delivery, (iii) seamless health data exchange and organization rates via Filecoin, and (iv) optimization of shared data and immutable ledger performance. Results demonstrate that the BHI-5 framework effectively meets the operational needs of 6G-ready edge healthcare environments, achieving secure, scalable, and optimized data management suitable for real-world healthcare applications.

title: Smart Contracts- Cloud Storage-Assisted Medical Image Encryption and Sharing Solution
authors: Jianmeng Liu, Zhenlong Man
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: To address the security risks in sharing medical imaging data and the challenges of insufficient data interoperability between heterogeneous systems, and to achieve secure sharing of medical information across institutions, this paper proposes a secure encryption scheme based on the characteristics of medical images, and uses cloud storage and smart contracts to build a secure framework for cross-institutional information sharing. First, in response to the regional characteristics and uneven information distribution of medical images, an innovative two-stage encryption scheme of “partitioning, diffusion, and coupled scrambling” is proposed. The first stage applies information entropy equalisation processing to non-ROI regions, enhancing their information effectiveness and generating dynamic parameters. The second stage utilises the cross-region coupling dynamic parameters generated in the first stage to optimise the encryption results within ROI regions. Finally, a key driven scrambling factor is employed to complete the global pixel position reconstruction, achieving high-security encryption. Each medical institution encrypts medical images and uploads them to unified cloud storage, along with binary thumbnails generated from the corresponding plaintext images. Simultaneously, essential metadata—including image hash values, ownership details, and access permission policies—is stored on the blockchain. After an authorized user selects the desired data by browsing cloud thumbnails, a smart contract automatically executes the transaction, completing on-chain permission verification, data traceability, and access authentication, thereby coordinating the user's secure access to the corresponding encrypted image data off-chain. Eight experiments have demonstrated the security of the encryption scheme and the proposed sharing framework.

title: An Instance Segmentation and Feature Visualization Method for Multi-source Partial Discharge Diagnosis
authors: Yanshen Liang, Hanbo Zheng, Jinheng Li, Shihao Lu, Maofeng Li, Jinrui Kang, Wenhong Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Partial discharge (PD) visualization diagnosis is essential for assessing insulation degradation in power transformers under complex multi-source coupling conditions. However, existing methods lack a unified framework capable of accurate instance-level identification, multi-scale feature discrimination, and interpretable visualization in phase-resolved PD (PRPD) patterns. To address this issue, a multi-source PRPD visualization diagnosis method is proposed. An experimental platform is first constructed to acquire and integrate diverse PD signals, forming a high-quality multi-source PRPD dataset. A dedicated instance segmentation model, termed YOLO-MAF (YOLO with Multi-scale and Attention Fusion), is then developed by incorporating a WindScale module for multi-scale feature extraction, a C2TSSA attention mechanism for semantic enhancement, GSConv for lightweight feature fusion, and a tiny segmentation head to improve small-small-scale discharge detection. Experimental results show that the complete YOLO-MAF achieves a recall of 99.8% and an mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50:95</sub> of 88.3% on multi-source PRPD patterns, and improves AP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50:95</sub> across multi-scale PD types compared with the baseline model, with a maximum gain of 13.2% for small-scale floating discharges. Based on the segmentation results, an innovative visual diagnosis algorithm integrating Paddle-OCR and edge detection is further developed to extract and present key discharge attributes, including phase, magnitude, and type distribution, thereby enhancing diagnostic interpretability.

title: ADA-Net: Avalanche Differential Attention Network with Covariance Correlation Differential Transformer for Multimodal Snow Avalanche Susceptibility Mapping
authors: Isma Kulsoom, Yuanjun Jiang, Sadaqat Hussain, Muhammad Wasi, Muhammad Sami, Garee Khan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Multimodal data integration plays a critical role in accurately mapping snow avalanche susceptibility, yet existing models struggle to capture the complex nonlinear interactions among optical imagery, SAR backscatter, snowpack measurements, and meteorological variables. Traditional statistical and shallow machine learning (ML) approaches often rely on handcrafted features and linear assumptions, while recent deep learning (DL) methods employ simplistic fusion strategies that overlook higher-order dependencies and cross-modal discrepancies. To address these limitations, this study introduces a novel multimodal DL framework combining a Covariance-Correlation Differential Transformer (CCDT) for second-order feature extraction with an Avalanche Differential Attention Network (ADA-Net) for targeted cross-modal fusion. CCDT constructs differential covariance correlation matrices to reveal subtle interdependencies across 17 topographic, snowpack, and meteorological inputs, and ADA-Net’s Siamese transformer architecture amplifies critical discrepancies between modalities. Evaluated on a curated dataset of 150 avalanche events along China’s G217 and G218 highways in the Central Tianshan Mountains, our CCDT-ADA-Net framework achieves superior predictive performance (ROC-AUC = 0.9903, F1 = 0.96) and spatial accuracy, successfully classifying 97.4% of historical avalanche release areas within its high-susceptibility zone. It significantly outperforms benchmark models, including Vision Transformer, ConvLSTM, RNN, HybridMLP, and TabNet. Permutation importance analysis confirms snow density, elevation, and snow depth as dominant risk factors. This scalable, interpretable approach offers practical utility for operational avalanche forecasting and infrastructure protection in high-mountain regions.

title: ICAIQSA 2025 Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computation-Based Sensor Application (ICAIQSA)

title: Quantum-Enhanced Strain and Temperature Sensing Based on Self-Imaging Principle in a Single-Multi-Single-Mode Fiber Structure
authors: Dongxu Wang, Zihao Liu, Hailong Wang, Tenghui Mao, Cheng Peng, Chunliu Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: To overcome the precision limitation imposed by classical common noise and standard quantum limit (SQL) in traditional classical sensing, a single-multi-single-mode (SMS) fiber optic sensor based on the combination of self-imaging principle and two-mode squeezed light source is proposed and experimentally demonstrated. The SMS sensor is constructed by fusing a multi-mode fiber in between two single-mode fiber segments. By utilizing an intensity-correlated twin beam source (signal and idler), the signal beam acts as the sensing probe interacting with the SMS sensor, while the idler beam serves as a reference to implement balanced differential measurement. The experimental results explicitly demonstrate a maximum signal-to-noise ratio enhancement of 1.196±0.012 dB and 1.194±0.019 dB for strain sensing (0 με to 2077.9 με) and temperature sensing (30°C to 110°C), respectively. These findings confirm the sensor's capability for real-time, high-precision sub-Poissonian measurements, particularly for practical applications in electromagnetic-immune and the requirements of simultaneous discrimination of temperature and strain.

title: Priority-Aware DDPG Empowered Multi-Active-RIS Beamforming Framework for Energy-Efficient ISAC
authors: Bowen Yang, Yao Yu, Xin Hao, Haoyang Wu, Lei Guo
abstract: This paper investigates energy-efficient integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) aided by multiple active reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (active-RISs). We develop a multi-active-RIS-enabled joint beamforming framework and formulate a system-level power optimization problem that explicitly accounts for the active-RIS amplification power in ISAC. To support real-time beamforming decisions, we propose a priority-aware deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) algorithm with a hierarchical reward design that provides clear exploration guidance for high-dimensional joint beamforming. Simulation results show that the proposed framework improves overall power efficiency compared with passive-RIS and conventional DDPG baselines under different ISAC requirements and network scales.

title: Neuroimaging Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Harrish VP, Adwitiya Paul, Uma Priya D
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Diagnosing Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) presents a significant challenge due to its inherent heterogeneity and reliance on time-consuming clinical assessments. Traditional methods can be subjective and may lead to diagnostic delays, which are particularly detrimental given the importance of early intervention. To address these limitations, a growing body of research is exploring the use of neuroimaging-based diagnostics combined with machine learning (ML) models. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the current state of this research, comparing and contrasting various algorithms used for ASD classification. Our review process systematically examines a wide range of studies, focusing on the diversity of ML techniques from classical models to advanced deep learning architectures and the different neuroimaging modalities employed, such as functional and structural MRI. We analyze how different data preprocessing pipelines and feature extraction methods influence model performance and generalizability. Furthermore, we provide our own interpretations of the findings, discuss common challenges such as sample heterogeneity and data scarcity, and outline future directions for developing more robust, reproducible, and clinically applicable diagnostic tools. This work aims to synthesize the collective knowledge in the field and guide future research toward creating reliable, automated solutions that can augment clinical practice.

title: LES-Talker: Fine-Grained Emotion Editing for Talking Head Generation in Linear Emotion Space
authors: Guanwen Feng, Zhihao Qian, Yunan Li, Siyu Jin, Qiguang Miao, Chi-Man Pun
abstract: While existing one-shot talking head generation models have achieved progress in coarse-grained emotion editing, there is still a lack of fine-grained emotion editing models with high interpretability. We argue that for an approach to be considered fine-grained, it needs to provide clear definitions and sufficiently detailed differentiation. We present LES-Talker, a novel one-shot talking head generation model with high interpretability, to achieve fine-grained emotion editing across emotion types, emotion levels, and facial units. We propose a Linear Emotion Space (LES) definition based on Facial Action Units to characterize emotion transformations as vector transformations. We design the Cross-Dimension Attention Net (CDAN) to deeply mine the correlation between LES representation and 3D model representation. Through mining multiple relationships across different feature and structure dimensions, we enable LES representation to guide the controllable deformation of 3D model. In order to adapt the multimodal data with deviations to the LES and enhance visual quality, we utilize specialized network design and training strategies. Experiments show that our method provides high visual quality along with multilevel and inter pretable fine-grained emotion editing, outperforming mainstream methods. Project page: https://peterfanfan.github.io/LES-Talker/

title: Progress in Fine-Line Front-Side Printing for TOPCon Solar Cells
authors: Jörg Schube, Andreas Lorenz, Sebastian Mack, Laura Harnecker, Sebastian Pingel, Jonas D. De Rose, Andreas Wolf
abstract: As the silver price sets one record height after another, to curb costs, the need to reduce silver consumption in solar cell metallization increases. This work develops front-side metallization for tunnel oxide passivating contact (TOPCon) solar cells toward fine-line printing, i.e., feature sizes of less than 20 μm in width and a reduction of wet paste laydown. Besides silver reduction, this is motivated by increasing conversion efficiency, which further reduces cost per watt. To reach these goals by screen printing, three different fine-mesh configurations for knotless screens are utilized. Nominal screen opening widths vary from 24 µm to 13 μm. Moreover, printing with a dual-layer stencil featuring a nominal opening width of 9 μm is analyzed. The printed and fired contacts are characterized toward key geometrical and electrical performance indicators. Based on the corresponding findings, M10-sized TOPCon solar cells featuring fine-line grids with a core finger width wc  =  (16 ± 1) μm were fabricated. Compared to the reference group with wc  =  (23 ± 2) μm, a conversion efficiency gain of (0.9 ± 0.2)% rel is achieved, which is mainly driven by a reduction of shading-induced losses. Furthermore, wet paste laydown on the front is reduced by ∼30% rel using such metallization.

title: Generation of 12.8-fs pulses with 1.3-MW peak power at 1550 nm in conventional single-mode fiber
authors: Jiafeng Shan, Zhiwei Huang, Yu Cai, Zuxing Zhang, Zhiqiang Wang
abstract: We present a simple and efficient method for generating high-peak-power few-cycle pulses directly in conventional single-mode fibers (SMFs) by synergistically combining soliton self-compression with optimal pre-chirping of the input pulse. 255 fs pulses at 1550 nm is compressed to 12.8 fs over only 17 cm-long SMF without any additional phase compensation. The compressed pulses reach energies up to 16.7 nJ and peak power of 1.3 MW. To our knowledge, this is the highest pulse energy achieved for two-cycle pulses at 1550 nm from conventional SMF. Numerical simulations confirm the experimental results and elucidate the synergistic coupling between pre-chirp management and soliton dynamics. This work provides a robust and practical route to few-cycle pulse generation directly from standard optical fibers.

title: Communication-Efficient and Robust Multi-Modal Federated Learning via Latent-Space Consensus
authors: Mohamed Badi, Chaouki Ben Issaid, Mehdi Bennis
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed devices without sharing raw data, but applying FL to multi-modal settings introduces significant challenges. Clients typically possess heterogeneous modalities and model architectures, making it difficult to align feature spaces efficiently while preserving privacy and minimizing communication costs. To address this, we introduce CoMFed, a Communication-Efficient Multi-Modal Federated Learning framework that uses learnable projection matrices to generate compressed latent representations. A latent-space regularizer aligns these representations across clients, improving cross-modal consistency and robustness to outliers. Experiments on human activity recognition benchmarks show that CoMFed achieves competitive accuracy with minimal overhead.

title: Co-Channel Coexistence of Satellite Direct-to-Device and Terrestrial IMT in Cross-Border Scenarios: Deterministic PFD/EPFD Masks, Dynamic Validation, and Multi-System Aggregation
authors: Quan Liu, Yong Li, Lichong Wang, Jujuan Gu, Meng Zhou, Weisong Zhao
abstract: The operation of Satellite Direct-to-Device (SD2D) in International Mobile Telecommunications (IMT) bands necessitates rigorous co-channel coexistence assessment in cross-border deployments. This paper proposes a unified framework that derives deterministic protection masks from IMT-2020 (5G)-representative receiver characteristics and I/N thresholds, and validates these masks through Monte Carlo simulations, including sensitivity checks on time-step discretization, clutter shielding, and implementation margin. All satellite and IMT base-station (BS) antennas follow ITU-R/3GPP models and are represented by patterns with back-lobe attenuation to reflect rear-side blocking. The deterministic calculations yield a power flux density (PFD) mask for protecting IMT user equipment (UEs) and an equivalent PFD (EPFD) mask for protecting IMT BSs. Using two SD2D configurations, the dynamic validation provides the required minimum isolation distances. For SD2D-1, protecting both IMT UEs and BSs requires an isolation distance of 281 km, with border PFD/EPFD levels of −110.11/−141.33 dBW/m²/MHz. For SD2D-2, a directional antenna pattern, together with a stringent minimum service-elevation constraint, reduces the upper-tail interference risk, lowering the isolation distance to 120 km, with border PFD/EPFD levels of −103.77/−141.45 dBW/m²/MHz. The framework is extended to a Multiple-SD2D scenario via a fairness-aware joint search of per-system isolation distances, incorporating intra-system beam isolation, inter-system geofencing, and interference apportionment. Results show that, under the proposed coordination logic, adding a second SD2D system does not necessarily increase the high-percentile interference at IMT receivers; instead, it is largely mitigated through distance splitting and interference apportionment. A regulator-friendly non-cooperative apportionment baseline that translates the aggregate masks into per-system enforceable limits is also provided.

title: NeuDFL: Efficient Neuron-based Defense Against Label Flipping Attacks on Non-IID Data
authors: Kai Fan, Huixuan Wang, Wenjie Li, Xiang Li, Hui Li, Kuan Zhang, Yintang Yang, Lianhai Wang
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training while preserving data privacy, making it particularly attractive for large-scale Internet of Things (IoT) systems. However, in practical deployments, data collected by distributed clients are often non-independent and identically distributed (Non-IID), which amplifies the vulnerability of FL to poisoning attacks. Among them, Label-Flipping Attacks (LFA) are especially stealthy, as they can induce targeted misclassification without noticeably affecting overall accuracy, posing serious risks to safety-critical IoT applications. In this paper, we propose NeuDFL, a lightweight and robust defense framework against LFA under Non-IID settings. Unlike many existing defenses that primarily rely on gradient analysis over the full model update space or auxiliary clean datasets, NeuDFL exploits lightweight class-wise parameter statistics extracted from the final fully connected layer. By leveraging the cumulative and task-aligned nature of model parameters, NeuDFL enables reliable identification of attacked classes and filters malicious clients via an adaptive statistical threshold, improving robustness to data heterogeneity while incurring low computational overhead. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that NeuDFL offers an effective and efficient defense against label-flipping attacks, providing a robust solution for federated learning in complex real-world environments.

title: Plog: An Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Collaborative Learning Framework on Vertically Partitioned Graph Data
authors: Jiaqi Zhao, Hui Zhu, Junpeng Zhang, Xiaoyu Kou, Haonan Yan, Fengwei Wang, Hui Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: With the rapid advancement and widespread ap plication of the graph neural network (GNN), the collaborative graph learning, in which multiple parties collaboratively construct a GNN model using their respective graph data, has attracted increasing attention. However, this paradigm also raises significant privacy concerns, as both nodes and edges may contain sensitive personal information, while existing privacy preserving schemes often come at the cost of degraded model performance or substantial system overhead. Therefore, this paper proposes an efficient and privacy-preserving collaborative, and Hui Li, Member, IEEE, Xiaoyu Kou Social Platform learning framework on vertically partitioned graph data, dubbed Plog. Specifically, we first design a decomposition algorithm to split the sparse adjacency matrix into the summation of multiple independent permutations, which are lightweight, parallelizable, and well-suited for secure multi-party computation. Building on this, a weighted oblivious batch permutation protocol is carefully customized based on correlated randomness to securely and efficiently compute adjacency matrix multiplications, addressing the core efficiency bottleneck in GNN inference and training. The selective security of Plog is formally verified under the ideal-real paradigm. Extensive experimental results on three real world datasets demonstrate that compared to the state-of-the art scheme, Plog can reduce online communication rounds by 46% and achieve a 1.73× speedup in the overall inference and training time.

title: A Portable Microbial Growth Curve Analyzer for High-Throughput Monitoring of Yeast Fermentation Dynamics
authors: Yongjie Zhong, Zhuoyuan Lai, Fangzhu Lin, Long Qu, Yi Wan
abstract: Yeast fermentation is pivotal in bio-technology and food science, yet traditional monitoring methods face challenges such as labor intensity and lack of real-time cellular-level resolution. This study addresses these limitations by developing a portable microbial growth curve analyzer (pMGCA) system for automated, high-throughput screening of yeast growth under varying conditions. The pMGCA integrates a modular hardware design with high-precision optical detection, employing a closed-loop constant current driving scheme and 24-bit analog-to-digital conversion for accurate absorbance (OD) measurements. Validation tests demonstrated exceptional consistency across replicates for Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Yarrowia lipolytica strains, with coefficient of variation (CV) values below 5% for the turning-point detection time (second-derivative peak time of the fitted OD–time curve) and endpoint OD, and strong linear correlation (R² > 0.98) between OD and colony-forming units (CFU/mL). Experiments on pH and carbon source influences revealed strain-specific preferences: S. cerevisiae thrived at pH 5–6 with glucose, while Y. lipolytica strains preferred pH 6–7 with sucrose. These findings underscore the system's reliability for optimizing fermentation conditions, enhancing biomass yield, and supporting sustainable bioprocessing applications in industrial and research settings.

title: Non-Cooperative Localization via WiFi Traffic Sniffing
authors: Xuecheng Xie, Dongheng Zhang, Yadong Li, Tianyu Zhang, Liquan Fang, Shuai Yang, Yan Chen
abstract: The past few decades have seen significant advancements in WiFi indoor localization leveraging fine-grained Channel State Information (CSI). However, existing methods often require multiple access points (APs) or the target device to share sensing data. This cooperative localization complicates the deployment of practical localization systems. In this paper, we present SniFi, a non-cooperative localization method that seamlessly integrates with existing WiFi infrastructure. Unlike prior methods, SniFi does not require multiple CSI-capable APs or additional user actions. We use a single network interface card (NIC) to sniff WiFi traffic and obtain the angle information needed for localization. For APs that do not support CSI acquisition, we first utilize Beamforming Feedback Information (BFI) to estimate the Angle of Departure (AoD). Then, by analyzing the sniffed packets, we can obtain the CSI and the corresponding Angle of Arrival (AoA) from the target device to the sniffer. During experiments, we also address the challenge of angle ambiguity of the latest Intel AX210 NIC by integrating map constraints and temporal continuity. These techniques together enable us to achieve accurate localization without making changes to existing AP networks. Extensive evaluations across various environments and APs demonstrate that SniFi achieves decimeter-level accuracy in median error.

title: Weather-Resilient Road Safety Vision System
authors: Bhuvanraj R, Yadhavaramanan C, Harish V, Naveen Karthick P M, Sribharathi B
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Achievement of road safety and security during various weather conditions and lights poses a significant challenge in intelligent transportation system. In the meantime, the Weather-Resilient Road Safety Vision System is founded on the YOLOv8 and is designed to handle the problem of unpredictable weather and environmental conditions by integrating multi-feature detection and adaptive analysis on the road conditions to prevent accidents and inconvenience during traveling. It employs deep learning algorithm YOLOv8 to recognize potholes and speed bumps to avoid inconveniences in the road, and the detection of the lanes and road signs so that the vehicle can remain safe, and it does a real-time processing with the help of OpenCV. The decision to estimate the magnitude of the damage in the road surface is done through a risk analysis module which depends on the size of the bounding boxes and the intensity of the visuals. In order to enhance the strength of the model in terms of its operation and to render it lightweight, the weather conditions are enriched with the incorporation of various kinds of weather images in the training of the model. Intelligent mapping of road defects is also made possible due to the use of GPS tagging of each detection in the system. A visual analysis provided in the form of grade heatmaps and detection metrics is offered in an interactive dashboard created with the help of Streamlit and Plotly. Lastly, a GUI should be incorporated whereby non-technical users can also be able to access the detections and also be able to perform periodic maintenance of the roads by the municipality staff. It is an end to end system that is a scalable and weather conscious road monitoring system of safety and security during the various weather conditions and for installation in the smart city that integrates ITS infrastructure.

title: Flood Prediction using Machine Learning
authors: Parumanchala Bhaskar, SMD.Riyaz Naik, Nootheti Santhosh, Racheeti Sadhu Sundhar, Sagili Sudheer, Macharlla Vivek
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Image Processing and Capsule Networks (ICIPCN)
abstract: Flood forecasting has been very important in avert catastrophic impacts of floods on human life and property. The paper will present a machine learning model that would be hypothesized to forecast the likelihood of floods in case the environmental and socio-economic variable is considered in unison. It consists of 21 variables, i.e. monsoon, deforestation, climate change, urbanization and an unlimited target which is FloodProbability (0 to 1). It regresses with the assistance of regression flood risk models in the form of Linear Regression, XGBoost Regressor, Random Forest Regressor and Stacking Regressor. What is also performed is preprocessing with the data that involves feature scaling and training test data division. The comparison tools applied in the analysis of the model performance are Mean Squared Error (MSE), and R 2. The results of the research are pointing at Stacking Regressor and the Linear Regression models that have given the most promising results with the R 2 equal to 0.8449. With the rescue in optimization of the flood forecasting as mentioned in this paper, machine learning will surely arrive with the salvage in the disaster and resource management.

title: Research on Critical Node Identification in Cyber-Physical Power Systems Based on GCN-IDDA*
authors: Yulei Huang, Yan Li, Tianqi Xu, Zhaolei He, Mengmeng Zhu
abstract: In recent years, Cyber-Physical Power Systems (CPPS) have increasingly encountered the risk of cascading failures stemming from critical node disruptions, a challenge exacerbated by their intricate topological structures and dynamic interactions.This study proposes the Graph Convolutional Network with Integrated Deformable Depthwise Attention (GCN-IDDA) model to accurately identify critical nodes in CPPS, enhancing system reliability and resilience. The GCN-IDDA model employs a sophisticated integration of multi-scale feature extraction, deformable convolution, depthwise separable convolution, and an attention mechanism to effectively capture both topological and physical attributes of the network, with its performance validated on Barabási-Albert (BA) model-generated networks as well as real-world CPPS networks. Experimental results reveal that GCN-IDDA substantially surpasses traditional methods and baseline graph neural networks in Kendall’s tau coefficient assessments, demonstrating exceptional robustness and generalization capabilities. This research delivers an efficient framework for critical node identification in CPPS, paving the way for future enhancements in model optimization and broader applications to large-scale and dynamic network analyses, thereby advancing the development of resilient smart grids.

title: On Low-Carbon Economic Dispatch of CHP: An Economy-Carbon Operation Region Model
authors: Zhongfan Gu, Guangsheng Pan, Xiwei Fu, Yuejun Luo, Bo Yuan, Peng Xia
abstract: Although combined heat and power (CHP) units improve energy efficiency and reduce fuel consumption, they still produce unavoidable carbon emissions. In the context of energy transition, this study proposes an Economy-Carbon Operation Region (ECOR) model for CHP units to intuitively capture the coupling among cost, carbon emissions, and operating conditions. An iterative RANSAC (IRANSAC) algorithm is developed to fit the three-dimensional surface of the ECOR model using multiple planes. These fitted planes are then triangulated and reformulated into a Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) form, allowing seamless integration into traditional economic dispatch models for low-carbon operation. Case studies validate the accuracy and effectiveness of the proposed method and highlight the advantages of the ECOR model.

title: Symbolic Regression Driven Path Loss Modeling with Kalman and Markov Features
authors: Ranjan P, Vidhyashankar R, Pritick V, Tejeswar G, Prakash Venugopal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: The modeling of radio propagation is essential to the next generation wireless networks and in high-frequency (millimeter-wave, mmWave) and dense urban areas where coverage and capacity are extremely sensitive to environment and movement dynamics. Despite the wide adoption of standardized Third Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) path loss (PL) models, many of these instruments do not incorporate support over mobility, interference, and traffic-aware rotating retention. This paper presents a single framework of simulation that integrates line-of-sight (LOS) and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) Urban Microcell (UMi), Urban Macrocell (UMa), and Rural Macrocell (RMa) models, as well as spatially correlated shadowing (SCS), Doppler fading, and Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA) proportional-fair scheduling. The model provides realistic mobility (random waypoints, vehicular, group and trace-driven) and Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) driven handovers, as well as creating dynamic traffic to analyze the end-to-end performance. Integrated mobility and interference modeling in dense UMi at 28 GHz decreased median SINR by 3.8 dB and 92% of coverage, and at 5 dB SINR, by 81%. They accomplished 17% better cell-edge throughput with proportional-fair scheduling than with round-robin allocation.The design is modular as it aids both academic studies and industrial modeling, allowing forecasting maintenance and adaptive resource distribution for wireless systems of the next generation.

title: Stacked Intelligence: EEG-Based Early Schizophrenia Detection with Hybrid Models
authors: Pratiksha Pandhare, Nagaraj R, Reena Pagare, Lakshmi Sutha Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (IConSCEPT)
abstract: Schizophrenia is a chronic mental disorder affecting behavior and cognition, with diagnosis primarily relying on clinical interviews, which are often subjective and prone to delays. Electroencephalography (EEG) provides a promising avenue for objective assessment. In this study, multi-view EEG features—including spectral, statistical, and wavelet-based representations—were extracted and enriched using band power, PSD, DWT, covariance, spectral entropy, and distance-based features. These features were reduced and transformed via mutual information-based selection and Kernel PCA. Multiple classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, Extra Trees, MLP, Ridge, Logistic Regression) were trained and combined through a stacking ensemble with a Ridge meta-learner. Evaluated under 10-fold stratified cross-validation, the ensemble achieved an average accuracy of 0.81, F1-score of 0.81, and ROC-AUC of 0.92. SHAP-based explainability revealed that Ridge, SVM, Extra Trees, MLP, Logistic Regression, and Random Forest contributed differently, highlighting the complementarity of deep spectral and hand-crafted EEG features. These results demonstrate that combining diverse models with interpretable AI can enhance EEG-based schizophrenia diagnostics.

title: Knowledge-Driven and Reinforcement Learning-Based Hybrid Resilient Dispatch for Process-Based Industrial Park Microgrids
authors: Guokai Hao, Yuanzheng Li, Zhaohui Qie, Jiehui Zheng, Shangyang He, Chi Yung Chung
abstract: Due to their continuous production characteristics, process-based industrial parks (PIPs) require a highly stable power supply. In recent years, extreme weather events have led to frequent power outages in the main grid, highlighting the importance of resilient dispatch for PIPs. However, because grid outages are low-probability events, traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches often struggle to develop effective dispatch policies for such scenarios. To address this, this paper proposes a knowledge-driven and RL-based hybrid resilient dispatch framework, which integrates resilient dispatch policies, linear programming methods, and learnable policies to enhance PIPs' resilience to main grid outages. A comparative analysis against the Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm, based on a battery production industrial park, is conducted to validate the proposed framework's effectiveness during outages.

title: A Probabilistic Block-based Medium-Long Term Transaction Method for Renewable Energy Participation
authors: Yuan Pei, Yanjun Jiang, Yuhang Zhang, Yuhan Huang, Tao Ding
abstract: Blocks represent electricity volumes equally allocated across a given time interval. Conventional block-based trading exposes renewable energy sources (RES) to high risks due to penalties for potential under-delivery. Therefore, we propose a novel probabilistic block (PB) based trading method for the medium-long term transaction. RES can additionally submit the probability of delivering the block electricity. Key challenges in PB clearing, including determining the clearing order and prices for both PBs and traditional deterministic blocks, are addressed. Additionally, a data-driven approach is introduced, allowing RES with different risk preferences to submit PBs with their desired probabilities. Case studies on IEEE and realistic systems demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: OIRL: Real-time Optimization of Peak Shaving and Energy Arbitrage in Industrial Microgrids with Offline Inverse Reinforcement Learning Framework
authors: Yulong Hu, Sen Li
abstract: Industrial microgrids offer a promising pathway toward sustainable manufacturing, but their real-time operation presents significant challenges in concurrently optimizing peak shaving and energy arbitrage. These challenges stem from renewable energy volatility, complex temporal dependencies in peak demand charges, and conflicting operational objectives that existing model-based approaches and standard reinforcement learning methods fail to adequately address. To overcome these limitations, we present a novel Offline Inverse Reinforcement Learning (OIRL) framework that formulates the optimization problem as a soft Markov Decision Process (MDP). The proposed solution features a dual reward-policy iterative optimization mechanism that uniquely integrates contrastive reward learning from expert demonstrations with conservative soft Q-learning. This innovative combination enables precise temporal reward decomposition for monthly operational costs while guaranteeing stable and sample-efficient policy improvement through regularized soft value function updates. Comprehensive validation using real-world data from a Chinese industrial microgrid shows our framework achieves remarkable performance improvements of 117.9% in economic efficiency and 226.4% in peak shaving capability compared to state-of-the-art baselines, while demonstrating consistent robustness across varying operational conditions.
